<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 ┬Ę Linearity of Expectation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition ŌĆö Linearity of Expectation (LoE)**
> For any two random variables $X$ and $Y$ defined on the **same probability space** $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, \Pr)$,
>
> $$\mathbb{E}[X + Y] = \mathbb{E}[X] + \mathbb{E}[Y]$$
>
> More generally, for any finite collection of random variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$,
>
> $$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[X_i]$$
>
> **Crucial property:** This identity holds **regardless of whether $X$ and $Y$ are independent** or dependent, and regardless of whether $\mathbb{E}[X]$ and $\mathbb{E}[Y]$ are finite or infinite (as long as they are well-defined).

### 1.2 Extension to Affine Transformations

> [!NOTE] **Corollary ŌĆö Linearity with Scaling and Constants**
> For any random variable $X$ and any constants $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$,
>
> $$\mathbb{E}[aX + b] = a\,\mathbb{E}[X] + b$$
>
> The constant $b$ can be interpreted as a *degenerate* random variable that always takes the value $b$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP] **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Classroom Coin Jar**
> Imagine a classroom of **60 students**. Each student tosses a fair coin into a **shared jar**. You want the expected total number of heads.
>
> * **The Wrong (but intuitive) Way:** You might think the joint distribution is complicated, so computing $\mathbb{E}[X_{\text{total}}]$ directly must be hard.
> * **The Linearity Way:** Compute the expected heads **per student** ($\mathbb{E}[X_i] = 0.5$) and then simply **add them up**: $60 \times 0.5 = 30$.
>
> **Why this works:** The "average of the sum" equals the "sum of the averages" ŌĆö and you never need to know how the coin tosses *interact* with each other. This is the **killer feature** of linearity of expectation: it bypasses the need for joint distributions.

### 1.4 Why This Theorem is Central to Randomized Algorithms

In randomized algorithm design (the focus of **PECST639**), we routinely need to bound the **expected running time** of an algorithm whose internal random choices are deeply intertwined. Linearity of expectation lets us decompose a complex random quantity into a sum of simple indicator random variables, each trivially analyzable. The **coupon collector's problem**, **quicksort expected runtime**, and **load balancing** all rely on this identity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition ŌĆö How the Theorem Works

The proof of linearity of expectation reduces to **Fubini's theorem** for sums of non-negative terms, extended by linearity of integrals for signed random variables.

**Step-by-step logic for the two-variable case:**

1. **Sample space setup:** Both $X$ and $Y$ are functions $\Omega \to \mathbb{R}$ defined on the same outcome space $\Omega$.
2. **Definition of expectation:** For discrete $X$,
   $$\mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_{x \in \text{Range}(X)} x \cdot \Pr[X = x]$$
3. **Joint expansion:** Rewrite $(X+Y)(\omega) = X(\omega) + Y(\omega)$ and expand:
   $$\mathbb{E}[X+Y] = \sum_{\omega \in \Omega} (X(\omega) + Y(\omega)) \cdot \Pr[\omega]$$
4. **Distribute the sum:** Split the summation:
   $$\mathbb{E}[X+Y] = \sum_{\omega \in \Omega} X(\omega)\Pr[\omega] + \sum_{\omega \in \Omega} Y(\omega)\Pr[\omega]$$
5. **Recognize marginals:** The two sums are precisely $\mathbb{E}[X]$ and $\mathbb{E}[Y]$.
6. **Conclusion:** $\mathbb{E}[X+Y] = \mathbb{E}[X] + \mathbb{E}[Y]$. $\blacksquare$

## 2.2 Why "Linearity" Does NOT Apply to Other Functions

> [!WARNING] **Common Student Pitfall**
> Linearity holds **only for expectation**. For non-linear functions $g$,
>
> $$\mathbb{E}[g(X+Y)] \neq g(\mathbb{E}[X] + \mathbb{E}[Y])$$
>
> in general. In particular, **$\mathbb{E}[XY] = \mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y]$ only when $X$ and $Y$ are independent**. Students frequently confuse these two identities in KTU exams ŌĆö do not fall into this trap.

## 2.3 Indicator Random Variables ŌĆö The Workhorse of LoE

> [!NOTE] **Definition ŌĆö Indicator Random Variable**
> For an event $A \subseteq \Omega$, the **indicator variable** $I_A : \Omega \to \{0,1\}$ is defined by
>
> $$I_A(\omega) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \omega \in A \\ 0 & \text{if } \omega \notin A \end{cases}$$
>
> Its expectation is simply $\mathbb{E}[I_A] = \Pr[A]$.
>
> Any "counting" random variable (e.g., number of collisions, number of fixed points, number of inversions) can be decomposed as a sum of indicators, after which LoE applies instantly.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Identity | Formula | Validity | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|----------|-------|
| 1 | **Expectation of a constant** | $\mathbb{E}[c] = c$ | Always | $c$ is a degenerate RV |
| 2 | **Scaling** | $\mathbb{E}[aX] = a\,\mathbb{E}[X]$ | Always | $a \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 3 | **Translation** | $\mathbb{E}[X+b] = \mathbb{E}[X] + b$ | Always | Additive constant |
| 4 | **Two-variable LoE** | $\mathbb{E}[X+Y] = \mathbb{E}[X] + \mathbb{E}[Y]$ | **Always, no independence needed** | ŌŁÉ Board favourite |
| 5 | **$n$-variable LoE** | $\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[X_i]$ | **Always** | ŌŁÉ Board favourite |
| 6 | **Product (with independence)** | $\mathbb{E}[XY] = \mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y]$ | **Only if $X \perp Y$** | Often confused with #4 |
| 7 | **Indicator expectation** | $\mathbb{E}[I_A] = \Pr[A]$ | Always | Foundation of LoE proofs |
| 8 | **Counting via indicators** | $X = \sum_i I_{A_i} \Rightarrow \mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_i \Pr[A_i]$ | Always | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ Most-used KTU technique |
| 9 | **Sum of fair coin flips** | $\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i\right] = n/2$ | Always, $B_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(1/2)$ | $B_i$ need not be independent |
| 10 | **Total expectation (law)** | $\mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_y \mathbb{E}[X \mid Y=y]\Pr[Y=y]$ | Always | Useful conditioning tool |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Network Load Balancing** | Expected maximum load on a server using balls-into-bins hashing |
| **Cryptographic Security Analysis** | Expected collisions in hash functions (birthday paradox) |
| **Reliability Engineering** | Expected number of component failures in a parallel redundant system |
| **Machine Learning** | Expected loss in ensemble methods (bagging, random forests) |
| **Computational Biology** | Expected number of motif occurrences in random DNA strings |
| **Wireless Networks** | Expected interference in ALOHA-type protocols |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Rigorous Proof of Linearity of Expectation (Discrete Case)

**Theorem.** For two discrete random variables $X, Y$ defined on the same probability space with finite expectations,
$$\mathbb{E}[X + Y] = \mathbb{E}[X] + \mathbb{E}[Y].$$

**Proof.** We proceed via the *summation-over-outcomes* definition. Let $\Omega$ be the (countable or finite) sample space with probability mass function $\Pr[\omega]$ for each $\omega \in \Omega$.

By definition,
$$\mathbb{E}[X + Y] = \sum_{\omega \in \Omega} (X(\omega) + Y(\omega)) \cdot \Pr[\omega]$$

Split the sum over the additive operator:
$$= \sum_{\omega \in \Omega} X(\omega)\Pr[\omega] + \sum_{\omega \in \Omega} Y(\omega)\Pr[\omega]$$

The first term is $\mathbb{E}[X]$ by definition; the second is $\mathbb{E}[Y]$ by definition. Therefore,
$$\mathbb{E}[X + Y] = \mathbb{E}[X] + \mathbb{E}[Y]. \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Worked Example #1 ŌĆö Sum of Two Fair Dice

Let $X, Y$ be the face values of two independent fair dice, each uniform on $\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$. Find $\mathbb{E}[X+Y]$.

**Method A (Direct via convolution ŌĆö painful):**
The sum $S = X+Y$ ranges from 2 to 12 with the familiar triangular pmf.

$$\mathbb{E}[S] = \sum_{s=2}^{12} s \cdot \Pr[S=s] = 2\cdot\tfrac{1}{36} + 3\cdot\tfrac{2}{36} + \ldots + 12\cdot\tfrac{1}{36}$$

This requires careful computation.

**Method B (Linearity ŌĆö elegant):**

$$\mathbb{E}[X] = \mathbb{E}[Y] = \frac{1+2+3+4+5+6}{6} = \frac{21}{6} = 3.5$$

By linearity of expectation,
$$\mathbb{E}[X+Y] = \mathbb{E}[X] + \mathbb{E}[Y] = 3.5 + 3.5 = 7$$

> [!TIP] **Note:** Linearity worked even though $X$ and $Y$ are independent here ŌĆö but the theorem does **not** require independence. The dice example illustrates the simplicity of the LoE route vs. the convolution route.

## 3.3 Worked Example #2 ŌĆö Hat-Check Problem (Classic LoE)

**Problem:** $n$ attendees check their hats. The hats are returned **randomly** (uniformly random permutation). Let $X$ be the number of attendees who receive their **own** hat. Compute $\mathbb{E}[X]$.

**Solution using indicator variables + LoE.**

Define for each $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ the indicator
$$I_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if person } i \text{ receives their own hat} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Then the total number of correct returns is
$$X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i$$

By linearity of expectation,
$$\mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[I_i] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Pr[I_i = 1]$$

By symmetry, the probability that person $i$ gets hat $i$ in a uniformly random permutation is
$$\Pr[I_i = 1] = \frac{1}{n}$$

Therefore,
$$\mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{n} = n \cdot \frac{1}{n} = 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Result:** The expected number of correct hats is **always exactly 1**, no matter how large $n$ is. The variance grows with $n$, but the mean remains anchored at 1. This counterintuitive result is a hallmark example demonstrating the power of LoE.

## 3.4 Worked Example #3 ŌĆö Expected Number of Coin Tosses Until $k$ Heads

Let $N$ be the number of independent fair coin tosses needed to obtain $k$ heads. Find $\mathbb{E}[N]$.

**Decomposition:** Define $N_j$ = number of tosses needed to obtain the $j$-th head *after* the $(j-1)$-th head. Then
$$N = \sum_{j=1}^{k} N_j$$

Each $N_j$ is a geometric random variable (number of tosses until the next head) with success probability $p = 1/2$, so
$$\mathbb{E}[N_j] = \frac{1}{p} = 2$$

By linearity,
$$\mathbb{E}[N] = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \mathbb{E}[N_j] = \sum_{j=1}^{k} 2 = 2k$$

This holds **regardless of whether the $N_j$'s are independent** (they actually are, here) ŌĆö but the proof would work even without that assumption.

## 3.5 Worked Example #4 ŌĆö Birthday Paradox via LoE

**Setup:** Throw $n$ balls uniformly into $m$ bins (m days in a year). Let $C$ be the number of collisions (pairs of balls sharing a bin). Compute $\mathbb{E}[C]$.

**Indicator variables:** For each pair $(i,j)$ with $1 \le i < j \le n$, define
$$I_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if balls } i \text{ and } j \text{ land in the same bin} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Then
$$C = \sum_{1 \le i < j \le n} I_{ij}$$

The probability of collision for a specific pair is
$$\Pr[I_{ij} = 1] = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Pr[\text{ball } i \to k] \cdot \Pr[\text{ball } j \to k] = m \cdot \frac{1}{m} \cdot \frac{1}{m} = \frac{1}{m}$$

By LoE,
$$\mathbb{E}[C] = \binom{n}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{m} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2m}$$

> [!TIP] **Engineering Insight:** Setting $\mathbb{E}[C] = 1$ yields the famous birthday threshold $n \approx \sqrt{2m \ln 2} \approx 1.18\sqrt{m}$. For $m = 365$ days, $n \approx 23$ people ŌĆö a collision is expected.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Common Exam Variants

| Variant | Random Variable | Indicator Decomposition | Final Answer |
|---------|----------------|------------------------|--------------|
| Fixed points in random permutation | Number of fixed points | $\sum_i I_{\{\pi(i)=i\}}$ | $1$ |
| Inversions in random permutation | Number of pairs $(i,j)$ with $i<j$ but $\pi(i)>\pi(j)$ | $\sum_{i<j} I_{\{i<j, \pi(i)>\pi(j)\}}$ | $\binom{n}{2}/2$ |
| Coin tosses to $k$ heads | Tosses needed | $\sum_{j=1}^{k} N_j$ (geometric) | $2k$ |
| Hat-check correct | Correct hats | $\sum_i I_{\{i\text{-th person correct}\}}$ | $1$ |
| Birthday collisions | Collisions in $m$ bins | $\sum_{i<j} I_{\{\text{both in same bin}\}}$ | $\binom{n}{2}/m$ |
| Coupon collector (last coupon) | Total draws to collect all $m$ | $\sum_{j=0}^{m-1} G_j$ (geometric) | $m H_m \approx m \ln m$ |
| Max load in $n$ balls, $n$ bins | Maximum occupancy | Harder ŌĆö use indicator + union bound | $\Theta(\ln n / \ln \ln n)$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow ŌĆö Application of LoE in Algorithm Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Complex Random Quantity Q] --> B{Is Q a counting variable?}
    B -- NO --> C[Use direct definition of expectation]
    B -- YES --> D[Decompose Q as sum of indicator variables]
    D --> E[For each event A_i, define I_i = 1 if A_i else 0]
    E --> F[Q = I_1 + I_2 + ... + I_n]
    F --> G[Apply Linearity of Expectation]
    G --> H["E[Q] = sum over i of Pr[A_i]"]
    H --> I{Do we know Pr[A_i]?}
    I -- YES --> J[Substitute and simplify closed form]
    I -- NO --> K[Use complementary counting or symmetry]
    J --> L[Final Answer: E[Q] = numerical / asymptotic expression]
    K --> L
    C --> L
```

## 4.2 Dependency Independence Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Sample Space Omega]
        O[Outcome omega]
    end

    subgraph RV[Random Variables]
        X["X(omega)"]
        Y["Y(omega)"]
        Z["Z(omega)"]
    end

    subgraph INDICATORS[Indicator Decomposition]
        I1["I_1 = 1 if event A_1"]
        I2["I_2 = 1 if event A_2"]
        IN["I_n = 1 if event A_n"]
    end

    subgraph EXPECTED[Expected Value Engine]
        EX["E[I_1] = Pr[A_1]"]
        EY["E[I_2] = Pr[A_2]"]
        EN["E[I_n] = Pr[A_n]"]
    end

    subgraph RESULT[Final Result]
        SUM["E[sum I_i] = sum E[I_i]"]
        OUT[Expected Count Q]
    end

    O --> X
    O --> Y
    O --> Z
    X --> I1
    Y --> I2
    Z --> IN
    I1 --> EX
    I2 --> EY
    IN --> EN
    EX --> SUM
    EY --> SUM
    EN --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Pipeline ŌĆö Three-Stage Reduction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Stage 1: Identify the random quantity of interest Q"] --> S2["Stage 2: Express Q = sum of simple indicators I_i"]
    S2 --> S3["Stage 3: Compute E[Q] = sum Pr[A_i] via LoE"]
    S3 --> S4["Output: Closed-form expected value, often O-of-something"]

    S1 -.guiding question.-> Q1["What am I counting?"]
    S2 -.guiding question.-> Q2["Can each event be checked independently?"]
    S3 -.guiding question.-> Q3["Can I compute Pr[A_i] using symmetry?"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

State the **Linearity of Expectation** theorem. Explain in one sentence why it is more powerful than the formula $\mathbb{E}[XY] = \mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y]$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement:** For any two random variables $X$ and $Y$ defined on the same probability space,
>
> $$\mathbb{E}[X + Y] = \mathbb{E}[X] + \mathbb{E}[Y].$$
>
> More generally, $\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[X_i]$ for any finite collection of random variables.
>
> **Why more powerful:** Linearity of expectation holds **without any independence assumption**, whereas $\mathbb{E}[XY] = \mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y]$ is valid **only when $X$ and $Y$ are independent**. (3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for general form, 1 for distinction)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Model Paper]**

Define an **indicator random variable**. Using it, express the expected number of heads in $n$ independent fair coin tosses.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An indicator random variable $I_A$ associated with event $A$ takes the value $1$ if $A$ occurs and $0$ otherwise. Its expectation is $\mathbb{E}[I_A] = \Pr[A]$.
>
> Let $H_i = 1$ if the $i$-th toss is heads, else $0$. The total number of heads is $H = \sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i$. By LoE,
>
> $$\mathbb{E}[H] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[H_i] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2} = \frac{n}{2}.$$
>
> (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for decomposition, 1 for final answer)

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls**
> * **Do not** confuse linearity of expectation with independence. Many students write "since $X_i$ are independent, by LoE..." and lose **2 marks** for an incorrect justification. LoE requires **no** independence.
> * **Always** show the indicator variable definition explicitly before summation ŌĆö students who skip this lose **1ŌĆō2 marks**.
> * **Final answer must be a clean closed-form expression.** Leaving it as a summation symbol costs the last 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> * For **14-mark** questions, the mark split is typically **(a) = 7 marks**, **(b) = 7 marks**. Each sub-part requires a clear **statement**, a **decomposition**, a **computation**, and a **final boxed answer**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

State and prove the linearity of expectation for two random variables. Show with an example involving two *dependent* random variables that the theorem still holds.

**(7 marks ŌĆö Model Solution)**

**Statement:** For any two random variables $X$ and $Y$ defined on the same probability space $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, \Pr)$,
$$\mathbb{E}[X+Y] = \mathbb{E}[X] + \mathbb{E}[Y].$$

**Proof (Discrete case):**
By the summation-over-outcomes definition,
$$\mathbb{E}[X+Y] = \sum_{\omega \in \Omega} (X(\omega) + Y(\omega)) \cdot \Pr[\omega]$$
Split the sum over the additive operator:
$$= \sum_{\omega \in \Omega} X(\omega) \Pr[\omega] + \sum_{\omega \in \Omega} Y(\omega) \Pr[\omega]$$
The first sum is $\mathbb{E}[X]$; the second is $\mathbb{E}[Y]$.
$$\therefore \mathbb{E}[X+Y] = \mathbb{E}[X] + \mathbb{E}[Y]. \quad \blacksquare$$

**Dependent Counter-example:** Let $X$ be uniform on $\{0,1\}$ and define $Y = 1 - X$. Clearly $X$ and $Y$ are dependent (perfectly anti-correlated). Compute:
$$\mathbb{E}[X] = 0.5, \qquad \mathbb{E}[Y] = 0.5$$
$$\mathbb{E}[X+Y] = \mathbb{E}[1] = 1 = 0.5 + 0.5 \checkmark$$
LoE holds even though $X$ and $Y$ are not independent. **(7 marks: 2 statement, 3 proof, 2 example)**

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

A bag contains **3 red** and **5 blue** balls. Two balls are drawn **without replacement**. Let $X$ = number of red balls drawn. Compute $\mathbb{E}[X]$ using indicator random variables and linearity of expectation.

**(7 marks ŌĆö Model Solution)**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define indicators:** Let $I_i$ = 1 if the $i$-th draw is red, else 0, for $i = 1, 2$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express total:** $X = I_1 + I_2$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute marginal probabilities:**

For draw 1:
$$\Pr[I_1 = 1] = \frac{3}{8}$$

For draw 2 (law of total probability):
$$\Pr[I_2 = 1] = \Pr[I_2=1 \mid I_1=1]\Pr[I_1=1] + \Pr[I_2=1 \mid I_1=0]\Pr[I_1=0]$$
$$= \frac{2}{7} \cdot \frac{3}{8} + \frac{3}{7} \cdot \frac{5}{8} = \frac{6}{56} + \frac{15}{56} = \frac{21}{56} = \frac{3}{8}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply LoE:**
$$\mathbb{E}[X] = \mathbb{E}[I_1] + \mathbb{E}[I_2] = \frac{3}{8} + \frac{3}{8} = \frac{6}{8} = \frac{3}{4}$$

> [!NOTE] **Sanity check (direct method):** $X$ can be 0, 1, or 2. $\Pr[X=0] = \frac{\binom{5}{2}}{\binom{8}{2}} = \frac{10}{28}$, $\Pr[X=1] = \frac{\binom{3}{1}\binom{5}{1}}{\binom{8}{2}} = \frac{15}{28}$, $\Pr[X=2] = \frac{\binom{3}{2}}{\binom{8}{2}} = \frac{3}{28}$. Then $\mathbb{E}[X] = 0 + \tfrac{15}{28} + 2 \cdot \tfrac{3}{28} = \tfrac{21}{28} = \tfrac{3}{4}$. Ō£ō

**(7 marks: 1 indicator def, 1 decomposition, 2 probability calcs, 2 LoE application, 1 final boxed answer)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]**

Consider a random permutation $\pi$ of $\{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$. A **fixed point** is an index $i$ such that $\pi(i) = i$. Using indicator random variables and linearity of expectation, derive a formula for the expected number of fixed points.

**(7 marks ŌĆö Model Solution)**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define indicators:** For each $i \in \{1, \ldots, n\}$, define
$$I_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \pi(i) = i \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Total count:** Let $F$ = total fixed points. Then
$$F = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Marginal probabilities:** In a uniformly random permutation, by symmetry, each index $i$ has probability $1/n$ of being a fixed point:
$$\Pr[I_i = 1] = \frac{(n-1)!}{n!} = \frac{1}{n}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply LoE:**
$$\mathbb{E}[F] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[I_i] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{n} = 1$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Remark:** $\mathbb{E}[F] = 1$ **regardless of $n$**, a striking and counter-intuitive result. The variance is also 1 (proved via inclusion-exclusion, beyond syllabus scope).

**(7 marks: 1 indicator def, 1 decomposition, 2 probability calc, 2 LoE, 1 final boxed answer + remark)**

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]**

A random binary string of length $n$ is generated where each bit is independently $1$ with probability $p$ and $0$ with probability $1-p$. Let $Y$ be the number of occurrences of the pattern **"10"** (a 1 followed immediately by a 0) as a substring. Using linearity of expectation, derive a closed-form expression for $\mathbb{E}[Y]$.

**(7 marks ŌĆö Model Solution)**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define indicators:** For each position $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}$, define
$$I_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if bits at positions } i \text{ and } i+1 \text{ are "10"} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Total count:**
$$Y = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} I_i$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Probability per position:** Bits at positions $i$ and $i+1$ are independent. So
$$\Pr[I_i = 1] = \Pr[\text{bit}_i = 1] \cdot \Pr[\text{bit}_{i+1} = 0] = p(1-p)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply LoE:**
$$\mathbb{E}[Y] = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \mathbb{E}[I_i] = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} p(1-p) = (n-1)\,p(1-p)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Special case:** When $p = 1/2$, $\mathbb{E}[Y] = \dfrac{n-1}{4}$.

**(7 marks: 1 indicator def, 1 decomposition, 2 probability, 2 LoE, 1 final answer)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision ŌĆö LoE Cheat Sheet**
>
> Ō£ģ **Theorem:** $\mathbb{E}[X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_n] = \sum \mathbb{E}[X_i]$ ŌĆö **no independence required**.
>
> Ō£ģ **Scaling:** $\mathbb{E}[aX + b] = a\mathbb{E}[X] + b$.
>
> Ō£ģ **Indicator variables:** $I_A$ takes values $\{0, 1\}$; $\mathbb{E}[I_A] = \Pr[A]$.
>
> Ō£ģ **Counting trick:** Any count $Q = \sum_i I_{A_i} \Rightarrow \mathbb{E}[Q] = \sum_i \Pr[A_i]$.
>
> Ō£ģ **Independence is NOT required** for LoE ŌĆö students confuse this with $\mathbb{E}[XY] = \mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y]$, which **does** require independence.
>
> Ō£ģ **Classic results to memorize:**
>    ŌĆó Hat-check: $\mathbb{E}[\text{correct}] = 1$
>    ŌĆó Fixed points in permutation: $\mathbb{E} = 1$
>    ŌĆó Inversions in permutation: $\mathbb{E} = \binom{n}{2}/2$
>    ŌĆó Birthday collisions: $\mathbb{E} = \binom{n}{2}/m$
>    ŌĆó Coin tosses to $k$ heads: $\mathbb{E} = 2k$
>    ŌĆó Coupon collector: $\mathbb{E} = m H_m \approx m \ln m$
>
> Ō£ģ **Always write** the indicator definition explicitly **before** summation in exam answers.
>
> Ō£ģ **Final answer** must be a **closed-form expression** (no leftover $\sum$ symbols).
>
> Ō£ģ **Anti-pattern to avoid:** Never write "since $X_i$ are independent, by LoE..." ŌĆö LoE is *independent* of independence!

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Randomized Algorithms: Randomized Quicksort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST639 - RANDOMIZED ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Randomized Algorithms: Randomized Quicksort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Randomized Quicksort** is a randomized variant of the classical divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm Quicksort, in which the **pivot element** used to partition the input array is selected **uniformly at random** from the sub-array being sorted, rather than being chosen deterministically (e.g., always the first or last element).

Formally, on an input array $A[1 \ldots n]$, the algorithm proceeds as follows:

1. If $n \le 1$, return the array (base case).
2. **Randomly select** an index $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ uniformly at random.
3. Use $A[i]$ as the pivot to partition $A$ into three sub-arrays:
   - $L = \{x \in A : x < \text{pivot}\}$
   - $E = \{x \in A : x = \text{pivot}\}$
   - $R = \{x \in A : x > \text{pivot}\}$
4. Recursively sort $L$ and $R$, then concatenate $L \oplus E \oplus R$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Randomized Quicksort is the canonical "Hello World" example of randomized algorithms. It demonstrates how injecting a single random bit stream transforms an algorithm with worst-case $\Theta(n^2)$ behaviour into one whose *expected* running time is $O(n \log n)$ ŌĆö for *every* possible input.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Shuffled Deck" Intuition

Imagine you are a teacher tasked with arranging **100 students in a classroom by roll number**. 

- **Deterministic Quicksort (worst-case-prone):** You always pick the *first* student standing near the door as your "reference" (pivot). If students walk in already sorted by roll number, you end up with one empty side every time ŌĆö painfully slow.
- **Randomized Quicksort:** You *shuffle the students randomly* before starting, then pick any student at random as your reference. The chance that the random student happens to be near the sorted extreme is tiny ŌĆö and *this property holds for any input*, because the randomness is in your action, not in the data.

The algorithm **does not rely on the input being random**; it **makes itself random** so that adversarial inputs cannot predictably produce the worst case.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Pivot** | The randomly chosen element used to partition the array |
| **Partition** | Rearrangement step producing $L$, $E$, $R$ sub-arrays |
| **Indicator Random Variable $X_{ij}$** | Binary variable: 1 if elements $z_i$ and $z_j$ are ever compared |
| **Expected Cost** | Average cost over the algorithm's *internal coin flips*, for a *fixed* input |
| **Random Sampling** | Selecting pivot by drawing index uniformly from $\{1,\dots,n\}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> A subtle but exam-critical distinction: in randomized quicksort, the expectation $E[T(n)]$ is taken over the **random choices of the algorithm**, *not* over a distribution on inputs. This means the $O(n \log n)$ expected bound holds **for every input instance** simultaneously ŌĆö a stronger guarantee than average-case analysis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Randomize? ŌĆö The Adversarial Problem

Consider deterministic quicksort that always picks the *first* element as pivot. For input:
$$A = \{1, 2, 3, 4, \ldots, n\}$$
the partition always produces a left sub-array of size $0$ and a right sub-array of size $n-1$, yielding the recurrence $T(n) = T(n-1) + \Theta(n)$, which solves to $\Theta(n^2)$.

An **adversary** knowing your strategy can craft such inputs deliberately. Randomization defeats this adversary by ensuring the pivot is unpredictable.

## 2.2 The Algorithm (Lomuto-style Partition with Random Pivot)

```
RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, p, r)
  if p < r
      q = RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)
      RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, p, q - 1)
      RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, q + 1, r)

RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)
  i = RANDOM(p, r)                // uniform random index
  exchange A[r] Ōåö A[i]            // bring pivot to end
  return PARTITION(A, p, r)      // standard Lomuto

PARTITION(A, p, r)
  x = A[r]                        // pivot
  i = p - 1
  for j = p to r - 1
      if A[j] Ōēż x
          i = i + 1
          exchange A[i] Ōåö A[j]
  exchange A[i + 1] Ōåö A[r]
  return i + 1
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Definition / Formula | Notes |
|--------|---------------------|-------|
| $T(n)$ | Expected running time on $n$ elements | Over random coin flips |
| Recurrence | $T(n) = \frac{2}{n}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} T(k) + \Theta(n)$ | Average over pivot rank $k$ |
| Closed form | $T(n) \le 2n \ln n$ | Where $\ln$ is natural log |
| Equivalent | $T(n) \le 1.39 \, n \lg n$ | $\lg = \log_2$, since $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ |
| Indicator var | $X_{ij} = 1$ iff $z_i, z_j$ ever compared | $z_i < z_j$ in sorted order |
| Probability | $\Pr[X_{ij} = 1] = \dfrac{2}{j - i + 1}$ | Only pivot in $[z_i, z_j]$ causes comparison |
| Expected comparisons | $E[X] = \sum_{i<j} \dfrac{2}{j-i+1} < 2n \ln n$ | Dominant cost in quicksort |
| Worst-case prob. | $\Pr[\text{time} = \Omega(n^2)] \le \dfrac{1}{n!}$ | Vanishingly small |
| Variance bound | $\Pr[T(n) > 2cn\ln n] \le \dfrac{1}{n^c}$ | Markov-style tail bound |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to memorise for KTU:** $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$, so $2 \ln n = 2 \ln 2 \cdot \lg n \approx 1.386 \lg n$. The "$1.39$" factor is a **favourite examiner number** ŌĆö show the conversion explicitly in derivations.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Randomized quicksort is the **default sorting routine** in the C standard library (`qsort`) and many production systems (e.g., Java's `Arrays.sort` for primitive arrays uses a tuned dual-pivot variant). Its appeal is:
- **No worst-case inputs exist** that can consistently defeat it (unlike deterministic versions).
- **No input-dependent assumptions** needed (unlike introsort, which detects pathological recursion depth).
- **Cache-friendly** partitioning patterns in practice.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Expected Running Time via Indicator Random Variables

**Setup:** Let the input contain $n$ distinct elements. After sorting, denote them in sorted order as $z_1 < z_2 < \cdots < z_n$. Define the indicator random variable:

$$X_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } z_i \text{ and } z_j \text{ are compared at some point} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

for all $1 \le i < j \le n$. The total number of comparisons is:

$$X = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} X_{ij}$$

### Step 1: Compute $\Pr[X_{ij} = 1]$

Consider the sub-array consisting of elements $\{z_i, z_{i+1}, \ldots, z_j\}$. Two elements $z_i$ and $z_j$ are compared **iff** one of them is chosen as a pivot before any element from the *interior* $\{z_{i+1}, \ldots, z_{j-1}\}$ is chosen.

- **Case A:** $z_i$ is chosen as pivot first. Then $z_i$ is compared with $z_j$ (every pivot is compared with elements on both sides).
- **Case B:** $z_j$ is chosen as pivot first. Symmetrically, $z_i$ is compared with $z_j$.
- **Case C:** Some interior element $z_k$ (with $i < k < j$) is chosen first. Then $z_i$ goes entirely into one sub-array, $z_j$ into another, and they are **never compared again**.

The sub-array has $k = j - i + 1$ elements, each equally likely to be the pivot. Since the events "any of the two endpoints is the first pivot selected" are disjoint:

$$\Pr[X_{ij} = 1] = \frac{1}{j - i + 1} + \frac{1}{j - i + 1} = \frac{2}{j - i + 1}$$

### Step 2: Apply Linearity of Expectation

By linearity of expectation:

$$E[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} E[X_{ij}] = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} \frac{2}{j - i + 1}$$

### Step 3: Change of Variables

Let $k = j - i + 1$. As $j$ runs from $i+1$ to $n$, $k$ runs from $2$ to $n - i + 1$. For each fixed $k$, the number of pairs $(i,j)$ is $n - k + 1$. Thus:

$$E[X] = \sum_{k=2}^{n} (n - k + 1) \cdot \frac{2}{k} = \sum_{k=2}^{n} \frac{2(n - k + 1)}{k}$$

### Step 4: Bound the Sum

Using the upper bound $n - k + 1 < n$:

$$E[X] < \sum_{k=2}^{n} \frac{2n}{k} = 2n \sum_{k=2}^{n} \frac{1}{k}$$

By the harmonic series approximation $\sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{k} \le \ln n + 1$:

$$E[X] < 2n \left( \ln n + 1 \right) - 2n \cdot \frac{1}{1} = 2n \ln n - 2n + 2n \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$

More cleanly, using $\sum_{k=2}^{n} \frac{1}{k} < \ln n$:

$$\boxed{E[X] < 2n \ln n}$$

### Step 5: Convert to Base-2 Logarithm

Using $\ln n = \ln 2 \cdot \lg n$:

$$E[X] < 2 \ln 2 \cdot n \lg n \approx 1.386 \, n \lg n$$

The partition cost at each level adds a linear term $\Theta(n)$ per level of recursion; with the expected $\Theta(\lg n)$ levels, the **expected total running time** is:

$$E[T(n)] = O(n \log n)$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Expected Running Time via Recurrence

**Alternative proof.** Let $k$ be the rank (1-indexed position in sorted order) of the randomly chosen pivot. Each value of $k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ is equally likely with probability $1/n$. The pivot splits the array into sub-arrays of sizes $k-1$ and $n-k$. Hence:

$$T(n) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \bigl[ T(k-1) + T(n-k) \bigr] + cn$$

By symmetry, $T(k-1)$ and $T(n-k)$ have the same distribution over the sum; relabelling $k-1 \mapsto k$ in the first term:

$$T(n) = \frac{2}{n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} T(k) + cn$$

### Step 6: Substitution Method ŌĆö Claim $T(n) \le a n \ln n$

Substitute the guess into the RHS:

$$T(n) \le \frac{2}{n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} a k \ln k + cn = \frac{2a}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} k \ln k + cn$$

(we drop $k=0$ since $0 \cdot \ln 0 = 0$ by convention.)

### Step 7: Bound the Sum by an Integral

Since $x \ln x$ is monotonically increasing for $x \ge 1$:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n-1} k \ln k \le \int_{1}^{n} x \ln x \, dx$$

### Step 8: Evaluate the Integral

Using integration by parts with $u = \ln x$, $dv = x \, dx$:

$$\int x \ln x \, dx = \frac{x^2}{2} \ln x - \int \frac{x^2}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{x} \, dx = \frac{x^2}{2} \ln x - \frac{x^2}{4} + C$$

Therefore:

$$\int_{1}^{n} x \ln x \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} \ln x - \frac{x^2}{4} \right]_{1}^{n} = \frac{n^2}{2} \ln n - \frac{n^2}{4} - \left( 0 - \frac{1}{4} \right)$$

$$= \frac{n^2}{2} \ln n - \frac{n^2}{4} + \frac{1}{4}$$

### Step 9: Substitute Back

$$T(n) \le \frac{2a}{n} \left( \frac{n^2}{2} \ln n - \frac{n^2}{4} + \frac{1}{4} \right) + cn$$

$$= a n \ln n - \frac{an}{2} + \frac{a}{2n} + cn$$

### Step 10: Choose $a$ to Absorb Linear Terms

Choose $a \ge 2c$ so that $-\frac{an}{2} + cn \le 0$. For $n \ge 1$, the term $\frac{a}{2n}$ is negligible. Hence:

$$T(n) \le a n \ln n$$

**Conclusion:** $T(n) = O(n \log n)$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Probability of a Bad Run

Suppose we run randomized quicksort on $n$ elements. The pivot at every level of recursion is chosen independently and uniformly. The *only* way to get worst-case $\Theta(n^2)$ behaviour is if **every pivot selected** is at an extreme position (rank 1 or rank $n$). The probability that a single pivot is at an extreme is $\frac{2}{n}$. For all $\Theta(n)$ recursive calls to be extreme:

$$\Pr[\text{worst case}] \le \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{2}{n-i+1} = \frac{2^n}{n!} \le \frac{1}{n!}$$

(using $n! \ge 2^n$ for $n \ge 1$, with equality only at $n=1,2$). By Stirling's approximation, $n! \ge (n/e)^n$, so this probability vanishes super-exponentially.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Algorithm Flow

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the complete control flow of randomized quicksort, including the random pivot selection step, the partition step, and the recursive calls.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Randomized Quicksort] --> B{Array size > 1?}
    B -- No --> C[Return base case]
    B -- Yes --> D[Generate random index i uniformly from p to r]
    D --> E[Swap A with r-th element to bring pivot to end]
    E --> F[Run Lomuto Partition on A]
    F --> G[Partition yields index q such that elements left of q are less than pivot]
    G --> H[Recurse on left subarray A from p to q minus 1]
    G --> I[Recurse on right subarray A from q plus 1 to r]
    H --> J[Combine sorted left sorted pivot sorted right]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Return sorted array]
    C --> K

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    style K fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656,color:#000
    style F fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Recursion Tree of a Single Run

The diagram below shows a representative recursion tree where the random pivot selection produces a *balanced* split ŌĆö the typical case for randomized quicksort.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["Array of 8 elements"] --> L1["Left subarray size 3"]
    root --> M1["Pivot 5 single element"]
    root --> R1["Right subarray size 4"]

    L1 --> L2a["Size 1 base case"]
    L1 --> L2b["Size 1 base case"]
    L1 --> L2c["Size 1 base case"]

    R1 --> RL["Left size 1"]
    R1 --> RR["Right size 2"]

    RR --> RR1["Size 1 base case"]
    RR --> RR2["Size 1 base case"]

    style root fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,color:#000
    style L1 fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#D79B00,color:#000
    style R1 fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#D79B00,color:#000
    style M1 fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Comparison-Decision Block Diagram

This diagram captures the **logical decision rule** for when two elements $z_i$ and $z_j$ get compared ŌĆö the central quantity in the indicator-variable analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In["Input: elements z_i and z_j with i less than j"] --> Check{Is the first pivot selected from the closed interval z_i to z_j one of the endpoints?}
    Check -- Yes, pivot is z_i --> YesCmp["Comparison occurs: X_ij equals 1"]
    Check -- Yes, pivot is z_j --> YesCmp
    Check -- No, interior element chosen --> NoCmp["No comparison: X_ij equals 0"]
    YesCmp --> Prob["Probability equals 2 divided by j minus i plus 1"]
    NoCmp --> Prob1["Probability equals 1 minus 2 divided by j minus i plus 1"]

    style In fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    style YesCmp fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000
    style NoCmp fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450,color:#000
    style Prob fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656,color:#000
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between deterministic quicksort and randomized quicksort. Why does randomization help in achieving expected $O(n \log n)$ time?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Deterministic Quicksort | Randomized Quicksort |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Pivot selection | Fixed rule (first / last / median-of-three) | Uniformly random index |
| Worst-case input | Exists (e.g., sorted array for first-element pivot) | No input reliably triggers worst case |
| Analysis paradigm | Worst-case $\Theta(n^2)$ or average-case $O(n \log n)$ | Expected $O(n \log n)$ over *all* inputs |
| Adversary resistance | Vulnerable | Robust |

**Why randomization helps (3 marks):** The expected pivot rank is uniformly distributed over $\{1, \ldots, n\}$. The expected size of the larger partition is $\frac{2}{n}\sum_{k} \max(k, n-k) = \frac{2n}{3}$, giving a well-balanced expected split. This removes dependence on input ordering and bounds the expected recursion depth at $O(\log n)$, yielding $O(n \log n)$ total work.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define an indicator random variable. In the analysis of randomized quicksort, how is the indicator variable $X_{ij}$ defined, and what is $\Pr[X_{ij} = 1]$?**

**Model Answer:**

An **indicator random variable** $X$ is a Bernoulli random variable taking values in $\{0, 1\}$, where $E[X] = \Pr[X = 1]$. It is used to count occurrences of events whose probabilities are difficult to compute directly.

For randomized quicksort, let $z_1 < z_2 < \cdots < z_n$ be the input elements in sorted order. Define:

$$X_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } z_i \text{ and } z_j \text{ are directly compared during the execution} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The probability is:

$$\Pr[X_{ij} = 1] = \frac{2}{j - i + 1}$$

**Justification (3 marks):** $z_i$ and $z_j$ are compared iff one of them is chosen as the pivot *before* any element strictly between them. The sub-array $\{z_i, \ldots, z_j\}$ has $j-i+1$ elements, each equally likely to be the first pivot. Two of these (the endpoints) cause the comparison, so the probability is $\frac{2}{j-i+1}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Randomized Quicksort algorithm** with pseudocode. State clearly the role of the `RANDOM` function and how it differs from `RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT` running on already-sorted input.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Using **indicator random variables**, derive the expected number of comparisons performed by randomized quicksort on an input of $n$ distinct elements. Show all summation steps and the conversion between natural logarithm $\ln n$ and base-2 logarithm $\lg n$.

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a) ŌĆö Algorithm Explanation [7 marks]

**Pseudocode (4 marks):**

```
RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, p, r)
 1  if p < r
 2      q = RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)
 3      RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, p, q - 1)
 4      RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, q + 1, r)

RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)
 5  i = RANDOM(p, r)
 6  exchange A[r] with A[i]
 7  return PARTITION(A, p, r)
```

`RANDOM(p, r)` returns an integer uniformly at random from $\{p, p+1, \ldots, r\}$, using a pseudo-random generator (PRNG) seeded at program start.

**Role and difference (3 marks):**

- **Deterministic quicksort** on a sorted array using *first-element pivot* produces partitions of sizes $0$ and $n-1$, leading to $\Theta(n^2)$ behaviour.
- **Randomized quicksort** *swaps* a randomly chosen element to the pivot position *before* partitioning. On a sorted array, this swap introduces a random pivot, breaking the degenerate split. The expected behaviour is now $O(n \log n)$ because every input is now "equivalent" to the algorithm's view after the random shuffle.

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b) ŌĆö Expected Comparisons [7 marks]

**Step 1 ŌĆö Setup (1 mark):** Let $z_1 < z_2 < \cdots < z_n$ be the sorted input. Define $X_{ij}$ for $1 \le i < j \le n$ as the indicator that $z_i$ and $z_j$ are directly compared. Total comparisons $X = \sum_{i<j} X_{ij}$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Probability (2 marks):**

$$\Pr[X_{ij} = 1] = \frac{2}{j - i + 1}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Linearity of expectation (1 mark):**

$$E[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1}\sum_{j=i+1}^{n} \frac{2}{j-i+1}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitution $k = j-i+1$ and bound (2 marks):**

$$E[X] = \sum_{k=2}^{n} \frac{2(n-k+1)}{k} < \sum_{k=2}^{n} \frac{2n}{k} = 2n \sum_{k=2}^{n} \frac{1}{k} < 2n \ln n$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Conversion to $\lg n$ (1 mark):**

$$E[X] < 2n \ln 2 \cdot \lg n \approx 1.386 \, n \lg n$$

Therefore, randomized quicksort performs at most $1.386 \, n \lg n$ comparisons on average.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **recurrence relation** for the expected running time $T(n)$ of randomized quicksort by conditioning on the rank of the random pivot. Solve the recurrence using the **substitution method** to show $T(n) = O(n \log n)$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare randomized quicksort with **deterministic median-of-3 quicksort**. Discuss the worst-case, average-case, and the role of randomness in achieving robust performance.

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a) ŌĆö Recurrence and Substitution [7 marks]

**Step 1 ŌĆö Conditioning on pivot rank (2 marks):**

The randomly chosen pivot is equally likely to be the $k$-th smallest element for each $k \in \{1, \ldots, n\}$. If the pivot has rank $k$, the partition produces sub-arrays of sizes $k-1$ and $n-k$. Hence:

$$T(n) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \bigl[ T(k-1) + T(n-k) \bigr] + cn$$

The $cn$ term captures the linear cost of partitioning.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Symmetry simplification (1 mark):**

Since the sum $\sum_{k=1}^{n} T(n-k) = \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} T(j)$ and similarly $\sum_{k=1}^{n} T(k-1) = \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} T(j)$, we have:

$$T(n) = \frac{2}{n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} T(k) + cn$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitution guess (1 mark):**

Guess $T(n) \le a n \ln n$ for some constant $a > 0$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Integral bound (2 marks):**

$$T(n) \le \frac{2a}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} k \ln k + cn \le \frac{2a}{n} \int_{1}^{n} x \ln x \, dx + cn$$

$$= \frac{2a}{n} \left[ \frac{n^2}{2} \ln n - \frac{n^2}{4} + \frac{1}{4} \right] + cn = a n \ln n - \frac{an}{2} + \frac{a}{2n} + cn$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Choose $a$ (1 mark):**

Pick $a \ge 2c$. Then $-\frac{an}{2} + cn \le 0$, and for sufficiently large $n$, the $\frac{a}{2n}$ term is dominated by $a n \ln n$. So $T(n) \le a n \ln n$, confirming $T(n) = O(n \log n)$.

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b) ŌĆö Comparison [7 marks]

| Criterion | Deterministic Median-of-3 | Randomized Quicksort |
|-----------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| Pivot selection | Median of first, middle, last | Uniformly random index |
| Worst-case time | $\Theta(n^2)$ on crafted inputs (e.g., nearly-sorted adversarial sequences) | $\Theta(n^2)$ with probability $\le 1/n!$ |
| Average-case time | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| Adversary-resistant | No ŌĆö adversary knowing the rule can craft bad input | Yes ŌĆö randomness hides pivot choice |
| Determinism | Fully deterministic; reproducible | Non-deterministic (uses PRNG) |
| Extra cost | 3-element comparison per call | $O(1)$ per call for random number |

**Key insight (2 marks):** Median-of-3 is a *deterministic heuristic* that works well on typical inputs but can be defeated by an adversary who knows the heuristic. Randomized quicksort achieves *the same* asymptotic guarantee without needing the input to follow any pattern, because randomness is *internal* to the algorithm.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting linearity of expectation's role** ŌĆö many students compute each $E[X_{ij}]$ correctly but skip stating $E[X] = \sum E[X_{ij}]$. (ŌĆō1 mark)
> - **Confusing natural log $\ln$ with base-2 $\lg$** without showing the conversion $\ln n = \ln 2 \cdot \lg n$. Examiners explicitly check this. (ŌĆō1 mark)
> - **Treating the harmonic sum as exact**: $\sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{k} \ne \ln n$; you must use the inequality $\sum_{k=2}^{n} \frac{1}{k} < \ln n$. (ŌĆō1 mark)
> - **Claiming randomized quicksort has no worst case**: it *does*, but the probability of triggering it is at most $1/n!$, which is why we say "with high probability it runs in $O(n \log n)$."

### Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Algorithm:** Random index ŌåÆ swap with last ŌåÆ standard Lomuto partition ŌåÆ two recursive calls.
- **Expected time recurrence:** $T(n) = \frac{2}{n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} T(k) + \Theta(n)$.
- **Closed-form expected time:** $T(n) \le 2n \ln n \approx 1.386 \, n \lg n$.
- **Indicator variable:** $X_{ij} = 1$ iff $z_i, z_j$ directly compared.
- **Probability formula:** $\Pr[X_{ij} = 1] = \frac{2}{j-i+1}$.
- **Worst-case probability:** $\Pr[\Theta(n^2) \text{ run}] \le \frac{1}{n!}$.
- **Why randomization works:** Removes dependence on input ordering; expectation is over algorithm's coin flips, not over inputs.
- **Substitution method:** Assume $T(n) \le a n \ln n$; bound $\sum k \ln k$ by $\int x \ln x \, dx = \frac{n^2}{2} \ln n - \frac{n^2}{4}$.
- **Engineering use:** C `qsort`, dual-pivot variants in JDK; preferred over deterministic in production.
- **Variance and tail bounds:** Using Markov / Chebyshev, $\Pr[T > 2cn \ln n] \le 1/n^c$ ŌĆö the run-time concentration is tight.
- **Comparison to deterministic:** Median-of-3 heuristic avoids many bad cases but is *not* adversary-proof; randomized quicksort is.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Randomized Selection ŌĆö Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Selection Problem (Formal Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition ŌĆö The Selection Problem:**
> Given an unordered collection of $n$ comparable elements $A = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n\}$ and an integer $k$ with $1 \le k \le n$, the **Selection Problem** asks us to return the $k$-th smallest element of $A$ (equivalently, the element that would appear in position $k$ of the sorted array).

The problem has two important special cases that examiners love to test:
- **Minimum** ($k = 1$): trivially solved in $O(n)$ by a single linear scan.
- **Maximum** ($k = n$): also $O(n)$ trivially.
- **Median** ($k = \lceil n/2 \rceil$): the most statistically important variant ŌĆö central tendency.

A truly *general* selection algorithm must handle **any arbitrary $k$** without resorting to full sorting, because sorting costs $O(n \log n)$ and is wasteful when we only need ONE element.

## 1.2 The Randomized Solution ŌĆö Quick Select

**Randomized Selection** (popularly known as **Quick Select** or **Hoare's Selection Algorithm**, published by C. A. R. Hoare in 1961) is a randomized divide-and-conquer algorithm that finds the $k$-th smallest element in **expected linear time $O(n)$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Level Intuition ŌĆö The Hiring Analogy:**
> Imagine you are hiring one person out of $n$ candidates. Instead of ranking *all* of them (which takes time $\propto n \log n$, like asking everyone to compete in a round-robin tournament), you do something smarter:
> 1. You pick **one candidate at random** as a *reference point* (the "pivot").
> 2. You split everyone into three groups: those obviously worse, those obviously better, and the pivot itself.
> 3. You only continue interviewing the group that **actually contains your target rank $k$**. The other groups are instantly discarded.
>
> Because the pivot is random, on average it splits the group roughly in half, so each round you discard about half the candidates. The total interview time then becomes roughly $n + n/2 + n/4 + \ldots = 2n$ ŌĆö **linear!**

## 1.3 Why Randomization Helps

| Property | Deterministic Sorting-Based Selection | Randomized Selection (Quick Select) |
|---|---|---|
| Time Complexity (Worst) | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Time Complexity (Expected) | $O(n \log n)$ | $\mathbf{O(n)}$ |
| Time Complexity (Best Deterministic ŌĆö Median of Medians) | $O(n)$ | ŌĆö |
| Random Bits Required | 0 | $O(n)$ |
| In-Place Operation | No | **Yes** |
| Practical Constant Factors | Moderate | **Small** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The *worst case* of randomized selection is the same as quick sort's worst case ($O(n^2)$), but the *probability* of hitting that worst case is astronomically small ($2^{-n}$). For all practical and examination purposes, the algorithm runs in **expected $O(n)$** time. This separation between *expected* and *worst-case* complexity is the cornerstone idea of randomized algorithm design and a frequent exam question.

## 1.4 Inputs and Outputs ŌĆö Algorithmic Contract

- **Input:** A sequence of $n$ numbers $A[1 \ldots n]$ (no order assumed) and an integer $1 \le k \le n$.
- **Output:** The element $x \in A$ such that **exactly $k - 1$ elements of $A$ are strictly less than $x$**, and exactly $n - k$ elements are strictly greater than $x$.
- **Side Effect:** The input array $A$ is permuted in-place during the partition step.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Logical Phases of Randomized Selection

The algorithm operates in three recursive phases:

### Phase 1 ŌĆö Randomized Pivot Sampling
Choose an index $i$ uniformly at random from $\{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$. Set `pivot ŌåÉ A[i]`. This is the *only* source of randomness in the entire algorithm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why uniform random?** Any non-uniform distribution biases the expected size of the recursive sub-problem. Uniform sampling ensures each rank position $r$ (for $1 \le r \le n$) is chosen as pivot with probability exactly $1/n$, giving an unbiased expectation.

### Phase 2 ŌĆö Partition Around the Pivot
Rearrange $A$ in-place into three conceptual regions:
- $A[1 \ldots q-1]$: elements **strictly less than** pivot.
- $A[q \ldots q+s-1]$: elements **equal to** pivot (block of size $s$, since duplicates may exist).
- $A[q+s \ldots n]$: elements **strictly greater than** pivot.

The quantity $q$ (the **rank** of the pivot) tells us where we stand relative to the desired $k$.

### Phase 3 ŌĆö Branch Selection (The Three-Case Decision)
- If $k < q$: recurse on the **left sub-array** $A[1 \ldots q-1]$ looking for the $k$-th smallest there.
- If $k > q + s - 1$: recurse on the **right sub-array** $A[q+s \ldots n]$ looking for the $(k - q - s + 1)$-th smallest there.
- If $q \le k \le q + s - 1$: the pivot itself is the answer ŌĆö **terminate immediately**.

## 2.2 The Recurrence Relation

Let $T(n)$ denote the **expected number of comparisons** made by randomized selection on an input of size $n$. The recurrence is:

$$
T(n) \;\le\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{r=1}^{n} T\bigl(\max(r-1,\; n-r)\bigr) \;+\; O(n)
$$

The $O(n)$ term captures the cost of the partition scan; the sum enumerates all $n$ equally likely pivot ranks $r$, and we recurse only into the larger of the two partitions.

## 2.3 Closed-Form Bounds

Through substitution (shown exhaustively in Section 3), we obtain:

$$
T(n) \;\le\; 4n
$$

for $n \ge 1$, meaning the **expected number of comparisons is at most $4n$**. This is a clean linear bound.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the contents of this table for KTU board exams ŌĆö it covers ~70% of derivation questions.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Expected Time Recurrence | $T(n) \le \frac{1}{n}\sum_{r=1}^{n} T(\max(r-1, n-r)) + cn$ | $c$ is the partition constant |
| 2 | Upper Bound | $T(n) \le 4n$ | Holds for all $n \ge 1$ |
| 3 | General Form | $T(n) \le an$ for suitable constant $a$ | Proved by induction |
| 4 | Worst-Case Time | $O(n^2)$ | Pivot always an extreme element |
| 5 | Probability of Worst Case | $\le \frac{2}{n!}$ or $\le 2^{-n}$ | Depends on derivation path |
| 6 | Expected Comparisons | $\le 4n$ | Hoare's original bound |
| 7 | Expected Recursive Sub-problem Size | $\frac{3n}{4}$ | When pivot is in middle 50% |
| 8 | Space Complexity | $O(\log n)$ expected, $O(n)$ worst | Stack depth of recursion |
| 9 | In-Place | Yes | Uses $O(1)$ extra memory |
| 10 | Stable? | No | Partition reorders equal elements |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility ŌĆö Why This Matters in the Real World

- **Database Query Optimizers:** Finding the *k*-th largest salary, the *k*-th most recent transaction, or order-statistic-based quantile estimation.
- **Statistical Computing:** Computing **quantiles**, **percentiles**, **medians** ŌĆö central to robust statistics and boxplots.
- **Order-Statistic Trees & KD-Trees:** Geometric data structures rely on selection for balanced splits.
- **Streaming & Big Data:** Approximate quantiles via reservoir sampling + selection.
- **Signal Processing:** Median filters for image denoising use selection under the hood.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Expected Linear Bound $T(n) \le 4n$

We prove by **strong induction on $n$** that $T(n) \le 4n$ for all $n \ge 1$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Verify the Base Case
For $n = 1$: a single element is trivially the answer; $T(1) = 0 \le 4(1) = 4$. Ō£ö

For $n = 2$: at most one comparison is needed to find the smaller or larger; $T(2) \le 1 \le 4(2) = 8$. Ō£ö

### Step 2 ŌĆö State the Inductive Hypothesis
Assume that for every integer $m$ with $1 \le m < n$, we have $T(m) \le 4m$.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Set Up the Recurrence for $T(n)$

Let us denote by $r$ the rank (1-indexed position in sorted order) of the randomly chosen pivot. The probability mass function is uniform:

$$
\Pr[R = r] \;=\; \frac{1}{n}, \qquad \text{for } r = 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

After partitioning, the two sub-arrays have sizes $r - 1$ (left) and $n - r$ (right). We recurse into the **larger** of these two. Therefore the cost of the recursive call is:

$$
\text{Recursive cost} \;=\; T\bigl(\max(r-1,\; n-r)\bigr)
$$

The total work is partition cost + recursive cost. Taking expectations:

$$
T(n) \;=\; O(n) \;+\; \sum_{r=1}^{n} \Pr[R = r]\cdot T\bigl(\max(r-1,\; n-r)\bigr)
$$

$$
T(n) \;\le\; cn \;+\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{r=1}^{n} T\bigl(\max(r-1,\; n-r)\bigr)
$$

where $c$ is a positive constant absorbing the constant factor in the partition.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Split the Sum into Two Halves

Because of symmetry, $\max(r-1, n-r)$ is small when $r$ is near the middle and large when $r$ is near the ends. We split:

$$
\sum_{r=1}^{n} T\bigl(\max(r-1,\; n-r)\bigr) \;=\; \sum_{r=1}^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} T(n-r) \;+\; \sum_{r=\lceil n/2 \rceil + 1}^{n} T(r-1)
$$

By a change of variable, these two sums are identical (both enumerate $T(0), T(1), \ldots, T(\lfloor n/2 \rfloor)$). So:

$$
\sum_{r=1}^{n} T\bigl(\max(r-1,\; n-r)\bigr) \;\le\; 2\sum_{i=0}^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} T(i)
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the Inductive Hypothesis

Using $T(i) \le 4i$ for $i < n$:

$$
\sum_{i=0}^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} T(i) \;\le\; \sum_{i=0}^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} 4i \;=\; 4\cdot\frac{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor(\lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1)}{2}
$$

For large $n$, $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor \le n/2$, so this is bounded by:

$$
4\cdot\frac{(n/2)(n/2 + 1)}{2} \;=\; n^2/2 + n \;\le\; n^2
$$

(A loose bound sufficient for our purposes.) Thus:

$$
\sum_{r=1}^{n} T\bigl(\max(r-1,\; n-r)\bigr) \;\le\; 2n^2
$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Divide by $n$ and Conclude

$$
T(n) \;\le\; cn + \frac{1}{n}\cdot 2n^2 \;=\; cn + 2n
$$

For any $c \le 2$, we get $T(n) \le 4n$. So choosing $c = 2$ works (and we can absorb the constant into the $O(n)$ term).

$$
\boxed{\;T(n) \;\le\; 4n\;}
$$

### Step 7 ŌĆö Convert Back to Asymptotic Form

Since $T(n) \le 4n$ implies $T(n) = O(n)$, we conclude:

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbb{E}[T(n)] \;=\; O(n)\;}
$$

## 3.2 The Tail Bound (Markov's Inequality Application)

We can also bound the probability of running "slow". Using Markov's inequality with the fact that $T(n) \le \binom{n}{2}$ (the absolute worst case, every pair compared):

$$
\Pr\bigl[T(n) \ge t\cdot 4n\bigr] \;\le\; \frac{\mathbb{E}[T(n)]}{t\cdot 4n} \;\le\; \frac{4n}{4tn} \;=\; \frac{1}{t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The probability that randomized selection takes more than $4tn$ time shrinks inversely with $t$. For example, $\Pr[T(n) > 40n] \le 1/10$ and $\Pr[T(n) > 400n] \le 1/100$. This *concentration* around the mean is why randomized selection is so reliable in practice.

## 3.3 Worst-Case Analysis

The adversarial worst case happens when the random pivot happens to be the **minimum or maximum** of the current sub-array. The probability that a uniformly chosen pivot is the minimum is $1/n$. The probability this happens *every* recursion level for $n, n-1, \ldots, 1$ is:

$$
\Pr[\text{worst case}] \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{i} \;=\; \frac{1}{n!}
$$

This is super-exponentially small. In the worst case, the recurrence becomes $T(n) = T(n-1) + O(n)$, which solves to $T(n) = O(n^2)$.

## 3.4 Comparison With Deterministic Median-of-Medians

The deterministic linear-time selection (Blum-Floyd-Pratt-Rivest-Tarjan, 1973) achieves $O(n)$ worst-case by cleverly picking the pivot as the median of medians of $\lceil n/5 \rceil$ groups. It guarantees the pivot lies in the middle 30% ŌĆö but the constant factors are large (about $5n$ comparisons), so **randomized selection is faster in practice**, even though its worst case is worse.

| Metric | Randomized Quick Select | Median of Medians |
|---|---|---|
| Worst-case | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Expected | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Constant Factor | $\approx 4n$ | $\approx 5n$ |
| Randomness | Yes | No |
| Practical Speed | **Faster** | Slower |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Control Flow of Randomized Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START: input A of size n, target k]) --> checkA{k equals 1?}
    checkA -- YES --> returnMinA[Return minimum of A] --> stopA([END])
    checkA -- NO --> checkB{k equals n?}
    checkB -- YES --> returnMaxA[Return maximum of A] --> stopA
    checkB -- NO --> pickA[Pick index i uniformly at random from 1 to n]
    pickA --> pivotA[pivot = A of i]
    pivotA --> partitionA[Partition A in place around pivot]
    partitionA --> identifyA[Identify pivot rank q in partitioned array]
    identifyA --> branchA{k less than q?}
    branchA -- YES --> recurseLeftA[Recurse on LEFT subarray of size q minus 1 looking for k-th smallest] --> stopA
    branchA -- NO --> branchB{k greater than q plus s minus 1?}
    branchB -- YES --> recurseRightA[Recurse on RIGHT subarray looking for k minus q minus s plus 1 th smallest] --> stopA
    branchB -- NO --> returnPivotA[Return pivot as the answer] --> stopA
```

## 4.2 Partition Operation ŌĆö In-Place Rearrangement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE PARTITION"]
        b1["A 1 ... n"]
    end
    BEFORE --> pivotB[Choose random pivot index i]
    pivotB --> swapB[Swap A of i with A of n]
    swapB --> scanB[Initialize store index j = 0]
    scanB --> loopB{For i = 1 to n minus 1}
    loopB -- yes --> cmpB{A of i less than pivot}
    cmpB -- yes --> incB[Increment j then swap A of j with A of i]
    incB --> loopB
    cmpB -- no --> loopB
    loopB -- no --> finalSwapB[Swap A of j plus 1 with A of n]
    finalSwapB --> returnB[Return pivot final index = j plus 1]
```

## 4.3 Recurrence Tree Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n0["T(n) ŌĆö problem size n"] --> c0["Partition: O(n)"]
    n0 --> r0["Sub-problem of size m"]
    r0 --> c1["Partition: O(m)"]
    r0 --> r1["Sub-sub-problem of size m2"]
    r1 --> c2["Partition: O(m2)"]
    r1 --> stopTree(["base case reached"])
    c0 -.cost.-> levelA["Level 0 total: O(n)"]
    c1 -.cost.-> levelB["Level 1 total: O(m) Ōēż O(3n/4)"]
    c2 -.cost.-> levelC["Level 2 total: O(9n/16)"]
    levelA --> sumTree["Geometric series: n + 3n/4 + 9n/16 + ... Ōēż 4n"]
    levelB --> sumTree
    levelC --> sumTree
    sumTree --> finalTree(["EXPECTED TOTAL: O(n)"])
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT MODULE"]
        i1["Array A of size n"]
        i2["Integer k in 1 to n"]
    end
    subgraph RANDOMIZER["RANDOMIZER MODULE"]
        r1["Uniform RNG over 1 to n"]
        r2["Pivot Selection Logic"]
    end
    subgraph PARTITION["PARTITION MODULE"]
        p1["In-place Rearrangement Engine"]
        p2["Rank Position Calculator"]
        p3["Duplicate Cluster Detector"]
    end
    subgraph RECURSER["RECURSER MODULE"]
        rec1["Branch Decision Unit"]
        rec2["Sub-array Extractor"]
        rec3["Recursive Call Dispatcher"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT MODULE"]
        o1["Termination Checker"]
        o2["Result Returner"]
    end
    INPUT --> RANDOMIZER
    RANDOMIZER --> PARTITION
    PARTITION --> RECURSER
    RECURSER --> PARTITION
    RECURSER --> OUTPUT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]
**State the selection problem. Why is randomized selection preferred over sorting-based selection?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The selection problem asks for the $k$-th smallest element of an unordered set of $n$ elements, without fully sorting the array. **(1 mark)**
Sorting-based selection requires $O(n \log n)$ time since it sorts the entire array even when only one element is needed. **(1 mark)**
Randomized selection (Quick Select) finds the $k$-th smallest element in expected $O(n)$ time by partitioning around a random pivot and recursing only into the relevant sub-array, giving an exponential asymptotic improvement. **(1 mark)**

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]
**What is the worst-case time complexity of randomized selection? Under what input condition is it triggered?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The worst-case time complexity is $O(n^2)$. **(1 mark)**
It is triggered when the randomly chosen pivot is consistently the minimum or maximum of the current sub-array, leading to a degenerate recursive structure. **(1 mark)**
The probability of this happening across all recursion levels is $\frac{1}{n!}$, which is negligibly small ŌĆö hence the expected running time remains $O(n)$. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the Randomized Selection algorithm (Quick Select) with a clear pseudocode. Define the three cases that arise after partitioning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expected running time of randomized selection and show that it is $O(n)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Randomized Selection Algorithm:**

The algorithm proceeds as follows:

1. **Base cases:** If $n = 1$, return the only element. If $k = 1$, return the minimum; if $k = n$, return the maximum.

2. **Pivot Selection:** Choose an index $i$ uniformly at random from $\{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ and set `pivot = A[i]`.

3. **Partition:** Rearrange $A$ in-place so that all elements less than `pivot` come first, followed by all elements equal to `pivot`, followed by all elements greater than `pivot`. Let $q$ be the starting index of the pivot cluster, and $s$ be the size of that cluster (with $s \ge 1$).

4. **Three Cases After Partition:**

- **Case 1 ŌĆö Left Branch:** If $k < q$, the answer lies entirely in $A[1 \ldots q-1]$; recurse on this sub-array, still searching for the $k$-th smallest. **(2 marks)**

- **Case 2 ŌĆö Pivot Hit:** If $q \le k \le q + s - 1$, the pivot element itself is the $k$-th smallest; return `pivot` immediately. **(2 marks)**

- **Case 3 ŌĆö Right Branch:** If $k > q + s - 1$, the answer lies in $A[q+s \ldots n]$; recurse on this sub-array, but now searching for the $(k - q - s + 1)$-th smallest. **(2 marks)**

**Termination:** The recursion bottoms out when the sub-array has size 1, in which case that single element is the answer. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Derivation of Expected $O(n)$ Time:**

Let $T(n)$ denote the expected number of comparisons made by randomized selection on input of size $n$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Recurrence Setup:**
Let $R$ be the rank of the randomly chosen pivot. By uniformity, $\Pr[R = r] = 1/n$ for $r = 1, \ldots, n$. **(1 mark)**

The cost of partitioning is $O(n)$. After partitioning, the left sub-array has size $r - 1$ and the right has size $n - r$. We recurse into the larger one:

$$
T(n) \;=\; cn \;+\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{r=1}^{n} T\bigl(\max(r-1,\; n-r)\bigr) \quad \text{for } n \ge 2 \tag{1}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Induction Hypothesis:**
Claim: $T(n) \le 4n$ for all $n \ge 1$. Base cases $T(1) = 0 \le 4$ and $T(2) \le 1 \le 8$ hold. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 ŌĆö Symmetry Split:**
By symmetry, $\sum_{r=1}^{n} T(\max(r-1, n-r)) = 2\sum_{i=0}^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} T(i)$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply Hypothesis:**
Using $T(i) \le 4i$:

$$
\sum_{i=0}^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} T(i) \;\le\; 4\sum_{i=0}^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} i \;\le\; 4 \cdot \frac{(n/2)(n/2 + 1)}{2} \;\le\; n^2
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute Back:**
$$
T(n) \;\le\; cn + \frac{1}{n} \cdot 2n^2 \;=\; cn + 2n
$$

Choosing $c = 2$ yields $T(n) \le 4n$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 6 ŌĆö Conclude:**
$\mathbb{E}[T(n)] \le 4n = O(n)$. Hence randomized selection runs in expected linear time. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024 ŌĆö Alternative]

**(a)** Discuss the worst-case behavior of randomized selection. Compute the probability that the worst case occurs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare randomized selection with the deterministic median-of-medians algorithm in terms of time complexity, constants, and practicality. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Worst-Case Scenario:**
The worst case arises when the random pivot is consistently the **minimum** or **maximum** of the current sub-array at every recursive call. The recurrence becomes: **(2 marks)**

$$
T(n) \;=\; T(n - 1) + O(n)
$$

which solves to:

$$
T(n) \;=\; T(1) + \sum_{i=2}^{n} O(i) \;=\; O(n^2)
$$

**Probability Computation:**
At any recursion level with $m$ elements remaining, the probability that the random pivot is the minimum (or symmetrically the maximum) is $1/m$. The probability that *every* level produces an extreme pivot from $n$ down to $1$ is: **(3 marks)**

$$
\Pr[\text{worst case}] \;=\; \prod_{m=1}^{n} \frac{1}{m} \;=\; \frac{1}{n!}
$$

For $n = 10$: probability $\approx 2.76 \times 10^{-7}$. For $n = 20$: probability $\approx 4.1 \times 10^{-19}$. **(1 mark)**

**Key Insight:**
This super-exponentially small probability justifies the use of randomized selection in practice. Even though the worst case is $O(n^2)$, it is essentially never observed in random inputs. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Aspect | Randomized Selection | Median of Medians |
|---|---|---|
| **Worst-case time** | $O(n^2)$ | $\mathbf{O(n)}$ |
| **Expected time** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Constant factor** | $\approx 4n$ comparisons | $\approx 5n$ comparisons (worse) |
| **Random bits needed** | $O(n)$ | None (deterministic) |
| **Implementation simplicity** | Simple ŌĆö one partition routine | Complex ŌĆö 5-element medians, recursive median of medians |
| **Cache efficiency** | Excellent | Mediocre (irregular access pattern) |
| **Industry adoption** | **Widespread** (C++ `std::nth_element`, NumPy `partition`) | Mostly theoretical |

**(3 marks for table comparison)**

**Discussion:** While median-of-medians achieves the theoretical optimum of $O(n)$ worst-case, its constant factor and implementation complexity make it slower in practice than randomized selection. **(2 marks)**

Randomized selection dominates real-world systems because:
1. Its expected $O(n)$ is achieved with a small constant.
2. The worst case is statistically negligible.
3. Modern libraries (C++ STL `std::nth_element`) implement randomized variants (Introselect) that fall back to median-of-medians only on degenerate cases. **(2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** randomized selection with quick sort. They share the partition step, but quick sort recurses into **both** halves while selection recurses into **only one** half. This is the single most common mistake.
> 2. **Always state the three cases** (left branch, pivot hit, right branch) explicitly when describing the algorithm. Skipping any one costs at least 2 marks.
> 3. **Mention the base cases** ($n = 1$, $k = 1$, $k = n$) ŌĆö many students forget these.
> 4. **In the derivation,** remember to handle the $\max(r-1, n-r)$ correctly. Many students incorrectly write $T(r-1) + T(n-r)$, summing **both** halves ŌĆö but selection only recurses into **one** half.
> 5. **When asked about worst-case probability**, write $\frac{1}{n!}$ or $\le \frac{2}{n!}$, not $\frac{1}{2^n}$ unless the question specifically asks about the median-of-medians-style geometric bound.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ The **Selection Problem** asks for the $k$-th smallest element of an unordered set in sub-sorting time.
- Ō£ģ **Randomized Selection (Quick Select)** uses a *random* pivot and partitions in-place, recursing into only **one** sub-array.
- Ō£ģ The three post-partition cases are: **left branch** ($k < q$), **pivot hit** ($q \le k \le q+s-1$), **right branch** ($k > q+s-1$).
- Ō£ģ The recurrence is $T(n) = O(n) + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{r=1}^{n} T(\max(r-1, n-r))$.
- Ō£ģ The expected time is **$O(n)$**, proved by induction yielding $T(n) \le 4n$.
- Ō£ģ The **worst case** is $O(n^2)$, occurring with probability $\frac{1}{n!}$ ŌĆö essentially never.
- Ō£ģ The algorithm is **in-place** and uses $O(\log n)$ expected stack depth.
- Ō£ģ **Tail bound** via Markov: $\Pr[T(n) > 4tn] \le 1/t$.
- Ō£ģ **Comparison**: Randomized is faster in practice; Median-of-Medians is faster in worst case.
- Ō£ģ **Industry use**: C++ `std::nth_element`, Python `statistics.median`, NumPy `np.partition` all use randomized selection under the hood.
- Ō£ģ **Randomization here** is **Las Vegas** (always correct, randomized time) ŌĆö *not* Monte Carlo.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Randomized Data Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST639 - RANDOMIZED ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Randomized Data Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Randomized Data Structure** is a data structure whose internal organization ŌĆö including the layout of nodes, the height of subtrees, the position of keys, or the sequence of probed slots ŌĆö is governed not by a fixed deterministic rule but by **random choices made independently during insertions, deletions, and rebalancing operations**. Every operation accesses a sequence of random bits from a *true* or *pseudo-random* source, and the structure satisfies its operational invariants (search, insert, delete, predecessor, successor) with probability **1** (Las Vegas) or returns the correct answer with probability bounded away from 1/2 (Monte Carlo).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim flavour):** "A randomized data structure is one whose space occupancy, update time, or query time is a random variable; performance is evaluated in the **expected case**, not the worst case over the adversary's input order."

The principal members of this family, all mandated under **PECST639 Module 1**, are:

| # | Structure | Randomized Mechanism |
|---|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Skip List** (Pugh, 1989) | Random level of each new node chosen via coin-flips |
| 2 | **Treap** (Seidel & Aragon, 1996) | Random priority assigned to each inserted key |
| 3 | **Universal / Perfect Hashing** | Random choice of hash function from a universal family |
| 4 | **Randomized BST** | Random permutation dictates insertion order |
| 5 | **Randomized Meldable Heap** | Random parent at each merge node |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Office Tower" Picture of a Skip List

Imagine a **vertical office tower** with floor numbers $L_0, L_1, \dots, L_h$. Every employee (a key) is on the ground floor ($L_0$). When a new employee is hired, the HR manager repeatedly flips a fair coin:

- **Heads** ŌåÆ the employee gets an "express elevator pass" and is duplicated on the **next higher floor**.
- **Tails** ŌåÆ the employee stops where they are.

So a junior employee sits only on the ground floor; a senior partner might have duplicates on floors 1, 4, and 6. An express elevator lets you skip thousands of floors in seconds.

To **search** for a name, you start at the top-left corner, walk **right** while the next key is smaller, then **drop down** one floor when the next key is larger. Each drop-down is a coin-flip decision made earlier.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Intuition (Why does randomness help?)**  
> Deterministic balanced trees (AVL, Red-Black) require *rebalancing rotations* after every insertion/deletion ŌĆö a guaranteed logarithmic worst case but constant-factor expensive in practice. A randomized structure trades the worst-case guarantee for an **expected** logarithmic cost with **zero rebalancing work** ŌĆö coin flips do the balancing for free.

### 1.3 The Two Performance Paradigms

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Las Vegas vs. Monte Carlo (must memorize for KTU viva)**  

- **Las Vegas randomized structure** ŌĆö *always* correct; only the running time is random (e.g., **Skip List, Treap**).
- **Monte Carlo randomized structure** ŌĆö *fast* running time; output may occasionally be wrong with bounded probability (e.g., **Bloom Filter** ŌĆö not in PECST639 Module 1, but worth knowing for contrast).

### 1.4 Key Metrics for Module 1

- **Expected search time:** $E[T(n)] = O(\log n)$
- **Expected height:** $E[H(n)] = O(\log n)$
- **Expected space:** $O(n)$ total nodes (sum of geometric tail)
- **Variance:** $\text{Var}[H(n)] = O(1)$ ŌĆö concentration of measure
