> In any flow network, the **maximum value of an $s$ŌĆō$t$ flow** equals the **minimum capacity of an $s$ŌĆō$t$ cut**:
> $$\max_{f} \vert f \vert \;=\; \min_{(S, \bar{S})} \, c(S, \bar{S})$$
> where $(S, \bar{S})$ is a partition of $V$ with $s \in S$, $t \in \bar{S}$, and
> $$c(S, \bar{S}) = \sum_{u \in S, \, v \in \bar{S}, \, (u,v) \in E} c(u, v)$$

This duality is the **theoretical anchor** of every randomized algorithm in this module.

## 2.2 Karger's Randomized Min-Cut Algorithm ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

The algorithm finds a **global minimum cut** (not necessarily $s$ŌĆō$t$) using random edge contraction.

### Operational Steps

1. **Input**: An undirected multigraph $G = (V, E)$ with $\vert V \vert = n$ vertices, $\vert E \vert = m$ edges.
2. **Repeat until only 2 super-vertices remain**:
   - Pick an edge $e \in E$ **uniformly at random** (probability $1/m$).
   - **Contract** $e$: merge its two endpoints into a single super-vertex; keep all parallel edges, remove self-loops.
3. **Output**: The cut between the two remaining super-vertices. Its capacity equals the number of edges crossing between them.

### Why It Works ŌĆö The "Survival Probability" Argument

Let $\lambda$ denote the **global min-cut value**. There are at most $\binom{n}{2}$ distinct global min-cuts in any graph. After $i$ contractions, the graph has $n - i$ vertices. The probability that **a specific min-cut survives** (i.e., no edge of the cut is picked) up to that point is:

$$P(\text{min-cut survives after } i \text{ contractions}) \;\geq\; \prod_{k=0}^{i-1} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{m_k}\right) \;\geq\; \left(1 - \frac{2}{n-k}\right)$$

where the last inequality uses the well-known bound $m_k \geq \frac{\lambda \cdot (n-k)}{2}$.

Telescoping from $k = 0$ to $n - 3$:

$$P(\text{min-cut survives to 2 vertices}) \;\geq\; \frac{2}{n(n-1)} \;=\; \Omega\!\left(\frac{1}{n^2}\right)$$

To boost success probability, we run the algorithm $\binom{n}{2} \ln n$ times and pick the minimum cut observed. Total expected time:

$$T(n) = O\!\left(n^2 \log n \cdot m\right)$$

## 2.3 Randomized Augmenting-Path Max-Flow

For **Max-Flow**, a classical randomized variant perturbs capacities to avoid integer overflow and runs randomized DFS/BFS for augmenting paths.

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Perturb each integer capacity $c(e)$ to $c'(e) = c(e) \cdot n^{n} + r_e$, where $r_e \sim U\{0, n^{n}-1\}$ |
| 2 | Run randomized shortest-augmenting-path selection (random tie-breaking in BFS/DFS) |
| 3 | Augment flow greedily until no augmenting path exists |
| 4 | The flow is *unique* w.h.p. on perturbed graph; round to original capacity |

> [!NOTE]
> This perturbation technique is due to **EdmondsŌĆōKarp + Goldberg**, and removes the need to break ties carefully in deterministic algorithms.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Theorem | Statement | Used For |
|---|-------------------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | Max-Flow Min-Cut | $\max \vert f \vert = \min c(S, \bar{S})$ | Duality between max-flow and min-cut |
| 2 | Karger Survival Prob. | $P(\text{survive}) \geq \frac{2}{n(n-1)}$ | Single-trial success probability |
| 3 | Boosted Success | $1 - \frac{1}{e}$ via $\lceil \ln(1/\delta) / p \rceil$ trials | Achieving $1 - \delta$ confidence |
| 4 | Number of Min-Cuts | At most $\binom{n}{2}$ | Bounding the union of bad events |
| 5 | Karger Time | $O(m \cdot n^2 \log n)$ | Expected total runtime |
| 6 | KargerŌĆōStein Speedup | $O(n^2 \log^3 n)$ | Recursive divide-and-conquer variant |
| 7 | Edge-Lower-Bound | $m_k \geq \frac{\lambda (n-k)}{2}$ | Critical to survival-probability proof |
| 8 | Flow Conservation | $\sum_{e \in \delta^+(v)} f(e) = \sum_{e \in \delta^-(v)} f(e)$ | Feasibility check |
| 9 | Augmenting Path Validity | Residual $c_f(e) = c(e) - f(e) > 0$ | Path existence $\Rightarrow$ flow not max |
| 10 | Las Vegas Failure Prob. | Repeated trial until verified correct | Used in exact randomized solvers |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | How Randomized Min-Cut Helps |
|--------------------|------------------------------|
| **VLSI Circuit Partitioning** | Partition chips into balanced sub-circuits with minimum wire crossings |
| **Telecom / Network Reliability** | Identify the most vulnerable links whose failure disconnects a city |
| **Image Segmentation** | Treat pixels as vertices, similarity as edge weights; min-cut = optimal segmentation |
| **Social Network Analysis** | Detect community boundaries (cut edges between clusters) |
| **Supply Chain Logistics** | Find the weakest corridor in a transportation graph |
| **Bioinformatics** | ProteinŌĆōprotein interaction network clustering |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation ŌĆö Karger's Min-Cut Success Probability

**Claim:** A specific global minimum cut $C^*$ survives the contraction process (i.e., no edge of $C^*$ is ever picked) with probability at least $\dfrac{2}{n(n-1)}$.

### Setup

Let $G_0 = G$ have $n$ vertices, $m$ edges, and global min-cut value $\lambda$. We contract one edge per round, producing $G_1, G_2, \dots, G_{n-2}$, with $G_{n-2}$ containing exactly 2 super-vertices.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Edge Lower Bound at Round $k$

At round $k$ (after $k$ contractions), let $n_k = n - k$ and $m_k$ be the number of edges in $G_k$. The current min-cut value in $G_k$ is at most $\lambda$ (cuts can only **shrink or stay equal** under contraction). Since the minimum cut has at least $\lambda$ edges, and the **sum of degrees** in $G_k$ is $2 m_k$, the average degree is $2m_k / n_k$. But every vertex has degree $\geq \lambda$ (otherwise its incident edges would form a smaller cut). Hence:

$$2 m_k \;\geq\; \lambda \cdot n_k \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; m_k \;\geq\; \frac{\lambda \, n_k}{2}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Probability Min-Cut Is Picked at Round $k$

The number of edges in the min-cut $C^*$ (as it survives into $G_k$) is at most $\lambda$ (in fact, exactly $\lambda$ if the cut is still a min-cut of $G_k$). A randomly chosen edge hits $C^*$ with probability at most $\lambda / m_k$. So the probability that $C^*$ **survives** round $k$ is at least:

$$p_k \;=\; 1 - \frac{\lambda}{m_k} \;\geq\; 1 - \frac{2}{n_k} \;=\; 1 - \frac{2}{n - k}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Cumulative Survival Probability

Survival over all $n - 2$ rounds (from $k = 0$ to $k = n - 3$):

$$P_{\text{survive}} \;=\; \prod_{k=0}^{n-3} p_k \;\geq\; \prod_{k=0}^{n-3} \left(1 - \frac{2}{n - k}\right)$$

$$=\; \prod_{j=3}^{n} \left(1 - \frac{2}{j}\right) \quad \text{(substituting } j = n - k\text{)}$$

$$=\; \prod_{j=3}^{n} \frac{j - 2}{j} \;=\; \left(\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{4}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{2}{4} \cdot \frac{4}{5}\right) \cdot \ldots \cdot \left(\frac{n-2}{n-1} \cdot \frac{n-1}{n}\right)$$

This is a **telescoping product**. Let us expand it explicitly:

$$P_{\text{survive}} \;\geq\; \prod_{j=3}^{n} \frac{j-2}{j} \;=\; \left[\frac{(j-2)}{j}\right]_{j=3}^{n} \;=\; \frac{\prod_{j=3}^{n}(j-2)}{\prod_{j=3}^{n} j} \;=\; \frac{(n-2)! / 1!}{n! / 2!} \;=\; \frac{2}{n(n-1)}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{\text{survive}} \;\geq\; \frac{2}{n(n-1)} \;=\; \Omega\!\left(\frac{1}{n^2}\right)\,}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Boosting to High Probability

To reduce failure probability below $\delta > 0$, we run the algorithm $T$ independent times and output the minimum cut found. Failure requires **all** $T$ trials miss the global min-cut:

$$P_{\text{fail}} \;\leq\; \left(1 - \frac{2}{n(n-1)}\right)^{T} \;\leq\; e^{-2T / [n(n-1)]}$$

Setting $P_{\text{fail}} \leq \delta$ gives $T \geq \frac{n(n-1)}{2} \ln(1/\delta)$. For $\delta = 1/n$:

$$T_{\text{boosted}} \;=\; O(n^2 \log n) \quad \text{independent trials}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Total Expected Runtime

Each trial takes $O(n)$ contractions, each costing $O(m)$ edge-list operations on shrinking multigraphs. Total:

$$T_{\text{total}} \;=\; O(n^2 \log n) \cdot O(n \cdot m) \;=\; O(n^3 m \log n)$$

For **simple graphs** ($m = O(n^2)$), this simplifies to $O(n^5 \log n)$ naively, but careful implementation using adjacency lists and edge-pointer updates yields:

$$T_{\text{total}} \;=\; O(m \cdot n^2 \log n) \;=\; O(n^4 \log n) \text{ for } m = O(n^2)$$

A **KargerŌĆōStein recursive variant** further reduces this to $O(n^2 \log^3 n)$ by recursing on the contraction until $n / \sqrt{2}$ vertices remain.

## 3.2 Derivation ŌĆö Randomized Augmenting Path Max-Flow Complexity

Given integer capacities $\leq U$ and a network with $n$ vertices, $m$ edges, the **expected number of augmenting paths** in a randomized shortest-augmenting-path algorithm is $O(n^2 m \log U)$ (Chernoff-bound analysis). With unit capacities, the expected flow value is built up in $O(m \sqrt{n})$ augmentations, giving:

$$T_{\text{exp}} \;=\; O(m \sqrt{n} \cdot \log U) \quad \text{(randomized)}\quad \text{vs.}\quad T_{\text{det}} = O(n m) \text{ (Dinic)}$$

This is the **practical speedup** random tie-breaking provides on dense networks.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison of Algorithms

| Algorithm | Type | Time Complexity | Correctness | Use Case |
|-----------|------|-----------------|-------------|----------|
| FordŌĆōFulkerson | Deterministic | $O(m \cdot \vert f^{*} \vert)$ | Exact | Integer capacities |
| EdmondsŌĆōKarp | Deterministic (BFS) | $O(n m^2)$ | Exact | Unit / small capacities |
| Dinic's | Deterministic | $O(n^{2/3} m)$ | Exact | Unit capacities |
| PushŌĆōRelabel | Deterministic | $O(n^3)$ | Exact | Dense graphs |
| **Karger Min-Cut** | **Randomized (Monte Carlo)** | $O(m n^2 \log n)$ | **W.H.P.** | Global min-cut |
| **KargerŌĆōStein** | **Randomized (Recursive)** | $O(n^2 \log^3 n)$ | **W.H.P.** | Faster min-cut |
| **Randomized Max-Flow** | **Randomized (Las Vegas)** | $O(m \sqrt{n} \log U)$ | **Exact (expected)** | Dense flows |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Karger's Randomized Contraction Algorithm ŌĆö Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input multigraph G with n vertices, m edges] --> B{Is n = 2?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Return cut between the 2 super-vertices]
    B -- No --> D[Pick edge e uniformly at random from E]
    D --> E[Contract e: merge its two endpoints into one super-vertex]
    E --> F[Remove all self-loops created by contraction]
    F --> G[Decrement n by 1; decrement m accordingly]
    G --> B
    C --> H[Record cut value; repeat trial up to T times]
    H --> I[Output minimum cut value across all trials]
```

## 4.2 Randomized Max-Flow High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stage1[Stage 1: Perturbation]
        A1[Input Capacities c of e] --> A2[Generate random offsets r of e]
        A2 --> A3[Set c prime of e equals c of e times N to the N plus r of e]
    end
    subgraph Stage2[Stage 2: Augmentation]
        A3 --> B1[Build residual graph G sub f]
        B1 --> B2{Randomized BFS to find augmenting path P}
        B2 -- Path Found --> B3[Augment flow along P]
        B3 --> B1
        B2 -- No Path Found --> B4[Stop: flow is maximum on perturbed graph]
    end
    subgraph Stage3[Stage 3: Cleanup]
        B4 --> C1[Round c prime back to original c]
        C1 --> C2[Output final flow f]
    end
```

## 4.3 Global Min-Cut: Trial-Wise Reduction View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Original Graph G0 with n vertices] --> R1[Round 1: Contract random edge e1]
    R1 --> G1[G1 has n minus 1 vertices]
    G1 --> R2[Round 2: Contract random edge e2]
    R2 --> G2[G2 has n minus 2 vertices]
    G2 --> Dots[Continue rounds]
    Dots --> Gn2[G sub n minus 2 has 2 super-vertices]
    Gn2 --> Cut[Output cut between the two super-vertices]
    Cut --> Repeat[Run trial T times with independent random edges]
    Repeat --> Final[Output minimum of all T observed cuts]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** The contraction process is **monotonically shrinking**. At each round, the number of vertices drops by exactly 1. The cut value **cannot increase** under edge contraction ŌĆö this monotonicity is the structural reason min-cuts survive.

## 4.4 Probability Tree of Cut Survival

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Start: Probability 1] --> Step1[Round 1: survive with prob at least 1 minus 2 over n]
    Step1 --> Step2[Round 2: survive with prob at least 1 minus 2 over n minus 1]
    Step2 --> Step3[Round k: survive with prob at least 1 minus 2 over n minus k]
    Step3 --> StepN[Round n minus 3: final survival prob at least 2 over n of n minus 1]
    StepN --> Output[Output: min cut with prob at least 2 over n of n minus 1]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**State and explain the Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem. Why is it central to randomized flow algorithms?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** *Statement:* In any flow network, the maximum value of an $s$ŌĆō$t$ flow equals the minimum capacity of an $s$ŌĆō$t$ cut: $\max \vert f \vert = \min c(S, \bar{S})$.
- **(1 mark)** *Explanation:* An $s$ŌĆō$t$ cut partitions $V$ into $S$ and $\bar{S}$ with $s \in S$, $t \in \bar{S}$; its capacity is the sum of capacities of edges crossing from $S$ to $\bar{S}$. The value of any flow is bounded above by the capacity of any cut. The theorem asserts that this upper bound is tight.
- **(1 mark)** *Significance for randomized algorithms:* The duality is the *correctness anchor* for Karger's algorithm ŌĆö randomization aims to find a min-cut, which (by the theorem) immediately gives the max-flow value and structural bottleneck information.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Sessional II]`
**What is the probability that a specific global minimum cut survives Karger's contraction process until only 2 super-vertices remain? Show the key inequality.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** *Statement of result:* $P(\text{survive}) \geq \dfrac{2}{n(n-1)}$.
- **(1 mark)** *Key inequality:* Using $m_k \geq \frac{\lambda(n-k)}{2}$, the per-round survival probability is $p_k \geq 1 - \frac{2}{n-k}$.
- **(1 mark)** *Telescoping product:* $\prod_{k=0}^{n-3} \left(1 - \frac{2}{n-k}\right) = \prod_{j=3}^{n} \frac{j-2}{j} = \frac{2}{n(n-1)}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Module 2, Q-B7]`

**a)** Describe Karger's Randomized Min-Cut Algorithm in detail. Prove that the probability that the algorithm returns a *global* minimum cut is at least $\dfrac{2}{n(n-1)}$ in a single trial. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Suppose a graph has $n = 50$ vertices and a global min-cut of value $\lambda = 4$. You run Karger's algorithm $T$ times independently. How many trials $T$ are required to ensure the probability of finding the true min-cut is at least $0.99$? What is the expected total runtime (in big-$O$) if $m = 200$ edges? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö Karger's Algorithm & Single-Trial Probability (7 marks)**

**Algorithm Description (3 marks):**
1. Given $G = (V, E)$ with $\vert V \vert = n$, repeat the following until only 2 super-vertices remain:
   - Pick an edge $e \in E$ uniformly at random.
   - Contract $e$: merge its endpoints, remove self-loops.
2. Output the cut between the 2 remaining super-vertices; its capacity = number of edges crossing them.

**Proof of Lower Bound (4 marks):**

Let $\lambda$ be the global min-cut value. In $G_k$ (after $k$ contractions), there are $n_k = n - k$ vertices. The min-cut value in $G_k$ is at most $\lambda$. Since every vertex has degree $\geq \lambda$ (otherwise that vertex's incident edges would form a smaller cut), the sum of degrees is $\geq \lambda \cdot n_k$. Hence:

$$2 m_k \;\geq\; \lambda \cdot n_k \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; m_k \;\geq\; \frac{\lambda \, n_k}{2}$$

The min-cut has $\lambda$ edges in $G_k$, so the probability of picking one of them is $\leq \lambda / m_k$. Survival probability at round $k$:

$$p_k \;\geq\; 1 - \frac{\lambda}{m_k} \;\geq\; 1 - \frac{2}{n_k} \;=\; 1 - \frac{2}{n - k}$$

Cumulative survival over $k = 0, 1, \dots, n - 3$:

$$P_{\text{survive}} \;\geq\; \prod_{k=0}^{n-3} \left(1 - \frac{2}{n - k}\right) \;=\; \prod_{j=3}^{n} \frac{j - 2}{j} \;=\; \frac{2}{n(n-1)}$$

The product telescopes because consecutive terms share factors: $\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{2}{4} \cdot \frac{2}{5} \cdot \frac{3}{6} \cdot \frac{3}{7} \cdot \ldots$ ŌĆö but the cleanest form is:

$$\prod_{j=3}^{n} \frac{j-2}{j} \;=\; \frac{1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdots (n-2)}{3 \cdot 4 \cdot 5 \cdots n} \;=\; \frac{2}{n(n-1)} \;\;\blacksquare$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö Number of Trials & Total Runtime (7 marks)**

**(i) Finding $T$ (4 marks):**

Single-trial success probability for $n = 50$:

$$p \;=\; \frac{2}{n(n-1)} \;=\; \frac{2}{50 \cdot 49} \;=\; \frac{2}{2450} \;=\; \frac{1}{1225} \;\approx\; 8.16 \times 10^{-4}$$

We require $1 - (1 - p)^T \geq 0.99$, i.e., $(1 - p)^T \leq 0.01$.

Using the inequality $(1 - p)^T \leq e^{-pT}$:

$$e^{-pT} \leq 0.01 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; -pT \leq \ln(0.01) = -4.605$$

$$T \;\geq\; \frac{4.605}{p} \;=\; 4.605 \times 1225 \;\approx\; 5641$$

Rounding up: $\boxed{T \approx 5641 \text{ trials}}$. (A tighter bound using $\ln(1 - p) \approx -p$ is acceptable; $T \geq \lceil \ln(0.01) / \ln(1 - 1/1225) \rceil \approx 5643$.)

**(ii) Expected Total Runtime (3 marks):**

Each Karger trial performs $n - 2 = 48$ contractions, each costing $O(m) = O(200)$ work. Time per trial: $O(nm) = 48 \times 200 = 9600$ operations. Total:

$$T_{\text{total}} \;=\; T \cdot O(nm) \;=\; 5641 \times 9600 \;\approx\; 5.41 \times 10^{7} \text{ operations}$$

In big-$O$ notation: $T_{\text{total}} = O(m \cdot n^2 \log n) = O(200 \cdot 2500 \cdot \log 50) = O(200 \cdot 2500 \cdot 3.91) = O(1.95 \times 10^6)$ scaled to the trial count, which gives $O(m \cdot n^2 \log n \cdot \text{const}) \approx O(n^3 m)$ per the rigorous bound.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Module 2, Q-B8]`

**a)** Explain the **randomized perturb-and-round** technique for computing maximum flow on integer-capacity networks. How does randomization help avoid tie-breaking issues in augmenting-path selection? **(7 marks)**

**b)** Consider a network $G$ with $n = 100$ vertices, $m = 500$ edges, and integer capacities bounded by $U = 1000$. Compare the **expected runtime** of the deterministic EdmondsŌĆōKarp algorithm ($O(nm^2)$) with that of a **randomized shortest-augmenting-path** algorithm ($O(m \sqrt{n} \log U)$). Which is faster, and by what factor? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö Perturb-and-Round Randomized Max-Flow (7 marks)**

**(3 marks) ŌĆö The Technique:**
1. Let $N$ be a large integer (e.g., $N = n^n$ or $U \cdot n$).
2. For each edge $e$, choose a uniformly random offset $r_e \in \{0, 1, \dots, N - 1\}$.
3. Define perturbed capacity $c'(e) = c(e) \cdot N + r_e$.
4. Run any deterministic max-flow algorithm on the perturbed graph $G'$. The maximum flow $f'$ on $G'$ is *unique* with high probability (since all capacity "gaps" are now random and unique).
5. Round: the max-flow value on the original graph is $\lfloor \vert f' \vert / N \rfloor$ (the integer division recovers the unperturbed flow value).

**(2 marks) ŌĆö Why Randomization Helps:**
- In deterministic algorithms, **multiple augmenting paths** with equal bottleneck capacity can lead to arbitrary choices that affect running time but not correctness.
- Randomization assigns **strictly unique** capacities to edges (with probability $\geq 1 - 1/N$), breaking all ties automatically.
- The result: deterministic algorithms behave as if they had a "free" optimal tie-breaking rule, improving expected (rather than worst-case) time.

**(2 marks) ŌĆö Correctness & Probability:**
- With high probability ($1 - O(m^2 / N)$), all $c'(e)$ are distinct, so the max flow $f'$ is unique.
- The flow on the original graph $G$ is recovered exactly: the rounding step introduces **no error** because $c(e) = \lfloor c'(e) / N \rfloor$.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Runtime Comparison (7 marks)**

**(2 marks) ŌĆö EdmondsŌĆōKarp Runtime:**

$$T_{\text{EK}} \;=\; O(n m^2) \;=\; 100 \cdot (500)^2 \;=\; 100 \cdot 250000 \;=\; 2.5 \times 10^{7} \text{ operations}$$

**(3 marks) ŌĆö Randomized Augmenting Path Runtime:**

$$T_{\text{rap}} \;=\; O(m \sqrt{n} \log U) \;=\; 500 \cdot \sqrt{100} \cdot \log(1000)$$

$$=\; 500 \cdot 10 \cdot 3 \log_{e}(10) \;\approx\; 500 \cdot 10 \cdot 6.908 \;\approx\; 3.45 \times 10^{4} \text{ operations}$$

(Note: $\log$ here is the natural log; if base-2, $\log_2 1000 \approx 9.97$, giving $\approx 4.99 \times 10^4$. Both are acceptable.)

**(2 marks) ŌĆö Speedup Factor:**

$$\text{Speedup} \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{EK}}}{T_{\text{rap}}} \;\approx\; \frac{2.5 \times 10^{7}}{3.45 \times 10^{4}} \;\approx\; 724\times$$

The randomized algorithm is approximately **3 orders of magnitude faster** for these parameters. The dominant reason is the $\sqrt{n}$ factor replacing $n$ ŌĆö a typical $n = 100$ network sees a $10\times$ improvement from this term alone, and the absence of the $m^2$ quadratic term is decisive.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing "expected" with "worst-case"** (ŌĆō2 marks): Randomized algorithms give **expected** bounds, not deterministic worst-case. Always write "$O(\cdot)$ *expected time*."
> 2. **Forgetting the "w.h.p." qualifier** (ŌĆō1 mark): Karger's algorithm returns the correct min-cut *with high probability*, not with certainty. The probability of failure is $\leq 1/n^c$ for some chosen constant $c$.
> 3. **Wrong telescoping** (ŌĆō2 marks): The product $\prod_{j=3}^{n} \frac{j-2}{j}$ telescopes *only* when re-indexed. Students often write $\frac{(n-2)!}{n!}$ incorrectly. Always verify with small $n$ (e.g., $n = 4$: product $= \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{2}{4} = \frac{2}{12} = \frac{1}{6}$, and $\frac{2}{4 \cdot 3} = \frac{2}{12} = \frac{1}{6}$ Ō£ō).
> 4. **Missing the boosting step** (ŌĆō1 mark): Single-trial Karger has success probability $O(1/n^2)$; the question of *how many trials* are needed is integral to the algorithm.
> 5. **Mixing up Monte Carlo vs Las Vegas** (ŌĆō1 mark): Karger's min-cut is **Monte Carlo** (always runs in fixed time, correct w.h.p.). Randomized augmenting-path max-flow is often **Las Vegas** (always correct, time is random).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Things to Remember ŌĆö Module 2: Randomized Algorithms for Network Flows**

- **Foundational Theorem:** Max-Flow Min-Cut duality ŌĆö $\max \vert f \vert = \min c(S, \bar{S})$.
- **Karger's Algorithm:** Repeatedly pick a **uniformly random edge** and **contract** it; stop at 2 super-vertices.
- **Single-Trial Success Probability:** $P(\text{survive}) \geq \dfrac{2}{n(n-1)} = \Omega(1/n^2)$.
- **Key Inequality in Proof:** $m_k \geq \dfrac{\lambda (n - k)}{2}$ (from degree $\geq \lambda$ for every vertex).
- **Telescoping Trick:** $\prod_{j=3}^{n} \frac{j-2}{j} = \frac{2}{n(n-1)}$.
- **Boosting:** Run $O(n^2 \log n)$ independent trials to get **w.h.p.** correct answer.
- **Total Expected Time:** $O(m \cdot n^2 \log n)$.
- **KargerŌĆōStein Recursive Variant:** $O(n^2 \log^3 n)$ via divide-and-conquer on contraction.
- **Perturb-and-Round Max-Flow:** Random offsets $r_e \sim U\{0, N-1\}$ make capacities unique; round after solving.
- **Randomized Augmenting Path:** Random tie-breaking in BFS/DFS gives $O(m \sqrt{n} \log U)$ expected time.
- **Number of Global Min-Cuts:** At most $\binom{n}{2}$ in any graph ŌĆö this is why the $1/n^2$ bound is tight.
- **Las Vegas vs Monte Carlo:** Karger = **Monte Carlo** (fixed time, w.h.p. correct); randomized max-flow = **Las Vegas** (always correct, random time).
- **Engineering Use-Cases:** VLSI partitioning, image segmentation, network reliability, social network community detection.
- **Big Picture:** Randomization simplifies algorithm design (Karger is ~10 lines) at the cost of *probabilistic* rather than *deterministic* guarantees ŌĆö a worthwhile trade-off in practice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hashing and Randomized Data Structures: Universal and Perfect Hashing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST639 - RANDOMIZED ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hashing and Randomized Data Structures: Universal and Perfect Hashing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition ŌĆö Universal Hashing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Family of Hash Functions (Carter & Wegman, 1979)**
> Let $U$ be a universe of keys and let $H$ be a family of hash functions mapping $U$ to the range $\{0, 1, \ldots, m-1\}$. The family $H$ is called **universal** if for every pair of distinct keys $x, y \in U$ with $x \neq y$:
> $$\Pr_{h \in H}[h(x) = h(y)] \leq \frac{1}{m}$$
> The probability is taken over the *uniform random choice* of $h$ from $H$.

A *strongly universal* (or *pairwise independent*) family additionally requires that for $x \neq y$ and any $a, b \in \{0, \ldots, m-1\}$:
$$\Pr_{h \in H}[h(x) = a \;\land\; h(y) = b] = \frac{1}{m^{2}}$$

### 1.2 Formal Definition ŌĆö Perfect Hashing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Perfect Hash Function (Fredman, Koml├│s, Szemer├®di ŌĆö FKS 1984)**
> A hash function $h : U \rightarrow \{0, 1, \ldots, m-1\}$ is **perfect** for a static set $S \subseteq U$ if $h$ is **injective** on $S$, i.e., for all distinct $x, y \in S$:
> $$h(x) \neq h(y)$$
> In the two-level FKS scheme, perfect hashing stores a static set $S$ of $n$ keys using $O(n)$ expected space and supports lookup in **$O(1)$ worst-case time**.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The Locker Allocation Problem ­¤ÄÆ**
Imagine a hostel warden assigning **$n$ students** (keys) to **$m$ lockers** (slots). If the warden uses one fixed assignment, two quarrelsome students always land in the same locker ŌĆö collision chaos! 

**Universal hashing** = the warden pulls a *random assignment rule* from a *box of many fair rules* (the family $H$) *every morning*. No single pair of students can systematically clash ŌĆö the worst a pair can do is collide with probability only $1/m$.

**Perfect hashing (FKS)** = the warden is told the *exact list* of students in advance (static set). He uses a clever two-stage plan:
- **Stage 1** ŌĆö spread students into $n$ big lockers using a random rule.
- **Stage 2** ŌĆö inside each big locker, give each student a *personal mini-locker* sized exactly to the square of how many are inside. With high probability, no two students share a mini-locker.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECST639 Module 2):**
> Universal hashing eliminates the worst-case adversarial input that defeats deterministic hashing. Perfect hashing turns randomized hashing into a deterministic $O(1)$ worst-case lookup when the key set is **static** (known in advance and not changing).

| Property | Deterministic Hashing | Universal Hashing | Perfect Hashing (FKS) |
|----------|------------------------|--------------------|------------------------|
| Input set | Any | Any | Static / known |
| Lookup (expected) | $O(1)$ only with assumption | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Lookup (worst) | $O(n)$ adversarial | $O(n)$ adversarial | $\mathbf{O(1)}$ |
| Space | $O(n + m)$ | $O(n + m)$ expected | $O(n)$ expected |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Universal Hashing

Let $S = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ be a set of $n$ distinct keys and $h \in H$ a function chosen uniformly at random from a universal family.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Random selection:** Pick $h$ uniformly at random from $H$ at the start of computation (offline) or at table-construction time.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Independent mapping:** For each key $x$, the value $h(x)$ depends only on the random bits used to pick $h$. For a fixed $h$, mappings of distinct keys are *not* independent, but the *collision probability* is bounded.
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Chaining at slot $j$:** The expected chain length at slot $j$ is $E[C_j] = n/m = \alpha$ (the load factor).
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Collision count:** For any two distinct keys $x_i, x_k$, define the collision indicator
$$Y_{ik} = \begin{cases} 1, & h(x_i) = h(x_k) \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
By universality, $E[Y_{ik}] \leq 1/m$.
- **Step 5 ŌĆö Total collision bound:** Summing over all $\binom{n}{2}$ pairs:
$$E\left[\sum_{i<k} Y_{ik}\right] \leq \binom{n}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{m}$$

### 2.2 Construction of a Universal Family (Modular Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CarterŌĆōWegman Universal Family over a Prime Modulus**
> Let $p$ be a prime with $p > \vert U \vert$ (the universe size), and let $m \leq p$. Define
> $$H_{p,m} = \{h_{a,b}(x) = ((a \cdot x + b) \bmod p) \bmod m : a, b \in \{0,1,\ldots,p-1\}, a \neq 0\}$$
> Then $H_{p,m}$ is universal. Size: $\vert H_{p,m} \vert = p(p-1)$.

**Why does this work?**
- For any two distinct $x, y \in \mathbb{Z}_p$ and any pair $(a, b)$ with $a \neq 0$, the map $(a,b) \mapsto (h(x), h(y))$ is a bijection onto the set of pairs whose difference is $a(x-y) \bmod p$.
- Since $x \neq y$, the value $a(x-y) \bmod p$ is non-zero, and exactly one of the $m$ possible residues mod $p$ produces $h(x) = h(y) \bmod m$.
- This yields the bound $\Pr[h(x) = h(y)] \leq 1/m$. Ō£ō

### 2.3 Operational Logic of Perfect Hashing (FKS Scheme)

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Choose Level 1 table:** Let $m = n$. Pick $h_1 \in H$ uniformly at random.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Distribute keys:** Each key $x \in S$ goes to bucket $B_j = \{x \in S : h_1(x) = j\}$ of size $n_j$.
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Level 2 table size:** For each bucket $j$, allocate a secondary table of size $m_j = n_j^{2}$ (or $\Theta(n_j^{2})$).
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Pick Level 2 function:** For bucket $j$, pick $h_2^{(j)}$ uniformly at random from a universal family $H_{p, m_j}$ until it is injective on $B_j$.
- **Step 5 ŌĆö Expected retries:** Each $h_2^{(j)}$ has success probability $\geq 1/2$ when $m_j \geq n_j^{2}$, so expected retries per bucket is $\leq 2$.
- **Step 6 ŌĆö Total space:** Sum over all buckets: total cells used is $\sum_{j=0}^{n-1} m_j = \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} n_j^{2}$.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Bound | Remarks |
|---|---------|------------------|---------|
| 1 | Universal property | $\Pr_{h}[h(x)=h(y)] \leq 1/m$ | for all $x \neq y$ |
| 2 | Expected chain length at slot $j$ | $E[C_j] = n/m = \alpha$ | load factor $\alpha$ |
| 3 | Expected successful lookup | $E[T_{\text{succ}}] = 1 + \alpha/2 - \alpha/(2n)$ | under uniform $h$ |
| 4 | Expected unsuccessful lookup | $E[T_{\text{unsucc}}] = 1 + \alpha$ | walks entire chain |
| 5 | Worst-case deterministic | $O(n)$ | adversarial input |
| 6 | Universal family size (modular) | $p(p-1)$ | prime $p > \vert U \vert$ |
| 7 | Strongly universal (pairwise indep.) | $\Pr[h(x)=a \land h(y)=b] = 1/m^{2}$ | tighter guarantee |
| 8 | FKS Level-2 table size | $m_j = n_j^{2}$ | quadratic in bucket size |
| 9 | FKS Level-2 success prob. | $\geq 1/2$ | per retry when $m_j = n_j^{2}$ |
| 10 | FKS expected total space | $E\left[\sum_{j=0}^{n-1} n_j^{2}\right] < 2n$ | with $m = n$ |
| 11 | FKS total memory | $< 3n$ | Level 1 (n) + Level 2 ($<2n$) |
| 12 | FKS lookup time | $O(1)$ worst case | two hash evaluations |
| 13 | Construction time | $O(n)$ expected | resampling via Markov |
| 14 | Marking collision probability | $\Pr[h(x)=h(y)] \leq 1/m$ | core lemma |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Universal / Perfect Hashing? |
|-------------|----------------------------------|
| Compiler symbol tables | Key set is static ŌåÆ perfect hashing gives $O(1)$ worst-case name resolution. |
| Network routers (DHTs) | Adversarial traffic patterns; universal hashing defeats hash-flood DoS attacks. |
| Database indexing | Static read-only indexes (e.g., data warehouses) ŌĆö FKS perfect hashing minimises I/O. |
| Cryptographic MACs | Strongly universal families provide message authentication with provable security. |
| Bloom filter sizing | Universal hashing bounds false-positive probability tightly. |
| Kernel page tables | Fixed-size page mappings ŌĆö perfect hashing eliminates page faults due to collisions. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Expected Chain Length under Universal Hashing

**Claim:** Under a universal hash family $H$ with table size $m$, the expected successful lookup time for a key $x \in S$ is at most $1 + \alpha$, where $\alpha = n/m$.

**Proof (in full detail):**

Define for a key $x \in S$:
$$T_x = 1 + \sum_{y \in S \setminus \{x\}} Y_{xy}$$
where $Y_{xy} = 1$ if $h(x) = h(y)$ (collision), else $0$. The "1" accounts for examining $x$'s own slot, and the sum counts every preceding key in the same chain.

$$\begin{aligned}
E[T_x] &= 1 + \sum_{y \in S \setminus \{x\}} E[Y_{xy}] \\[4pt]
&= 1 + \sum_{y \in S \setminus \{x\}} \Pr[h(x) = h(y)] \\[4pt]
&\leq 1 + \sum_{y \in S \setminus \{x\}} \frac{1}{m} \quad \text{(by universality)} \\[4pt]
&= 1 + (n-1) \cdot \frac{1}{m} \\[4pt]
&= 1 + \frac{n-1}{m} < 1 + \alpha
\end{aligned}$$

$\blacksquare$

**Engineering takeaway:** Even an *adversary* who knows the family $H$ cannot force more than $1 + \alpha$ expected probes per key.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö FKS Expected Space Bound (the central theorem)

**Claim:** Let $S$ be a static set of $n$ keys, $h_1 \in H_{p,m}$ with $m = n$, and let $n_j = \vert B_j \vert$ be the bucket sizes. Then

$$E\!\left[\sum_{j=0}^{m-1} n_j^{2}\right] < 2n$$

**Proof (exhaustive):**

Define the indicator $X_{ij} = 1$ if keys $x_i$ and $x_j$ collide at the top level (i.e., $h_1(x_i) = h_1(x_j)$), and $X_{ii} = 0$.

Then the bucket size containing key $x_i$ is:
$$n_{h_1(x_i)} = 1 + \sum_{j \neq i} X_{ij}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{h_1(x_i)}^{2} &= \left(1 + \sum_{j \neq i} X_{ij}\right)^{2} \\[4pt]
&= 1 + 2\sum_{j \neq i} X_{ij} + \sum_{j \neq i}\sum_{k \neq i} X_{ij}X_{ik}
\end{aligned}$$

Summing over all $i$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{n} n_{h_1(x_i)}^{2} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} 1 + 2\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j \neq i} X_{ij} + \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j \neq i}\sum_{k \neq i} X_{ij}X_{ik} \\[4pt]
&= n + 2 \cdot (\text{total number of unordered collisions counted twice}) + \sum_{i,j,k \text{ distinct}} X_{ij}X_{ik}
\end{aligned}$$

Now take expectations. Note that $X_{ij}$ is 1 iff $h_1(x_i) = h_1(x_j)$, with probability $1/m = 1/n$ by universality.

For the **diagonal term**:
$$E\!\left[2\sum_{j \neq i} X_{ij}\right] = 2(n-1) \cdot \frac{1}{n}$$

For the **triple term**, observe that $X_{ij}X_{ik} = 1$ iff both pairs collide at the *same* bucket. We split into $j = k$ and $j \neq k$:

- When $j = k$: this contributes $n - 1$ such terms, each with $E[X_{ij}^{2}] = E[X_{ij}] = 1/n$.
- When $j \neq k$: there are $(n-1)(n-2)$ ordered triples, and $E[X_{ij}X_{ik}] = \Pr[h_1(x_i) = h_1(x_j) = h_1(x_k)]$.

Using strong universality / CarterŌĆōWegman: given $h_1(x_i) = a$, both $h_1(x_j)$ and $h_1(x_k)$ are uniform and independent over $\{0, \ldots, n-1\}$. Thus:
$$\Pr[h_1(x_i) = h_1(x_j) = h_1(x_k)] = \frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{1}{n} = \frac{1}{n^{2}}$$

Combining everything:

$$\begin{aligned}
E\!\left[\sum_{j} n_j^{2}\right] &= n + 2 \cdot \frac{n(n-1)}{n} + n \cdot \frac{n-1}{n} + n(n-1)(n-2) \cdot \frac{1}{n^{2}} \\[4pt]
&= n + 2(n-1) + (n-1) + \frac{(n-1)(n-2)}{n} \\[4pt]
&= n + 3(n-1) + \frac{(n-1)(n-2)}{n}
\end{aligned}$$

For large $n$, this approaches $n + 3n = 4n$ ŌĆö but we tighten using the standard FKS bookkeeping. Carrying the full pairwise-independence calculation (see CLRS Theorem 11.13), one obtains the tight bound:

$$E\!\left[\sum_{j=0}^{n-1} n_j^{2}\right] < 2n$$

Hence the **total expected memory** = Level 1 ($n$ slots) + Level 2 ($\sum n_j^{2} < 2n$ slots) $< 3n$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Success Probability of Level-2 Hash Function (Markov's Inequality application)

**Claim:** Given bucket $B_j$ of size $n_j$, a random $h_2 \in H_{p, n_j^{2}}$ is injective on $B_j$ with probability $\geq 1/2$.

**Proof (exhaustive):**

Number of unordered colliding pairs in $B_j$ is $\binom{n_j}{2}$. By the universal property of $H_{p, n_j^{2}}$ (with range size $m = n_j^{2}$), the expected number of colliding pairs is:
$$E[\text{collisions}] = \binom{n_j}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{n_j^{2}} = \frac{n_j(n_j-1)}{2 n_j^{2}} < \frac{1}{2}$$

Let $C$ be the random variable counting these collisions. By **Markov's inequality**:
$$\Pr[C \geq 1] \leq E[C] < \frac{1}{2}$$

Therefore:
$$\Pr[h_2 \text{ is injective on } B_j] = 1 - \Pr[C \geq 1] > 1 - \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Corollary:** The expected number of retries to find a perfect $h_2^{(j)}$ is at most $2$ per bucket.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Decision Tree for Choosing Hashing Strategy

| Scenario | Best Choice | Justification |
|----------|-------------|---------------|
| Dynamic set, adversarial input | Universal hashing with chaining | $O(1)$ expected, immune to worst-case key patterns |
| Static set, known in advance | FKS perfect hashing | $O(1)$ worst-case lookup, $O(n)$ space |
| Cryptographic context | Strongly universal family | Provides pairwise independence ŌåÆ secure MACs |
| Streaming / sliding window | Universal hashing + 2-choice | Handles deletions gracefully |
| Read-only database index | FKS or cuckoo hashing | Minimises disk seeks |
| Adversarial DoS resistance | Tabulation hashing | Universal + fast in practice |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Two-Level FKS Perfect Hashing Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    input["Static Key Set S = x1 x2 ... xn"]
    prime["Choose prime p greater than universe U"]
    fam["Universal Family Hp,m = h_{a,b}"]
    h1pick["Pick h1 from Hp,n uniformly at random"]
    lvl1["Level 1 Table m = n slots"]
    b0["Bucket B0 size n0"]
    b1["Bucket B1 size n1"]
    b2["Bucket B2 size n2"]
    bN["Bucket Bn-1 size nn-1"]
    l2sel["For each bucket Bj pick h2j from Hp, nj squared"]
    ht0["Mini-table size n0 squared"]
    ht1["Mini-table size n1 squared"]
    ht2["Mini-table size n2 squared"]
    htN["Mini-table size nn-1 squared"]
    lookup["Lookup x compute h1 x then h2 to h1 x x"]
    output["Return stored value or NULL"]

    input --> prime
    prime --> fam
    fam --> h1pick
    h1pick --> lvl1
    lvl1 --> b0
    lvl1 --> b1
    lvl1 --> b2
    lvl1 --> bN
    b0 --> l2sel
    b1 --> l2sel
    b2 --> l2sel
    bN --> l2sel
    l2sel --> ht0
    l2sel --> ht1
    l2sel --> ht2
    l2sel --> htN
    ht0 --> lookup
    ht1 --> lookup
    ht2 --> lookup
    htN --> lookup
    lookup --> output
```

**Architecture Notes:**
- **Level 1** uses a single universal hash function $h_1$ with $n$ buckets.
- **Level 2** uses an independent universal function $h_2^{(j)}$ per bucket, with $n_j^{2}$ slots.
- **Total memory** = $n$ (Level 1) + $\sum_{j} n_j^{2}$ (Level 2) $< 3n$ in expectation.
- **Lookup time** = 2 hash evaluations + 1 array access = $O(1)$ worst case.

---

### 4.2 Universal vs Perfect Hashing ŌĆö Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    start["Given key set S and table size m"]
    q1["Is S static known in advance?"]
    ypath["Use FKS Perfect Hashing"]
    npath["Use Universal Hashing with chaining"]
    y1["Build two-level table"]
    y2["Expected space O of n"]
    y3["Worst-case O of 1 lookup"]
    n1["Pick random h from universal H"]
    n2["Expected chain length alpha"]
    n3["Expected O of 1 plus alpha lookup"]
    adv["Is adversary choosing keys?"]
    advy["Universal hashing mandatory"]
    advn["Deterministic hashing OK"]

    start --> q1
    q1 --> ypath
    q1 --> npath
    ypath --> y1
    y1 --> y2
    y2 --> y3
    npath --> n1
    n1 --> n2
    n2 --> n3
    npath --> adv
    adv --> advy
    adv --> advn
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Flow of CarterŌĆōWegman Universal Function

```mermaid
graph TB
    blockA["Input key x in Zp"]
    blockB["Multiply a times x mod p"]
    blockC["Add b mod p"]
    blockD["Take final result mod m"]
    blockE["Output slot index in 0 to m minus 1"]
    blockF["Random parameters a in 1 to p-1 and b in 0 to p-1 chosen once"]

    blockF --> blockB
    blockA --> blockB
    blockB --> blockC
    blockC --> blockD
    blockD --> blockE
```

**Key design notes:**
- $a \neq 0$ ensures the linear map is a bijection on $\mathbb{Z}_p$ before reduction mod $m$.
- The final mod $m$ step is what introduces the *uniform collision bound*.
- Total randomness consumed: $\log_2 p + \log_2 p$ bits, i.e., $O(\log \vert U \vert)$ bits per hash family.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define a *universal family of hash functions*. State the CarterŌĆōWegman construction and explain why it satisfies the universality condition.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A family $H$ of hash functions from $U$ to $\{0,\ldots,m-1\}$ is universal if for all distinct $x, y \in U$: $\Pr_{h \in H}[h(x) = h(y)] \leq 1/m$.
- **(1 mark)** CarterŌĆōWegman construction: let $p$ be prime with $p > \vert U \vert$, then $H_{p,m} = \{h_{a,b}(x) = ((ax + b) \bmod p) \bmod m\}$ for $a \in \{1,\ldots,p-1\}, b \in \{0,\ldots,p-1\}$.
- **(1 mark)** Universality proof sketch: For fixed $x \neq y$, as $(a,b)$ varies, $a(x-y) \bmod p$ is uniformly distributed over non-zero residues; hence exactly $1/m$ of the choices give $h(x) = h(y) \bmod m$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Sessional Exam]**
What is a *perfect hash function*? Briefly describe the two-level FKS construction and state its expected space requirement.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A perfect hash function on a static set $S$ is injective: $h(x) \neq h(y)$ for all distinct $x, y \in S$ (no collisions).
- **(1 mark)** Two-level FKS scheme: (i) Level 1 ŌĆö random universal hash $h_1 : S \to \{0,\ldots,n-1\}$ creates buckets $B_j$ of size $n_j$; (ii) Level 2 ŌĆö each bucket $B_j$ gets a secondary table of size $n_j^{2}$ and a freshly chosen universal $h_2^{(j)}$ that is retried until injective on $B_j$.
- **(1 mark)** Expected total space: $n$ (Level 1) + $\sum_j n_j^{2}$ (Level 2) $< 3n$, giving $O(n)$ memory. Lookup time is $O(1)$ worst-case.

---

### Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, internal choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Universal Hashing + Expected Lookup Time**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define a *strongly universal* hash family. Using the CarterŌĆōWegman construction $H_{p,m}$, prove that for any two distinct keys $x, y \in \mathbb{Z}_p$ and any pair of slots $a, b \in \{0,\ldots,m-1\}$:
$$\Pr_{h \in H_{p,m}}\!\bigl[h(x) = a \;\land\; h(y) = b\bigr] = \frac{1}{m^{2}}$$
Hence conclude that $H_{p,m}$ is (ordinary) universal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Suppose $S$ contains $n = 1000$ keys and the hash table has $m = 1009$ slots (a prime). Using a universal hash family:
1. Compute the *expected chain length* $\alpha$.
2. Compute the *expected number of collisions* between a specific key $x$ and all other keys.
3. Using Markov's inequality, find an *upper bound* on $\Pr[\text{chain length at slot } j \geq 50]$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Strongly Universal + CarterŌĆōWegman proof (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A family $H$ mapping $U \to \{0,\ldots,m-1\}$ is strongly universal if for every pair $x \neq y$ and every pair of values $a, b$:
$$\Pr_{h \in H}[h(x)=a \land h(y)=b] = \frac{1}{m^{2}}$$

**Proof (5 marks):** For $H_{p,m} = \{h_{a,b}\}$ with $a \in \{1,\ldots,p-1\}, b \in \{0,\ldots,p-1\}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
&\Pr[h_{a,b}(x) = a_0 \land h_{a,b}(y) = b_0] \\[4pt]
&= \Pr\bigl[((ax + b) \bmod p) \bmod m = a_0 \;\land\; ((ay + b) \bmod p) \bmod m = b_0\bigr] \\[4pt]
&= \Pr\bigl[(ax + b) \bmod p \in A \;\land\; (ay + b) \bmod p \in B\bigr]
\end{aligned}$$

where $A$ is the set of residues mod $p$ that reduce to $a_0$ mod $m$, and $B$ similarly. Since $m \leq p$, we have $\vert A \vert = \lceil p/m \rceil$ or $\lfloor p/m \rfloor$, and $\sum \approx p/m$.

For fixed $a \neq 0$, the map $(a,b) \mapsto (ax+b, ay+b)$ is a bijection from $\mathbb{Z}_p^{2} \setminus \{a=0\}$ to $\{(u,v) : u - v = a(x-y) \bmod p\}$. The pair $(u,v)$ is uniform over the $p(p-1)$ valid pairs. The constraint $u \in A, v \in B$ has $\approx (p/m)^{2}$ favourable pairs, giving probability $\approx 1/m^{2}$ exactly.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Setting $a = b$ in the strongly universal property immediately yields
$$\Pr[h(x) = h(y)] = \sum_{c=0}^{m-1} \Pr[h(x)=c \land h(y)=c] = \sum_{c=0}^{m-1} \frac{1}{m^{2}} = \frac{1}{m} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

1. **Expected chain length (2 marks):**
$$\alpha = \frac{n}{m} = \frac{1000}{1009} \approx 0.991$$

2. **Expected number of colliding partners for key $x$ (2 marks):**
By universality, $\Pr[h(x) = h(y)] \leq 1/1009$ for any fixed $y \neq x$. Let $C_x = \sum_{y \neq x} Y_{xy}$. Then
$$E[C_x] = \sum_{y \neq x} E[Y_{xy}] = (n-1) \cdot \frac{1}{m} = \frac{999}{1009} \approx 0.990$$

3. **Markov bound on long chain (3 marks):**
Let $Z = $ chain length at slot $j$ = number of keys $y$ with $h(y) = j$. By symmetry, $E[Z] = n/m = 1000/1009 < 1$.

By **Markov's inequality**:
$$\Pr[Z \geq 50] \leq \frac{E[Z]}{50} = \frac{1000/1009}{50} < \frac{1}{50} = 0.02$$

So $\Pr[Z \geq 50] < 0.02$ (i.e., at most 2%). Note: a tighter bound via Chernoff gives exponentially small probability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Question A:**
> - **(Common loss of 2 marks)** Students often write "$\Pr[h(x) = h(y)] = 1/m$" instead of "$\leq 1/m$". Equality holds only for *strongly* universal families; ordinary universality allows the inequality.
> - **(Common loss of 1 mark)** Markov's inequality is $\Pr[Z \geq a] \leq E[Z]/a$ for non-negative $Z$; using $E[Z]/50$ directly (not $E[Z]/49$) is correct here.
> - **(Common loss of 2 marks)** Forgetting the "$\bmod p$" step in CarterŌĆōWegman and writing $((ax+b) \bmod m)$ directly loses the injectivity guarantee that drives the proof.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Perfect Hashing (FKS Scheme)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Describe the two-level perfect hashing scheme of Fredman, Koml├│s, and Szemer├®di (FKS). For a static set $S$ of size $n$ and Level 1 table of size $m = n$:
1. State the sizes of Level 2 tables.
2. Prove that the *expected total* Level 2 space satisfies $E[\sum_j n_j^{2}] < 2n$.
3. Hence show that the *expected total memory* of the FKS scheme is $O(n)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a static set $S = \{7, 14, 21, 28, 35\}$ with $n = 5$ keys, stored using FKS perfect hashing with $m = 5$.
1. Suppose Level 1 uses $h_1(x) = (3x) \bmod 5$. Compute the bucket sizes $n_0, n_1, n_2, n_3, n_4$.
2. For each non-empty bucket, state the required Level 2 table size.
3. Verify the total memory bound: compute $\sum_j n_j^{2}$ and compare against $2n = 10$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) FKS Scheme + Expected Space Proof (7 marks):**

**Description (2 marks):**
- Pick a universal hash function $h_1 : U \to \{0,\ldots,m-1\}$ with $m = n$ uniformly at random.
- Partition $S$ into buckets $B_j = \{x \in S : h_1(x) = j\}$ of sizes $n_j$.
- For each bucket $B_j$, allocate a Level 2 table of size $m_j = n_j^{2}$.
- Pick a Level 2 universal hash $h_2^{(j)} \in H_{p, m_j}$ uniformly at random; reject and resample if not injective on $B_j$. By Markov's inequality, success probability $> 1/2$, so expected retries $\leq 2$.

**Proof (4 marks):** Define indicator $X_{ij} = 1$ if $h_1(x_i) = h_1(x_j)$ (and $X_{ii} = 0$). Then $n_{h_1(x_i)} = 1 + \sum_{j \neq i} X_{ij}$. Computing $n_{h_1(x_i)}^{2}$:

$$n_{h_1(x_i)}^{2} = 1 + 2\sum_{j \neq i} X_{ij} + \sum_{j \neq i}\sum_{k \neq i} X_{ij} X_{ik}$$

Summing over all $i \in \{1,\ldots,n\}$ and taking expectations, using $E[X_{ij}] = 1/m = 1/n$ and $E[X_{ij}X_{ik}] \leq 1/n^{2}$ (by pairwise independence of $H_{p,m}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
E\!\left[\sum_{i} n_{h_1(x_i)}^{2}\right] &\leq n + 2 \cdot \frac{n(n-1)}{n} + \frac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{n^{2}} + \frac{n(n-1)}{n} \\[4pt]
&= n + 2(n-1) + \frac{(n-1)(n-2)}{n} + (n-1) \\[4pt]
&< 2n \quad \text{(for } n \geq 2\text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

(Detailed step: the last term $(n-1)$ comes from $j=k$ diagonal in the triple sum, contributing $E[X_{ij}^{2}] = E[X_{ij}] = 1/n$ for each of the $n(n-1)$ ordered $(i,j)$ pairs with $j \neq i$.)

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Total memory = Level 1 slots + Level 2 slots $\leq n + 2n = 3n = O(n)$. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b) Numerical Verification with $S = \{7, 14, 21, 28, 35\}$ (7 marks):**

1. **Bucket sizes with $h_1(x) = 3x \bmod 5$ (3 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
h_1(7)  &= 21 \bmod 5 = 1 \\
h_1(14) &= 42 \bmod 5 = 2 \\
h_1(21) &= 63 \bmod 5 = 3 \\
h_1(28) &= 84 \bmod 5 = 4 \\
h_1(35) &= 105 \bmod 5 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

So $n_0 = 1, n_1 = 1, n_2 = 1, n_3 = 1, n_4 = 1$ (perfect distribution ŌĆö every bucket has exactly one element).

2. **Level 2 table sizes (2 marks):** Since $n_j = 1$ for all $j$, $m_j = n_j^{2} = 1$ for every non-empty bucket. Total Level 2 slots = $\sum_j n_j^{2} = 5$.

3. **Memory bound verification (2 marks):**
$$\sum_{j=0}^{4} n_j^{2} = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 5 < 2n = 10 \quad \checkmark$$

The total memory is $5 + 5 = 10 < 3n = 15$. The bound $2n = 10$ on Level 2 space holds comfortably here because the keys are uniformly distributed modulo 5.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Question B:**
> - **(Common loss of 2 marks)** When summing $n_{h_1(x_i)}^{2}$, students often forget the **diagonal** $j = k$ contribution in the triple sum and get a wrong coefficient for the $E[X_{ij}X_{ik}]$ term.
> - **(Common loss of 1 mark)** Confusing $\sum_j n_j^{2}$ (which counts each key multiple times in collision pairs) with $\sum_j n_j$ (which is always $= n$). The squaring is **not** a typo ŌĆö it is the core of FKS!
> - **(Common loss of 2 marks)** In numerical parts, students forget the **final $\bmod m$** step after $\bmod p$ in CarterŌĆōWegman ŌĆö without it, $h(x)$ can exceed $m-1$ and the modular family is broken.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Universal family**: $\Pr[h(x) = h(y)] \leq 1/m$ ŌĆö the *defining* inequality to remember verbatim.
- **Strongly universal** (pairwise independent): $\Pr[h(x)=a \land h(y)=b] = 1/m^{2}$.
- **CarterŌĆōWegman family size**: $p(p-1)$ for $h_{a,b}(x) = ((ax+b) \bmod p) \bmod m$, with $a \neq 0$.
- **Expected successful lookup** under universal hashing: $E[T] \leq 1 + \alpha$ where $\alpha = n/m$.
- **Perfect hashing applies only to static sets** ŌĆö cannot handle insertions/deletions efficiently.
- **FKS two-level**: Level 1 buckets + Level 2 mini-tables of size $n_j^{2}$.
- **FKS expected total memory**: $< 3n$ (Level 1 + Level 2).
- **FKS lookup**: $O(1)$ **worst-case** time using two hash evaluations + one array access.
- **Markov's inequality** is the workhorse for retry-bound analysis of Level 2 hashing.
- **Pairwise independence** of CarterŌĆōWegman yields $E[X_{ij}X_{ik}] \leq 1/n^{2}$, which is the technical heart of the $\sum n_j^{2} < 2n$ proof.
- **Strong universality ŌćÆ ordinary universality** (marginalise over the common value).
- **Tabulation hashing** (a faster practical alternative) is also universal but not strongly universal ŌĆö important nuance for KTU viva questions.
- **KTU Module 2 weightage**: Expect 7ŌĆō10 marks directly from this topic in ESE; perfect-hashing space derivation is a perennial favourite.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Skip Lists
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST639 - RANDOMIZED ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Skip Lists

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Skip List** is a **probabilistic data structure** introduced by **William Pugh in 1989** that allows fast search, insertion, and deletion of elements in a **sorted sequence**. It consists of a hierarchy of linked lists, where the bottom level contains all the elements (the base sorted linked list), and each higher level acts as an "express lane" containing a randomly selected subset of elements from the level below.

Formally, a skip list is a collection of sorted linked lists $\mathcal{L}_1, \mathcal{L}_2, \dots, \mathcal{L}_h$ such that:
- $\mathcal{L}_1$ contains every element of the set.
- For $i \geq 2$, $\mathcal{L}_i$ is a sub-sequence of $\mathcal{L}_{i-1}$ chosen by a **randomized coin flip** (typically with probability $p = \tfrac{1}{2}$).
- The height $h$ is bounded by $O(\log n)$ with high probability, where $n$ is the number of elements.

The expected time complexity for search, insert, and delete is $\mathbf{O(\log n)}$ in the average case, matching balanced BSTs without the explicit rebalancing logic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Skip Lists are a flagship example of how **randomization** can be used to obtain expected $O(\log n)$ data structure performance without deterministic balancing overhead (like AVL rotations or Red-Black recoloring). This makes them mandatory study under Module 2 of PECST639.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The "Express Lane" Train System

Imagine a **train journey from Cochin to Trivandrum** with stations ordered alphabetically:
- **Local Train (Level 1):** Stops at every single station. Slow but always available.
- **Fast Train (Level 2):** Stops only at major junctions.
- **Superfast Express (Level 3):** Stops only at major cities like Ernakulam, Thrissur, and Trivandrum.

When you want to reach a destination, you ride the **Superfast Express as far as you can**, then drop down to the **Fast Train**, and finally transfer to the **Local Train** for the last leg. You never overshoot, because each higher level still preserves the sorted order of stations.

This is precisely how a **Skip List** works: instead of scanning every node (like the local train), the search leaps through express lanes, dropping down a level whenever the next node on the current level would overshoot the target.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The randomness in *which* nodes get promoted to higher levels is what guarantees balanced performance on average ŌĆö without needing any explicit rotation or rebalancing rules.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Used Throughout KTU Examinations

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Level** | A horizontal sorted linked list. Level 1 is the base. |
| **Node** | Stores a key (and optional satellite data). |
| **Height** | The maximum level of any node currently in the structure. |
| **Header Node** | A sentinel node that anchors all levels. |
| **Forward Pointer** | The `next[i]` pointer at level $i$. |
| **Promotion Probability** $p$ | The probability that a node advances to the next higher level (standard value: $p = 1/2$). |

## 1.4 Why Skip Lists? Engineering Motivation

| Reason | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| Simple implementation | No complex rotation / recoloring logic |
| Lock-friendly concurrency | Multiple threads can modify different regions safely |
| Expected $O(\log n)$ | Matches AVL/Red-Black trees in practice |
| Probabilistic guarantees | Self-balancing without deterministic restructuring |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world usage:** Skip Lists are used in **Redis (sorted sets)**, **Apache HBase**, **LevelDB**, and **memSQL** because of their excellent concurrent performance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö How a Skip List Works

### Step-by-Step Construction Logic
1. **Initialize** an empty skip list with a header node. Decide the maximum allowed level $L_{max}$ (commonly $\log_{1/p} n$).
2. **Insertion** of a key proceeds in two phases:
   - **Phase A (Search path):** Find the position where the key belongs at Level 1 by traversing express lanes top-down.
   - **Phase B (Randomized height):** Flip a fair coin repeatedly. The node is promoted to Level $k+1$ if the coin lands heads (probability $p$).
3. **Search** traverses from the top level, moving right while the next key is smaller, and dropping down when no further rightward progress is possible.
4. **Deletion** removes the node from all levels where it exists by splicing out its forward pointers.

### The 'Why' Behind Randomization

The deterministic alternative (e.g., balanced BSTs) must maintain **strict** invariants such as height-balance or color rules. This requires expensive operations like rotations. Randomization **relaxes** these constraints:

- We accept occasional "imbalances" because on average, the structure **behaves** like a balanced BST.
- The **expected** search path length is bounded because the number of nodes at each level drops exponentially.

## 2.2 Coin-Flip Model of Node Heights

Let $p$ = probability of promotion (head), $q = 1 - p$ = probability of stopping (tail).

- A node has height exactly $k$ if and only if the first $k-1$ coin flips are heads and the $k$-th is tails.
- The expected height of a node is therefore $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot p^{k-1} q = \dfrac{1}{q}$.

For $p = q = \tfrac{1}{2}$, the expected node height is $\mathbf{2}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Result | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(\text{height} \geq k) = p^{k-1}$ | Probability a node reaches level $\geq k$ |
| 2 | $P(\text{height} = k) = p^{k-1}(1-p)$ | Exact probability of level $k$ |
| 3 | $\mathbb{E}[\text{height}] = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Expected node height |
| 4 | $n_k \approx n \cdot p^{k-1}$ | Expected number of nodes at level $k$ |
| 5 | $h \approx \log_{1/p} n = \dfrac{\log n}{\log(1/p)}$ | Expected maximum level (height) |
| 6 | $\mathbb{E}[\text{search cost}] = O(\log n)$ | Expected search time complexity |
| 7 | $\mathbb{E}[\text{space}] = O(n)$ | Expected space complexity |
| 8 | $P(h \geq c \log n) = \tfrac{1}{n^{c-1}}$ | High-probability bound on height (Chernoff-style) |
| 9 | $\mathbb{E}[\text{horizontal moves at level } k] = \dfrac{1}{q}$ | Forward scan bound |
| 10 | $T_{\text{search}} = \dfrac{1}{q} \cdot \log_{1/p} n = O(\log n)$ | Total expected search time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Note:** For the canonical KTU setting, examiners assume **$p = q = 1/2$**, so the expected node height is $\mathbf{2}$ and the expected maximum level is $\mathbf{\log_2 n}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Distributed Systems** | Skip Lists underpin the sorted-set structures in **Redis (ZSET)**. |
| **Database Indexing** | Used in **HBase**, **LevelDB**, **RocksDB** for memtable indexing. |
| **Network Routing** | Certain peer-to-peer overlay networks use randomized skip list structures. |
| **Concurrent Programming** | Easier to lock-free synchronize than AVL/Red-Black trees. |

## 2.5 Backwards Analysis Intuition

The expected number of **horizontal steps** at level $k$ equals $\tfrac{1}{q}$ because, when scanning backward from a randomly chosen node at level $k$, we expect $\tfrac{1}{q}$ nodes before reaching one that fails to promote (tails appears within $\tfrac{1}{q}$ flips).

Combining **vertical** ($\log_{1/p} n$ levels) and **horizontal** ($\tfrac{1}{q}$ per level) cost gives the classical $O(\log n)$ bound.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Probability of a Node Reaching Level k

Let $X$ = height of a new node after a sequence of independent Bernoulli($p$) trials.

**Step 1.** A node reaches level $k$ if and only if the first $k-1$ flips are heads.

**Step 2.** Since flips are independent,
$$P(X \geq k) = p^{k-1}$$

**Step 3.** Hence, the exact probability of height exactly $k$ is
$$P(X = k) = P(X \geq k) - P(X \geq k+1) = p^{k-1} - p^{k} = p^{k-1}(1-p)$$

**Step 4.** Specializing to $p = \tfrac{1}{2}$:
$$P(X = k) = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{k}$$

This geometric distribution decays exponentially, which is precisely the reason skip lists achieve logarithmic performance.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Expected Height of a Node

Compute $\mathbb{E}[X]$ using the geometric series identity:

$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot p^{k-1}(1-p)
$$

Substituting $q = 1 - p$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = q \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot p^{k-1}
$$

Using the well-known identity $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k x^{k-1} = \dfrac{1}{(1-x)^2}$ with $x = p$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = q \cdot \frac{1}{(1-p)^2} = q \cdot \frac{1}{q^2} = \frac{1}{q}
$$

For $p = q = \tfrac{1}{2}$, $\mathbb{E}[X] = \mathbf{2}$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Expected Number of Nodes at Level k

Each of the $n$ existing nodes independently reaches level $k$ with probability $p^{k-1}$. By linearity of expectation:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\text{nodes at level } k] = n \cdot p^{k-1}
$$

For $p = \tfrac{1}{2}$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\text{nodes at level } k] = \frac{n}{2^{k-1}}
$$

Hence the expected total number of pointers across all nodes is:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} n \cdot p^{k-1} = \frac{n}{1-p}
$$

For $p = \tfrac{1}{2}$, expected space is $\mathbf{2n}$ pointers, i.e., $O(n)$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Expected Maximum Level h

We want the smallest $h$ such that expected number of nodes at level $h$ is $\leq 1$:
$$n \cdot p^{h-1} \leq 1$$

Solving for $h$:

$$
h \geq \log_{1/p} n
$$

For $p = \tfrac{1}{2}$:
$$h_{\max} \approx \log_2 n$$

The expected height is therefore $\mathbf{\log_2 n}$.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Expected Search Time via Backwards Analysis

Consider the search path from the target node back up to the top header. This path alternates between horizontal steps (forward pointers traversed at a fixed level) and vertical steps (downward transitions).

**Step 1 (Horizontal analysis):** When a node at level $k$ is reached, the number of nodes to its right at the same level before finding one that was promoted from level $k-1$ follows a geometric distribution with success probability $q$. So expected horizontal steps per level = $\tfrac{1}{q}$.

**Step 2 (Vertical count):** The number of levels traversed is the height of the target, which we bound by $O(\log n)$.

**Step 3 (Total expected search cost):**

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{\text{search}}] = \frac{1}{q} \cdot \log_{1/p} n
$$

For $p = q = \tfrac{1}{2}$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{\text{search}}] = 2 \log_2 n = O(\log n)
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison with Other Structures

| Operation | Sorted Linked List | Balanced BST (AVL/Red-Black) | Skip List |
|---|---|---|---|
| Search (worst) | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ worst, $O(\log n)$ expected |
| Search (expected) | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Insert (expected) | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Delete (expected) | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Implementation | Trivial | Complex (rotations) | Moderate |
| Concurrent locking | Easy | Hard | Easy (lock-free variants exist) |
| Space | $n$ pointers | $n$ pointers + balance data | $\approx 2n$ pointers expected |

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A skip list has $n = 16$ elements with $p = 1/2$. Estimate:
1. The expected maximum height.
2. The expected number of nodes at level 3.

**Solution:**

**(1) Expected maximum height:**
$$h = \log_{1/p} n = \log_2 16 = 4$$

**(2) Expected nodes at level 3:**
$$\mathbb{E}[\text{nodes at level 3}] = \frac{n}{2^{3-1}} = \frac{16}{4} = 4$$

**Answer:** Expected maximum height is **4**; expected nodes at level 3 is **4**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of a Skip List Operation

The following block-level diagram captures the interactions during a **Search Operation** in a skip list. (Mermaid cannot easily render the crisscrossing horizontal pointers of a real skip list, so we use a layered functional flow.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Search Initiation: target key K]) --> startB[Set current node to header]
    startB --> startC[Set current level L to max height h]
    startC --> levelCheck{Is forward node at level L less than K?}
    levelCheck -- Yes --> stepA[Move forward at level L]
    stepA --> levelCheck
    levelCheck -- No --> dropCheck{Is L greater than 1?}
    dropCheck -- Yes --> dropDown[Drop down one level: L minus 1]
    dropDown --> levelCheck
    dropCheck -- No --> finalCheck{Is forward node at level 1 equal to K?}
    finalCheck -- Yes --> success([Search SUCCESS: node found])
    finalCheck -- No --> failure([Search FAILURE: key not present])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every "No" branch from `levelCheck` causes a **level drop**, mimicking the "transferring from express train to local train" analogy. The expected number of such drops is $O(\log n)$.

## 4.2 Insertion Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    insA([Insertion Initiation: key K]) --> insB[Locate insert positions update array update 1 ... update h]
    insB --> insC[Determine random height via repeated coin flips]
    insC --> flipCheck{Flip a coin: heads or tails?}
    flipCheck -- Heads --> nextLevel[Increase level by 1]
    nextLevel --> flipCheck
    flipCheck -- Tails --> finalizeHeight[Set final level L]
    finalizeHeight --> splice[Splice new node into levels 1 ... L using update array]
    splice --> done([Insertion COMPLETE])
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Skip List Structure (Conceptual ASCII Rendering)

For deeper intuition, here is a **textual sketch** of a skip list storing $\{7, 14, 21, 25, 30, 42, 51\}$:

```
Level 4:  HEAD -----------------------------------------------------------------> NIL
Level 3:  HEAD --------> 14 ---------------------------------------------------> NIL
Level 2:  HEAD --------> 14 --------> 21 --------> 30 --------> 42 -----------> NIL
Level 1:  HEAD --> 7 --> 14 --> 21 --> 25 --> 30 --> 42 --> 51 ------------> NIL
```

**Interpretation:**
- Level 4 is empty (only the header sentinel).
- Level 3 contains only 14 (a node that "won" three consecutive coin flips).
- Level 2 contains $\{14, 21, 30, 42\}$.
- Level 1 contains every element.

When searching for **25**, the algorithm traverses:
- **Level 3:** 14 ŌåÆ drop down.
- **Level 2:** 21 ŌåÆ drop down.
- **Level 1:** 25 found.

This requires only **3 forward moves and 2 level drops**, vastly fewer than the 5 linear scans required in a plain sorted linked list.

## 4.4 Memory Layout Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Node 25"
    node25K["key = 25"]
    node25L1["next 1: 30"]
    node25L2["next 2: NULL"]
    end
    subgraph "Node 21"
    node21K["key = 21"]
    node21L1["next 1: 25"]
    node21L2["next 2: 30"]
    end
    subgraph "Node 14"
    node14K["key = 14"]
    node14L1["next 1: 21"]
    node14L2["next 2: 21"]
    node14L3["next 3: NULL"]
    end
```

Each node stores an array of forward pointers indexed by level. Node 14, for instance, has 3 pointers because it was randomly promoted through 3 levels.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Skip List. Explain how randomization is used in skip list operations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Skip List** is a probabilistic data structure that maintains a sorted set of elements using multiple levels of linked lists, where the lowest level contains all elements and each higher level contains a randomly chosen subset of the level below. **(1 mark)**

**Randomization** is used during insertion: the height of each new node is determined by repeatedly flipping a biased coin (with probability $p = 1/2$). For each "head," the node is promoted to the next higher level; on the first "tail," the height is finalized. **(1 mark)**

This randomization yields expected time complexity of $O(\log n)$ for search, insertion, and deletion, **without** requiring explicit rebalancing logic like AVL rotations. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2 ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the expected number of nodes at level k in a skip list with n elements and promotion probability p = 1/2?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The expected number of nodes at level $k$ is given by:

$$\mathbb{E}[n_k] = n \cdot p^{k-1}$$

For $p = \tfrac{1}{2}$:

$$\mathbb{E}[n_k] = \frac{n}{2^{k-1}}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Example:** For $n = 100$, the expected number of nodes at level 3 is $\tfrac{100}{4} = 25$. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### Question 1A ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the Search and Insertion algorithms in a Skip List with a suitable example diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Search Algorithm (3.5 marks):**

The Search operation traverses the skip list top-down, moving right whenever possible:

1. Start at the header node at the highest level.
2. While the next key on the current level is less than the search key $K$, advance forward.
3. Drop down one level.
4. Repeat until level 1 is reached.
5. If the next node on level 1 equals $K$, the search succeeds; otherwise it fails.

**Example:** Searching for **25** in the skip list from ┬¦4.3:
- Level 3: header ŌåÆ 14 (14 < 25, advance), next is NIL ŌåÆ drop to Level 2.
- Level 2: 14 ŌåÆ 21 (21 < 25, advance), next is 30 (30 > 25) ŌåÆ drop to Level 1.
- Level 1: 21 ŌåÆ 25 (25 == 25) ŌåÆ **success**, found.

**Insertion Algorithm (3.5 marks):**

The Insert operation proceeds in two phases:

**Phase A ŌĆö Locate insert positions:**
1. Perform a search to find the predecessor of the new key at every level, storing these predecessors in an array `update[1..h]`.

**Phase B ŌĆö Random height generation:**
2. Flip a coin repeatedly. For each head, the node advances to the next level. Stop on the first tail.
3. If the resulting height exceeds the current maximum, extend the header's level accordingly.

**Phase C ŌĆö Splicing:**
4. Insert the new node at each level $1, 2, \dots, L$ by linking it between `update[i]` and `update[i].next[i]`.

**Example:** Inserting **28** into the skip list of ┬¦4.3:
- Search path: 14 (L3) ŌåÆ 21 (L2) ŌåÆ 25 (L1).
- Coin flips: H, H, T ŌåÆ node height = 2.
- Splice 28 into Level 1 (between 25 and 30) and Level 2 (between 21 and 30).

---

**(b) Analyze the expected time complexity of Search in a Skip List using backwards analysis. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Setting up the analysis (2 marks):**
Let $p$ be the promotion probability and $q = 1 - p$ the stopping probability. Assume $p = q = 1/2$.

**Backwards analysis (3 marks):**
Trace the search path backwards from the target node. The path consists of alternating horizontal and vertical moves.

- **Vertical moves:** The number of levels descended equals the height of the target, which is $O(\log n)$ (by Derivation 4).
- **Horizontal moves per level:** When moving backward from a random node at level $k$, the number of forward pointers at the same level before finding a non-promoted node follows a geometric distribution with mean $\tfrac{1}{q}$.

For $p = q = \tfrac{1}{2}$:

$$\mathbb{E}[\text{horizontal moves per level}] = \frac{1}{q} = 2$$

**Total expected cost (2 marks):**

$$\mathbb{E}[T_{\text{search}}] = \frac{1}{q} \cdot \log_{1/p} n = 2 \log_2 n = O(\log n)$$

**Conclusion:** The expected time complexity of Skip List Search is **$\mathbf{O(\log n)}$**, matching balanced BSTs in the average case.

---

### Question 1B (Alternative Choice) ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the Deletion algorithm in a Skip List with an example. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Deletion operation removes a key from all levels where it appears:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Search for the target (2 marks):**
1. Traverse the skip list top-down as in Search.
2. Maintain an array `update[i]` storing the predecessor at each level.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Verify existence (1 mark):**
3. If the next node on level 1 is not equal to the target, the key is absent and the operation terminates.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Splice out at each level (3 marks):**
4. For each level $i$ from 1 up to the node's height:
   - Set `update[i].next[i]` to `target.next[i]` (bypass the node).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Trim header (1 mark):**
5. If the removed node was at the maximum level, decrement the header's level accordingly.

**Example:** Deleting **21** from the skip list of ┬¦4.3:
- `update[1]` = 14, `update[2]` = 14, `update[3]` = header.
- Splice: at L1, link 14 ŌåÆ 25; at L2, link 14 ŌåÆ 30.
- Header level remains 3 (21 did not occupy Level 3).

---

**(b) Compare Skip Lists with Balanced Binary Search Trees (AVL/Red-Black) in terms of complexity, implementation effort, and concurrency. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Skip List | AVL/Red-Black Tree |
|---|---|---|
| Time complexity (avg) | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Time complexity (worst) | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Space | $O(n)$ expected (~2n pointers) | $O(n)$ + balance data |
| Implementation | Simple ŌĆö only pointers | Complex ŌĆö rotations + recoloring |
| Rebalancing | Automatic via randomness | Manual deterministic rules |
| Concurrency | Easier (lock-free variants) | Harder (global rebalancing) |
| Performance variance | Probabilistic but bounded | Deterministic |

**Discussion (3 marks):**
- Skip Lists trade **worst-case** guarantees for **implementation simplicity**.
- For **read-mostly workloads**, both structures perform similarly.
- For **write-heavy concurrent workloads**, Skip Lists are often preferred due to localized pointer modifications.
- In **probabilistic analysis**, Skip Lists offer nearly identical average-case guarantees to balanced BSTs but with substantially simpler code, making them attractive for production systems like Redis.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing expected vs. worst-case complexity.** Skip Lists are $O(\log n)$ in **expected** time, **not** worst-case. Writing $O(\log n)$ without the qualifier loses marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of $p$.** Always state the assumed promotion probability ($p = 1/2$ unless stated otherwise).
> 3. **Skipping the update[] array in Insert/Delete.** Examiners specifically test whether students remember the two-phase pattern: *search-and-record* then *splice*.
> 4. **Not justifying the height bound.** Stating "$h = \log n$" without deriving it from $n \cdot p^{h-1} \leq 1$ costs significant marks.
> 5. **Mixing up horizontal vs. vertical analysis.** In backwards analysis, students often forget that horizontal moves are geometric and vertical moves correspond to the height.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap ŌĆö Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Skip List = sorted linked lists stacked in levels with **randomized promotion**.
- **Promotion rule:** New node's height is determined by repeated coin flips with heads probability $p = 1/2$.
- **Search pattern:** Top-down, right-when-possible, drop when next overshoots.
- **Insert pattern:** Two-phase ŌĆö (i) record predecessor via search, (ii) random height, (iii) splice.
- **Delete pattern:** Search + splice at all occupied levels + trim header.
- **Expected node height** for $p = 1/2$ is **2**.
- **Expected maximum level** is $\log_2 n$.
- **Expected number of nodes at level $k$** is $n / 2^{k-1}$.
- **Expected search cost** is $\tfrac{1}{q} \cdot \log_{1/p} n = 2 \log_2 n = O(\log n)$.
- **Expected space** is $\approx 2n$ pointers = $O(n)$.
- **Real-world use:** Redis ZSET, LevelDB, HBase memtable, RocksDB.
- **Key advantage over balanced BSTs:** Simpler implementation + better concurrent behaviour.
- **Key disadvantage:** Probabilistic rather than worst-case guarantees.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** Always derive any numerical answer using the three core formulae:
> 1. $P(\text{height} = k) = p^{k-1}(1-p)$
> 2. $\mathbb{E}[n_k] = n \cdot p^{k-1}$
> 3. $\mathbb{E}[T_{\text{search}}] = \dfrac{1}{1-p} \cdot \log_{1/p} n$
>
> Substituting $p = 1/2$ gives the canonical numerical answers examiners expect.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bloom Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST639 - RANDOMIZED ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bloom Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Bloom Filter** is a space-efficient, randomized probabilistic data structure conceived by Burton Howard Bloom in 1970. It is used to test whether an element is a member of a set. The defining mathematical property of a Bloom Filter is that it answers membership queries with one-sided error: it can definitively return a **false positive** (claiming an element is present when it is not), but it **never** returns a **false negative** (claiming an element is absent when it actually is in the set). In formal notation, for any element $x$:

$$\text{Query}(x) = \begin{cases} \text{Definitely NO}, & \text{if } x \notin S \text{ and all hash bits are 0} \\ \text{Probably YES}, & \text{if all hash bits are 1 (may be false positive)} \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A Bloom Filter guarantees **zero false negatives**. The only possible error is a **false positive**. This asymmetric error property makes it invaluable in applications where a "maybe" is acceptable but a "miss" is catastrophic (e.g., cache lookups, malicious URL detection).

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Nightclub Guest List with Multiple Bouncers**

Imagine a VIP nightclub with a strict guest list. Instead of one bouncer carrying a thick paper register, the club employs **5 bouncers**, each using a different rule of thumb to remember guests:

- Bouncer 1 remembers guests whose names start with a vowel.
- Bouncer 2 remembers guests taller than 6 feet.
- Bouncer 3 remembers guests wearing red shoes.
- Bouncer 4 remembers guests who arrived in the last hour.
- Bouncer 5 remembers guests who paid in cash.

When a new person arrives, all 5 bouncers are asked simultaneously:
- If **even one** bouncer says "I don't recall this person" ŌåÆ the person is **definitely NOT on the list** (no false negatives).
- If **all 5 bouncers** say "I think I've seen them" ŌåÆ the person is **probably on the list** (could be a false positive because two different guests could match all 5 conditions by coincidence).

This is exactly how a Bloom Filter works. Each bouncer is a **hash function**, and their memory is a shared **bit array**.

## 1.3 Key Structural Components

A Bloom Filter is constructed from three mathematical primitives:

| Component | Symbol | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| Bit Array (Bit Vector) | $m$ | An array of $m$ bits, all initialized to **0** |
| Hash Functions | $k$ | A family of $k$ independent, uniform hash functions $h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_k$ |
| Inserted Elements | $n$ | The number of elements currently in the set $S$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The defining **standard metrics** evaluated in KTU examinations are:
> - **Space efficiency**: $O(m)$ bits, where $m \ll$ storing the actual set
> - **Time efficiency**: $O(k)$ per operation (insertion or query)
> - **False positive probability**: $f$ ŌĆö the rate at which non-members are incorrectly reported as members

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
