# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics (Step-by-Step Logic)

The Bloom Filter operates through two fundamental operations, each driven by randomized hashing:

### Step 1 ŌĆö Initialization
A bit array $B$ of size $m$ is created. Every bit $B[i]$ is set to **0**:

$$B = [\underbrace{0, 0, 0, 0, \ldots, 0, 0}_{m \text{ bits}}]$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Insertion of Element $x$
For each of the $k$ hash functions, compute the bit position and set it to 1:

$$B\bigl[h_1(x)\bigr] \leftarrow 1, \quad B\bigl[h_2(x)\bigr] \leftarrow 1, \quad \ldots, \quad B\bigl[h_k(x)\bigr] \leftarrow 1$$

**Why this works:** If a particular bit is already 1, it remains 1. Once set to 1, a bit **never reverts to 0** in a standard Bloom Filter. This irreversibility is precisely what prevents false negatives.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Membership Query for Element $y$
Compute the $k$ hash positions and check the bit array:

$$\text{Query}(y) = \begin{cases} \text{ŌĆ£Probably in setŌĆØ} & \text{if } B[h_i(y)] = 1 \text{ for all } i = 1, 2, \ldots, k \\ \text{ŌĆ£Definitely NOT in setŌĆØ} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö The 'Why' Behind the Probabilistic Nature
Because multiple distinct elements can hash to overlapping bit positions, the query result of "all 1s" is **necessary but not sufficient** evidence of membership. The collision in bit positions is the *source* of false positives.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every equation you must memorize for KTU examinations. All symbols follow standard MIT/CLRS notation.

| # | Formula | Description | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $p_{\text{zero}} = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{m}\right)^{kn} \approx e^{-kn/m}$ | Probability a given bit remains **0** after $n$ insertions with $k$ hashes | Foundation for deriving false positive rate |
| 2 | $f = \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^{k}$ | **False positive probability** $f$ as a function of $m, n, k$ | Direct measure of filter accuracy |
| 3 | $k_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{m}{n} \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \cdot \dfrac{m}{n}$ | **Optimal number of hash functions** to minimize $f$ for fixed $m, n$ | Used to design the most efficient filter |
| 4 | $m_{\text{opt}} = -\dfrac{n \ln f}{(\ln 2)^{2}}$ | **Optimal bit array size** for a target false positive rate $f$ | Used when designer specifies acceptable $f$ |
| 5 | $f_{\min} = \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{k} = 2^{-k}$ | **Minimum achievable false positive rate** (when $k = k_{\text{opt}}$) | Theoretical lower bound on $f$ |
| 6 | $m/n = -\dfrac{\log_2 f}{0.6931} = -1.44 \log_2 f$ | **Bits per element** required for target false positive $f$ | Memory budgeting for large-scale systems |
| 7 | Time per operation: $O(k)$ | Insertion and query complexity | Constant-time lookup guarantee |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical notation note for KTU valuation:** The symbol $f$ denotes the false positive probability, while $p_{\text{zero}}$ denotes the probability of a bit remaining zero. Do NOT interchange them in the examination hall ŌĆö this is a frequent marker of a partial-credit answer.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bloom Filters are deployed in production systems where memory is scarce and occasional false positives are tolerable:

- **Google Bigtable & Apache HBase**: To avoid costly disk lookups for non-existent rows.
- **Bitcoin (BTC)**: To synchronize the lightweight SPV wallet with full nodes on the network.
- **Medium & Quora**: To filter out articles already seen by the user.
- **Network routers**: To identify malicious URLs in cache hierarchies.
- **Database joins (Bloom join)**: To pre-filter tuples before expensive distributed joins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The engineering trade-off is always **space vs. accuracy**. Doubling the number of bits $m$ (and thereby halving $f$) costs memory but preserves $O(k)$ query time. This makes Bloom Filters uniquely suited to big-data pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: False Positive Probability $f$

**Goal:** Derive the probability that all $k$ bit positions checked during a query of a *non-member* element are 1.

### Step 1: Set up the basic probability of a bit being set
Each hash function $h_i$ is assumed to be a uniform random function mapping elements to one of $m$ bit positions. For a single insertion, the probability that a given bit position is selected by one specific hash function is:

$$P(\text{bit set by one hash}) = \frac{1}{m}$$

### Step 2: Probability that the bit is NOT set by one hash
The probability that a specific hash function does *not* flip this particular bit is:

$$P(\text{bit NOT set}) = 1 - \frac{1}{m}$$

### Step 3: Probability that the bit is NOT set by any of the $k$ hash functions of one element
Because the $k$ hash functions are independent, multiply the single-hash probabilities:

$$P(\text{bit not set after one element}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{k}$$

### Step 4: Probability that the bit is NOT set after $n$ distinct elements
Since each of the $n$ inserted elements independently uses $k$ hashes, the total number of hash evaluations is $kn$. Assuming independence across elements:

$$p_{\text{zero}} = P(\text{bit still } 0 \text{ after all insertions}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{kn}$$

### Step 5: Apply the standard limit identity
For large $m$, we use the well-known limit $\lim_{m \to \infty}\left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{m} = e^{-1}$. Substituting $m \to m/(kn)$:

$$\left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{kn} = \left[\left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{m}\right]^{kn/m} \approx \left(e^{-1}\right)^{kn/m} = e^{-kn/m}$$

Therefore:

$$p_{\text{zero}} \approx e^{-kn/m}$$

### Step 6: Probability a bit is 1 (the "set" probability)
By the complement rule:

$$p_{\text{one}} = 1 - p_{\text{zero}} = 1 - e^{-kn/m}$$

### Step 7: Probability of a false positive on a non-member query
A false positive occurs when **all $k$** hash positions of the queried non-member element point to bits that are already 1. Since the $k$ bit positions are distinct (with high probability) and independent:

$$f = (p_{\text{one}})^{k} = \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^{k}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **master equation** for Bloom Filter analysis. Every other result in the formula sheet is derived from this single expression.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Optimal Number of Hash Functions $k_{\text{opt}}$

**Goal:** Find the value of $k$ that minimizes $f$ for fixed $m$ and $n$.

### Step 1: Define the cost function
We treat $f$ as a function of $k$ with $m$ and $n$ held constant:

$$f(k) = \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^{k}$$

### Step 2: Take the natural logarithm to simplify the minimization
Let $g(k) = \ln f(k)$. Minimizing $f$ is equivalent to minimizing $g$:

$$g(k) = k \cdot \ln\left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)$$

### Step 3: Differentiate $g(k)$ with respect to $k$
Using the product rule, where the derivative of the inner $\ln$ term requires the chain rule (the inner argument also depends on $k$):

$$\frac{dg}{dk} = \ln\left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right) + k \cdot \frac{1}{1 - e^{-kn/m}} \cdot \left(-e^{-kn/m}\right) \cdot \left(-\frac{n}{m}\right)$$

$$\frac{dg}{dk} = \ln\left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right) + \frac{k \cdot n/m \cdot e^{-kn/m}}{1 - e^{-kn/m}}$$

### Step 4: Set $\frac{dg}{dk} = 0$ for the optimum
$$\ln\left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right) = -\frac{k \cdot n/m \cdot e^{-kn/m}}{1 - e^{-kn/m}}$$

### Step 5: Recognize the structural simplification
Let $u = e^{-kn/m}$. Then $1 - e^{-kn/m} = 1 - u$ and the equation becomes:

$$\ln(1 - u) = -\frac{ku}{1 - u}$$

### Step 6: Try the candidate $u = 1/2$
Substituting $u = 1/2$:

- Left side: $\ln(1/2) = -\ln 2$
- Right side: $-\dfrac{k \cdot (1/2)}{1/2} = -k$

Equating: $-\ln 2 = -k$, hence $k = \ln 2$.

### Step 7: Solve for $k$ in terms of $m$ and $n$
The condition $u = 1/2$ means $e^{-kn/m} = 1/2$, so:

$$\frac{kn}{m} = \ln 2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k = \frac{m}{n}\ln 2$$

Thus:

$$k_{\text{opt}} = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \cdot \frac{m}{n}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Optimal Bit Array Size $m_{\text{opt}}$

**Goal:** Given a target false positive rate $f$, find the minimum $m$ required.

### Step 1: Substitute $k = k_{\text{opt}}$ into the master equation
When $k = (m/n)\ln 2$, the exponent becomes $kn/m = \ln 2$, and:

$$e^{-kn/m} = e^{-\ln 2} = \frac{1}{2}$$

### Step 2: Compute the resulting minimum $f$
$$f_{\min} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\right)^{k_{\text{opt}}} = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{k_{\text{opt}}} = 2^{-k_{\text{opt}}}$$

### Step 3: Take logarithm base 2
$$k_{\text{opt}} = -\log_2 f_{\min}$$

### Step 4: Equate with the $k_{\text{opt}}$ expression
$$\frac{m}{n}\ln 2 = -\log_2 f_{\min}$$

### Step 5: Convert $\log_2$ to $\ln$ using $\log_2 x = \frac{\ln x}{\ln 2}$
$$\frac{m}{n}\ln 2 = -\frac{\ln f_{\min}}{\ln 2}$$

### Step 6: Solve for $m$
$$m = -\frac{n \cdot \ln f_{\min}}{(\ln 2)^{2}} \approx -1.4427 \cdot n \cdot \ln f_{\min}$$

For a given target $f$, the minimum bit array size is:

$$\boxed{m_{\text{opt}} = -\frac{n \ln f}{(\ln 2)^{2}}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A Bloom Filter uses $m = 8000$ bits, $n = 100$ inserted elements, and $k = 4$ hash functions. Compute the false positive rate $f$.

**Solution:**

$$\frac{kn}{m} = \frac{4 \times 100}{8000} = 0.05$$

$$e^{-0.05} \approx 0.9512$$

$$p_{\text{one}} = 1 - 0.9512 = 0.0488$$

$$f = (0.0488)^{4} = 5.66 \times 10^{-6}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is approximately **1 false positive in 176,000 queries** ŌĆö an exceptionally efficient design that uses only $8000/100 = 80$ bits per element.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bloom Filter Architecture ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete lifecycle of a Bloom Filter: initialization, insertion, and query.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Bit Array B of size m] --> B[All Bits Set to 0]
    B --> C{Operation Type}

    C -->|Insert x| D[Compute k Hashes h1 x to hk x]
    D --> E[Set B h1 x to 1]
    E --> F[Set B h2 x to 1]
    F --> G[Set B hk x to 1]
    G --> H[Insertion Complete]

    C -->|Query y| I[Compute k Hashes h1 y to hk y]
    I --> J[Check B h1 y]
    J --> K[Check B h2 y]
    K --> L[Check B hk y]
    L --> M{All k Bits Equal 1?}

    M -->|No| N[Return Definitely NOT in Set]
    M -->|Yes| O[Return Probably in Set]
    O --> P{Member Verification via Backend}
    P -->|Verified| Q[Return True Positive]
    P -->|Not Found| R[Return False Positive]
```

## 4.2 False Positive Probability Surface ŌĆö Parameter Relationship Map

The diagram below maps the relationship between $k$, $m/n$, and the false positive rate $f$, helping visualize the optimal design region.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bit Array Size m] -->|m increases| B[More Memory Used]
    C[Hash Functions k] -->|k increases| D[More Computation per Query]
    A -->|larger m| E[Lower False Positive f]
    C -->|too few k| F[Under-hashed, f high]
    C -->|too many k| G[Over-hashed, bit array saturates, f rises]
    F --> H[Optimal k at m over n times ln 2]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Minimum False Positive f equals 2 power minus k]
```

## 4.3 Insertion and Query Dataflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INSERT["Insertion Path"]
        I1[Input Element x] --> I2[Apply Hash Function 1]
        I2 --> I3[Apply Hash Function 2]
        I3 --> I4[Apply Hash Function k]
        I4 --> I5[Compute k Bit Positions]
        I5 --> I6[Flip Each Bit to 1]
    end

    subgraph QUERY["Query Path"]
        Q1[Input Element y] --> Q2[Apply Same k Hashes]
        Q2 --> Q3[Read k Bit Positions]
        Q3 --> Q4{Bitwise AND of all k bits}
        Q4 -->|All 1| Q5[Return Possibly Present]
        Q4 -->|Any 0| Q6[Return Definitely Absent]
    end

    I6 -.->|Updates shared state| Q3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]

**Q: Define a Bloom Filter. Explain why it can produce false positives but never false negatives.**

**Model Answer:**

A Bloom Filter is a space-efficient randomized probabilistic data structure used for set membership testing. It consists of a bit array $B$ of size $m$ and a family of $k$ independent uniform hash functions $h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_k: U \to \{0, 1, \ldots, m-1\}$.

**Insertion:** For element $x$, compute all $k$ hash positions and set $B[h_i(x)] = 1$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$.

**Query:** For element $y$, check whether $B[h_i(y)] = 1$ for all $i$. If yes, return "probably present"; otherwise return "definitely absent."

**False positives** occur because different elements can hash to overlapping bit positions. A non-member element $y$ may, by coincidence, have all $k$ of its hash positions already set to 1 by previously inserted elements, falsely indicating membership.

**No false negatives** are possible because bits are **monotonically non-decreasing** ŌĆö once set to 1, a bit never reverts to 0. Therefore, if $x$ was inserted, all $k$ positions $h_i(x)$ are guaranteed to be 1, ensuring any future query for $x$ will return "probably present."

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]

**Q: State and explain the formula for the false positive probability of a Bloom Filter.**

**Model Answer:**

The false positive probability of a Bloom Filter with bit array size $m$, $k$ hash functions, and $n$ inserted elements is given by:

$$f = \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^{k}$$

**Derivation outline:** The probability that a specific bit remains 0 after all insertions is $p_{\text{zero}} = (1 - 1/m)^{kn} \approx e^{-kn/m}$. Therefore, the probability a bit is 1 is $1 - e^{-kn/m}$. A false positive requires all $k$ hashed bit positions to be 1, yielding the formula above.

For a fixed $m/n$ ratio, the minimum $f$ is achieved at $k_{\text{opt}} = (m/n)\ln 2$, giving:

$$f_{\min} = 2^{-k_{\text{opt}}} = 2^{-(m/n)\ln 2}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Descriptive Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Q: Derive the optimal number of hash functions $k_{\text{opt}}$ for a Bloom Filter that minimizes the false positive probability for a given bit array size $m$ and $n$ inserted elements.**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the false positive formula:

$$f(k) = \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^{k}$$

To minimize, take the natural log:

$$g(k) = \ln f(k) = k \ln\left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)$$

Differentiate using the product rule:

$$\frac{dg}{dk} = \ln\left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right) + \frac{k \cdot (n/m) \cdot e^{-kn/m}}{1 - e^{-kn/m}}$$

Set $\frac{dg}{dk} = 0$ and substitute $u = e^{-kn/m}$:

$$\ln(1-u) = -\frac{ku}{1-u}$$

Testing $u = 1/2$:

- LHS: $\ln(1/2) = -\ln 2$
- RHS: $-\frac{k(1/2)}{1/2} = -k$

Thus $k = \ln 2$. The condition $u = 1/2$ gives:

$$e^{-kn/m} = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{kn}{m} = \ln 2$$

Therefore:

$$k_{\text{opt}} = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \cdot \frac{m}{n}$$

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Q: A Bloom Filter is designed with $m = 4800$ bits, $n = 1000$ elements, and $k = 4$ hash functions. Compute the false positive rate. If the target false positive rate is 0.001, what is the minimum $m$ required?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the false positive rate for the given parameters**

$$\frac{kn}{m} = \frac{4 \times 1000}{4800} = \frac{4000}{4800} = 0.8333$$

$$e^{-0.8333} \approx 0.4346$$

$$p_{\text{one}} = 1 - 0.4346 = 0.5654$$

$$f = (0.5654)^{4} \approx 0.1023$$

So the current false positive rate is approximately **10.23%**.

**Step 2: Compute the minimum $m$ for $f = 0.001$**

Using $m_{\text{opt}} = -\dfrac{n \ln f}{(\ln 2)^{2}}$:

$$m_{\text{opt}} = -\frac{1000 \cdot \ln(0.001)}{(\ln 2)^{2}} = -\frac{1000 \cdot (-6.9078)}{0.4805} = \frac{6907.8}{0.4805} \approx 14376$$

Therefore, the minimum bit array size required is approximately **$m \approx 14376$ bits**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Students often confuse $\ln$ (natural log) with $\log_{10}$ when applying the formula $m_{\text{opt}} = -n \ln f / (\ln 2)^2$. Use natural log throughout; this single error costs 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> - **Pitfall 2:** The formula for $f$ requires $kn/m$ to be computed as a *pure dimensionless ratio* before exponentiation. Mixing units (e.g., using $m$ in bytes instead of bits) gives incorrect $f$.
> - **Pitfall 3:** When $k$ is *not* optimal, do not apply the simplified $f_{\min} = 2^{-k}$ formula. Use the full expression $f = (1 - e^{-kn/m})^k$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Q: Compare Bloom Filters with Hash Tables in terms of space, query semantics, and operational complexity. When would you prefer a Bloom Filter over a Hash Table?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Bloom Filter | Hash Table |
|---------|--------------|------------|
| Space | $O(m)$ bits, $m$ much smaller than storing elements | $O(n)$ with overhead for keys and values |
| False positives | Yes (probabilistic) | No (deterministic) |
| False negatives | Never | No |
| Deletion | Not supported in standard version | Fully supported |
| Storing actual values | No | Yes |
| Query time | $O(k)$ | $O(1)$ average |
| Memory per element | ~10 bits for $f = 1\%$ | ~100s of bits (with pointer overhead) |

**When to prefer a Bloom Filter:**
1. When the set is too large to fit in main memory.
2. When a small false positive rate is acceptable.
3. When only membership testing is needed (no value retrieval).
4. In distributed systems where network round-trips are expensive (e.g., Bigtable, HBase).

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Q: For a Bloom Filter with $n = 10000$ elements and a target false positive rate of $f = 0.01$, compute (i) the optimal bit array size $m$, (ii) the optimal number of hash functions $k$, and (iii) the minimum false positive probability.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step (i): Compute optimal $m$**

$$m_{\text{opt}} = -\frac{n \ln f}{(\ln 2)^{2}} = -\frac{10000 \cdot \ln(0.01)}{0.4805} = -\frac{10000 \cdot (-4.6052)}{0.4805} = \frac{46052}{0.4805} \approx 95853$$

So $m \approx 95853$ bits $\approx 11.7$ KB.

**Step (ii): Compute optimal $k$**

$$k_{\text{opt}} = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2 = \frac{95853}{10000} \times 0.6931 \approx 9.585 \times 0.6931 \approx 6.64$$

Rounding to the nearest integer, $k = 7$ hash functions.

**Step (iii): Compute minimum false positive probability**

$$f_{\min} = 2^{-k_{\text{opt}}} = 2^{-6.64} \approx 0.01$$

This confirms the design ŌĆö the chosen $m$ and $k$ achieve the target $f = 0.01$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Bloom Filters:**

- **Definition:** Space-efficient probabilistic data structure for set membership queries.
- **Components:** Bit array of size $m$, $k$ hash functions, $n$ elements.
- **Insertion:** Set $k$ bit positions to 1 (monotonic; bits never revert to 0).
- **Query:** Check all $k$ bit positions ŌĆö all 1 means "probably present"; any 0 means "definitely absent."
- **Zero false negatives** is the *defining* mathematical property.
- **Master equation:** $f = (1 - e^{-kn/m})^{k}$
- **Optimal $k$:** $k_{\text{opt}} = (m/n)\ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \cdot m/n$
- **Optimal $m$:** $m_{\text{opt}} = -n \ln f / (\ln 2)^{2}$
- **Minimum false positive:** $f_{\min} = 2^{-k_{\text{opt}}}$
- **Time complexity:** $O(k)$ for both insert and query operations.
- **Bits per element:** $m/n = -1.44 \log_2 f$ (e.g., $\approx 10$ bits for $f = 1\%$).
- **No native deletion** ŌĆö variants like Counting Bloom Filters address this.
- **Engineering applications:** Bigtable, HBase, Bitcoin SPV, cache filtering, network security.
- **Remember:** Use **natural log** ($\ln$) in all formulas; $\log_2 f$ appears only after conversion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Markov Chains and Random Walks: Introduction to Markov Chains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST639 - RANDOMIZED ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Markov Chains and Random Walks: Introduction to Markov Chains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 ŌĆö Introduction to Markov Chains

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Markov Chain** is a discrete-time stochastic process $\{X_0, X_1, X_2, \dots\}$ defined over a countable (usually finite) **state space** $S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n\}$ that satisfies the **Markov Property** (also called the *memoryless property*):

$$P(X_{t+1} = s_j \mid X_t = s_i, X_{t-1} = s_{i_1}, \dots, X_0 = s_{i_t}) = P(X_{t+1} = s_j \mid X_t = s_i)$$

This equation states that the conditional probability of transitioning to state $s_j$ at time $t+1$, given the **entire history** of past states, depends **only** on the present state $s_i$. The future is conditionally independent of the past given the present.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight ŌĆö PECST639 / Module 2**
> The Markov Property is the *single most important* definition in this module. Board questions frequently test: *"State and explain the Markov property"* or *"Why is a process called memoryless?"* ŌĆö always quote both the conditional-probability equation and the verbal statement in your answer.

The transition probability from state $s_i$ to state $s_j$ at time $t$ is denoted:

$$P_{ij}^{(t)} = P(X_{t+1} = s_j \mid X_t = s_i)$$

For a **time-homogeneous** Markov chain, $P_{ij}^{(t)} = P_{ij}$ is independent of $t$, and the chain is fully described by its **transition probability matrix** $\mathbf{P} = [P_{ij}]$ of size $\vert S \vert \times \vert S \vert$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Block (KTU Examiner's Vocabulary)**
> - **State Space $S$** ŌĆö The set of all possible values the random process can take. In KTU standard problems, $\vert S \vert \le 5$.
> - **Transition Probability $P_{ij}$** ŌĆö Probability of going from state $i$ to state $j$ in one step.
> - **Stochastic Matrix** ŌĆö A square matrix where every row sums to 1.
> - **Initial Distribution** ŌĆö The vector $\pi^{(0)} = (\pi_1^{(0)}, \pi_2^{(0)}, \dots, \pi_n^{(0)})$ where $\pi_i^{(0)} = P(X_0 = s_i)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Weather Frog

Imagine a frog living on three lily pads labeled **Sunny**, **Cloudy**, and **Rainy**. Every morning the frog hops to a pad based only on *today's* pad ŌĆö it has no memory of where it was yesterday or last week. The frog is "memoryless."

- If today is **Sunny**, tomorrow there is a **70%** chance it stays **Sunny**, **20%** chance it becomes **Cloudy**, and **10%** chance it rains.
- If today is **Rainy**, tomorrow it is almost certainly going to be **Cloudy** (**80%**) or **Rainy** again (**15%**), with only a **5%** chance of sudden sunshine.

The frog's *tomorrow* depends **only** on *today*. This is the **Markov Property**. Mathematically, this is exactly a 3-state Markov chain with transition matrix:

$$\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.70 & 0.20 & 0.10 \\ 0.30 & 0.50 & 0.20 \\ 0.05 & 0.80 & 0.15 \end{bmatrix}$$

The frog's weather sequence `Sunny ŌåÆ Sunny ŌåÆ Cloudy ŌåÆ Rainy ŌåÆ Cloudy ŌåÆ ...` is the random process, and the frog itself is the Markov chain.

### 1.3 Classification of Markov Chains (KTU High-Yield)

| Classification Type | Definition | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Discrete-Time vs Continuous-Time** | Time index $t \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ (discrete) vs $t \in \mathbb{R}^+$ (continuous) | Coin toss game (discrete); radioactive decay (continuous) |
| **Finite vs Infinite State Space** | $\vert S \vert < \infty$ vs $\vert S \vert = \infty$ | Board game squares (finite); Random walk on $\mathbb{Z}$ (infinite) |
| **Time-Homogeneous vs Non-Homogeneous** | $P_{ij}^{(t)} = P_{ij}$ for all $t$ vs depends on $t$ | Weather model (homogeneous); Seasonal weather (non-homogeneous) |
| **Irreducible** | Every state reachable from every other state | Fully connected transition graph |
| **Periodic** | $\gcd\{n : (P^n)_{ii} > 0\} > 1$ for some state $i$ | Two-state oscillation |
| **Aperiodic** | $\gcd = 1$ | Generic random walk |
| **Ergodic** | Irreducible **AND** aperiodic | Most real-world chains |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** For Module 2 introductory questions, focus on **discrete-time, finite-state, time-homogeneous** chains. Continuous-time and infinite-state chains belong to Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Markov Property ŌĆö Why and How

The Markov property is *not* a vague philosophical idea; it is a precise probabilistic independence condition that has two equivalent formulations:

**Formulation 1 ŌĆö Conditional Independence (Most Common in KTU Papers):**

$$P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i, X_{n-1} = i_{n-1}, \dots, X_0 = i_0) = P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i)$$

The future $X_{n+1}$ is **conditionally independent** of the past $(X_0, \dots, X_{n-1})$ given the present $X_n$.

**Formulation 2 ŌĆö Pairwise Markov Property:**

$$P(X_{n+1} = j, X_{n-1} = k \mid X_n = i) = P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i) \cdot P(X_{n-1} = k \mid X_n = i)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility ŌĆö Why Should a B.Tech Student Care?**
> Markov chains are the backbone of **PageRank (Google)**, **MCMC sampling (AI/ML)**, **Queueing theory (network engineering)**, **Speech recognition (HMMs)**, and **Randomized algorithm analysis (Las Vegas / Monte Carlo)** ŌĆö which is the very subject of this course PECST639.

## 2.2 The Transition Probability Matrix (Stochastic Matrix)

For a finite state space $S = \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$, the **one-step transition matrix** is:

$$\mathbf{P} = [P_{ij}]_{n \times n}, \quad \text{where } P_{ij} = P(X_{t+1} = j \mid X_t = i)$$

This matrix must satisfy **two fundamental properties** for all $i, j \in S$:

**Property 1 ŌĆö Non-negativity:**

$$P_{ij} \geq 0 \quad \forall i, j \in S$$

**Property 2 ŌĆö Row-Stochasticity:**

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} P_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall i \in S$$

These two together make $\mathbf{P}$ a **right-stochastic matrix**.

## 2.3 n-Step Transition Probabilities

The probability of going from state $i$ to state $j$ in exactly $n$ steps is denoted:

$$P_{ij}^{(n)} = P(X_{t+n} = j \mid X_t = i)$$

This is the $(i, j)$-th entry of the matrix $\mathbf{P}^n$. The proof follows from the **law of total probability** and the Markov property (shown in ┬¦3).

## 2.4 State Distribution After n Steps

If the initial distribution is the row vector $\pi^{(0)} = (\pi_1^{(0)}, \pi_2^{(0)}, \dots, \pi_n^{(0)})$, then the distribution after one step is:

$$\pi^{(1)} = \pi^{(0)} \mathbf{P}$$

And in general:

$$\pi^{(n)} = \pi^{(0)} \mathbf{P}^n$$

This is a **left-stochastic multiplication** ŌĆö the distribution vector moves leftward through powers of $\mathbf{P}$.

## 2.5 ChapmanŌĆōKolmogorov Equation (KTU Star Formula ŌŁÉ)

For any $0 \le m < n < t$:

$$P_{ij}^{(t)} = \sum_{k \in S} P_{ik}^{(m)} \cdot P_{kj}^{(t-m)}$$

In matrix form:

$$\mathbf{P}^{(t)} = \mathbf{P}^{(m)} \cdot \mathbf{P}^{(t-m)}$$

A direct corollary for $t = m + n$:

$$\mathbf{P}^{(m+n)} = \mathbf{P}^{(m)} \cdot \mathbf{P}^{(n)} = \mathbf{P}^{m+n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked *"What is the probability of being in state $j$ after exactly 4 steps starting from state $i$?"* ŌĆö answer using $\pi^{(0)} \mathbf{P}^4$ or $(\mathbf{P}^4)_{ij}$, **not** $\mathbf{P}^4 \pi^{(0)}$.

## 2.6 State Classification ŌĆö Quick Reference

A state $j$ is **reachable** from state $i$ (written $i \to j$) if $(P^n)_{ij} > 0$ for some $n \geq 1$.

| Concept | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Communicating states** ($i \leftrightarrow j$) | $i \to j$ AND $j \to i$ |
| **Closed class** | A set $C$ such that no state outside $C$ is reachable from any state inside $C$ |
| **Irreducible chain** | The whole state space $S$ is one single communicating class |
| **Transient state** | Probability of ever returning is $< 1$ |
| **Recurrent state** | Probability of ever returning is $= 1$ |
| **Absorbing state** | $P_{ii} = 1$, i.e., once entered, never left |
| **Stationary distribution $\pi$** | $\pi \mathbf{P} = \pi$ with $\sum_i \pi_i = 1$ |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save This Table ŌĆö Covers 90% of Module 2 Numerical Questions**

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Markov Property | $P(X_{n+1}=j \mid X_n=i, X_{n-1}=\dots) = P(X_{n+1}=j \mid X_n=i)$ | Memorylessness |
| 2 | Transition Probability | $P_{ij} = P(X_{t+1}=j \mid X_t=i)$ | $0 \le P_{ij} \le 1$ |
| 3 | Row-Stochastic | $\sum_{j} P_{ij} = 1$ | Holds for every row |
| 4 | n-Step Transition | $P_{ij}^{(n)} = (\mathbf{P}^n)_{ij}$ | Matrix power |
| 5 | Distribution Evolution | $\pi^{(n)} = \pi^{(0)} \mathbf{P}^n$ | Row vector left-multiplied |
| 6 | ChapmanŌĆōKolmogorov | $\mathbf{P}^{(m+n)} = \mathbf{P}^{(m)} \mathbf{P}^{(n)}$ | Equivalent to $\mathbf{P}^{m+n}$ |
| 7 | First-Passage (intro) | $f_{ij}^{(n)} = P(T_j = n \mid X_0 = i)$ | Detailed in Module 3 |
| 8 | Stationary Distribution | $\pi \mathbf{P} = \pi$, $\sum \pi_i = 1$ | Exists iff chain is ergodic |
| 9 | Period of state $i$ | $\gcd\{n \ge 1 : (P^n)_{ii} > 0\}$ | Aperiodic $\Leftrightarrow$ period = 1 |
| 10 | Hitting Probability | $h_{ij} = P(\text{ever reach } j \mid X_0 = i)$ | $h_{ij} = \sum_k P_{ik} h_{kj}$ |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the n-Step Transition Probability Formula

**Theorem.** For a time-homogeneous Markov chain, the probability of being in state $j$ after exactly $n$ steps starting from state $i$ is given by:

$$P_{ij}^{(n)} = (\mathbf{P}^n)_{ij}$$

**Proof by Induction on $n$.**

**Base Case ($n = 1$):** By definition,
$$P_{ij}^{(1)} = P(X_1 = j \mid X_0 = i) = (\mathbf{P})_{ij}$$
since $\mathbf{P}^1 = \mathbf{P}$. The base case holds trivially.

**Inductive Hypothesis:** Assume $P_{ij}^{(n)} = (\mathbf{P}^n)_{ij}$ for some $n \ge 1$.

**Inductive Step:** We compute $P_{ij}^{(n+1)}$ using the law of total probability, conditioning on the state at time $n$:

$$P_{ij}^{(n+1)} = P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_0 = i) = \sum_{k \in S} P(X_{n+1} = j, X_n = k \mid X_0 = i)$$

$$= \sum_{k \in S} P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = k, X_0 = i) \cdot P(X_n = k \mid X_0 = i)$$

By the **Markov property**, the future depends only on the present, so:
$$P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = k, X_0 = i) = P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = k) = P_{kj}$$

Therefore:
$$P_{ij}^{(n+1)} = \sum_{k \in S} P_{kj} \cdot P_{ik}^{(n)}$$

Applying the inductive hypothesis $P_{ik}^{(n)} = (\mathbf{P}^n)_{ik}$:
$$P_{ij}^{(n+1)} = \sum_{k \in S} (\mathbf{P}^n)_{ik} \cdot P_{kj} = \sum_{k \in S} (\mathbf{P}^n)_{ik} \cdot (\mathbf{P})_{kj} = (\mathbf{P}^n \cdot \mathbf{P})_{ij} = (\mathbf{P}^{n+1})_{ij}$$

This completes the induction. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Example ŌĆö Three-State Weather Chain

**Problem:** Given the transition matrix
$$\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.2 & 0.1 \\ 0.3 & 0.5 & 0.2 \\ 0.05 & 0.8 & 0.15 \end{bmatrix}$$
where states 1, 2, 3 represent **Sunny**, **Cloudy**, **Rainy**. If today (Day 0) is Sunny with probability 1, find the probability it will be Rainy on Day 2.

**Solution:**

The initial distribution is $\pi^{(0)} = (1, 0, 0)$.

**Step 1:** Compute $\mathbf{P}^2$. For the entry $(\mathbf{P}^2)_{1,3}$ (Sunny $\to$ Rainy in 2 steps):
$$(\mathbf{P}^2)_{1,3} = \sum_{k=1}^{3} P_{1k} \cdot P_{k3} = P_{11} P_{13} + P_{12} P_{23} + P_{13} P_{33}$$

$$= (0.7)(0.1) + (0.2)(0.2) + (0.1)(0.15)$$

$$= 0.07 + 0.04 + 0.015 = 0.125$$

**Step 2:** Using the distribution formula:
$$\pi^{(2)} = \pi^{(0)} \mathbf{P}^2 = (1, 0, 0) \mathbf{P}^2$$

The third component is exactly $(\mathbf{P}^2)_{1,3} = 0.125$.

**Answer:** $P(\text{Rainy on Day 2}) = \boxed{0.125}$

## 3.3 Derivation of the ChapmanŌĆōKolmogorov Equation

**Theorem.** For any integers $m, n \ge 1$ and states $i, j \in S$:

$$P_{ij}^{(m+n)} = \sum_{k \in S} P_{ik}^{(m)} P_{kj}^{(n)}$$

**Proof.** Condition on the state $X_m = k$ at the intermediate time step:

$$P_{ij}^{(m+n)} = P(X_{m+n} = j \mid X_0 = i) = \sum_{k \in S} P(X_{m+n} = j, X_m = k \mid X_0 = i)$$

$$= \sum_{k \in S} P(X_{m+n} = j \mid X_m = k, X_0 = i) \cdot P(X_m = k \mid X_0 = i)$$

By the **Markov property**, conditioning on $X_0 = i$ becomes irrelevant once we know $X_m = k$:
$$P(X_{m+n} = j \mid X_m = k, X_0 = i) = P(X_{m+n} = j \mid X_m = k) = P_{kj}^{(n)}$$

Therefore:
$$P_{ij}^{(m+n)} = \sum_{k \in S} P_{kj}^{(n)} P_{ik}^{(m)} = \sum_{k \in S} P_{ik}^{(m)} P_{kj}^{(n)}$$

In matrix form, this is exactly $\mathbf{P}^{m+n} = \mathbf{P}^m \mathbf{P}^n$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Worked Example ŌĆö Computing Stationary Distribution (2-State Chain)

**Problem:** A two-state chain has $\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}$. Find the stationary distribution $\pi = (\pi_1, \pi_2)$.

**Solution:** We solve the linear system $\pi \mathbf{P} = \pi$ subject to $\pi_1 + \pi_2 = 1$.

**Equation 1:** $\pi_1 = 0.6 \pi_1 + 0.3 \pi_2$
**Equation 2:** $\pi_2 = 0.4 \pi_1 + 0.7 \pi_2$
**Equation 3:** $\pi_1 + \pi_2 = 1$

From Equation 1:
$$\pi_1 - 0.6 \pi_1 = 0.3 \pi_2 \implies 0.4 \pi_1 = 0.3 \pi_2 \implies \pi_2 = \frac{0.4}{0.3} \pi_1 = \frac{4}{3} \pi_1$$

Substituting into Equation 3:
$$\pi_1 + \frac{4}{3} \pi_1 = 1 \implies \frac{7}{3} \pi_1 = 1 \implies \pi_1 = \frac{3}{7}$$

Therefore $\pi_2 = \frac{4}{7}$.

**Answer:** $\pi = \left(\dfrac{3}{7}, \dfrac{4}{7}\right)$

**Verification:** $\pi \mathbf{P} = \left(\frac{3}{7}, \frac{4}{7}\right) \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix} = \left(\frac{1.8 + 1.2}{7}, \frac{1.2 + 2.8}{7}\right) = \left(\frac{3}{7}, \frac{4}{7}\right) = \pi$ Ō£ō

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Markov Chain Computation Pipeline

The following block diagram illustrates the **end-to-end computational flow** when analyzing a Markov chain, from input specification to long-run behavior.

```mermaid
graph TD
    inpA[INPUT State Space S]
    inpB[INPUT Transition Matrix P]
    inpC[INPUT Initial Distribution pi0]

    valA[VALIDATE Row sums equal 1]
    valB[VALIDATE All Pij between 0 and 1]
    valC[VALIDATE pi0 sums to 1]

    powA[COMPUTE P to the power n]
    powB[COMPUTE pi0 times P to the power n]

    outA[OUTPUT n-step transition prob]
    outB[OUTPUT Distribution at time n]
    outC[OUTPUT Stationary Distribution]
    outD[OUTPUT State Classification]

    inpA --> valA
    inpB --> valA
    inpB --> valB
    inpC --> valC

    valA --> powA
    valB --> powA
    valA --> powB
    inpC --> powB

    powA --> outA
    powB --> outB
    powA --> outC
    inpB --> outD

    style inpA fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC
    style inpB fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC
    style inpC fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC
    style valA fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600
    style valB fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600
    style valC fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600
    style powA fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#009900
    style powB fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#009900

```

**Pipeline Stages Explained:**

| Stage | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **INPUT (Blue)** | User supplies the three primary objects of a Markov chain: state space, transition matrix, initial distribution |
| **VALIDATE (Orange)** | Sanity checks: row-stochasticity, non-negativity, distribution normalization |
| **COMPUTE (Green)** | Matrix powers and matrixŌĆōvector products yield all derived quantities |
| **OUTPUT (Pink)** | Final answers for KTU problems: $n$-step probabilities, evolution of $\pi$, stationary distribution, state classes |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
**State and explain the Markov property. Why is it called the "memoryless" property?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Markov property states that for any sequence of states and any time $n$:

$$P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i, X_{n-1} = i_{n-1}, \dots, X_0 = i_0) = P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i)$$

In words, the conditional probability of the next state $X_{n+1}$ depends only on the **current** state $X_n$, and is **independent** of the entire past history $(X_0, X_1, \dots, X_{n-1})$ given $X_n$.

It is called *memoryless* because the process "forgets" its history ŌĆö once the present state is known, knowledge of all previous states provides no additional information about the future. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**What is a stochastic matrix? Mention the two essential properties it must satisfy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **stochastic matrix** (or transition probability matrix) is a square matrix $\mathbf{P} = [P_{ij}]_{n \times n}$ whose entries are the one-step transition probabilities of a Markov chain.

The two essential properties are:

1. **Non-negativity:** $P_{ij} \geq 0$ for all $i, j \in S$.  **(1 mark)**

2. **Row-stochasticity:** $\sum_{j=1}^{n} P_{ij} = 1$ for every row $i$. This reflects the fact that from any state $i$, the chain must transition to *some* state with total probability 1.  **(2 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define a Markov chain. For the transition matrix given below, verify that it is a valid stochastic matrix and compute the probability of moving from state 2 to state 3 in exactly 2 steps. **(7 marks)**

$$\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.5 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.4 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.6 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(b)** Starting with initial distribution $\pi^{(0)} = (0.5, 0.3, 0.2)$, compute the distribution after 2 steps, $\pi^{(2)}$, using matrix multiplication. Identify whether the chain is periodic or aperiodic and justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Definition (2 marks):** A Markov chain is a discrete-time stochastic process $\{X_n\}_{n \ge 0}$ on a state space $S$ satisfying the Markov property: future state depends only on the present state, not on the past.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Verify stochasticity (2 marks):**
- All entries $\geq 0$ Ō£ō
- Row 1: $0.2 + 0.5 + 0.3 = 1.0$ Ō£ō
- Row 2: $0.4 + 0.4 + 0.2 = 1.0$ Ō£ō
- Row 3: $0.1 + 0.6 + 0.3 = 1.0$ Ō£ō

The matrix is a valid stochastic matrix.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $P_{23}^{(2)} = (\mathbf{P}^2)_{2,3}$ (3 marks):**

$$(\mathbf{P}^2)_{2,3} = P_{21} P_{13} + P_{22} P_{23} + P_{23} P_{33}$$

$$= (0.4)(0.3) + (0.4)(0.2) + (0.2)(0.3)$$

$$= 0.12 + 0.08 + 0.06 = 0.26$$

**Answer:** $P_{23}^{(2)} = \boxed{0.26}$

**(b) Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $\mathbf{P}^2$ entirely (3 marks):**

Row 1 of $\mathbf{P}^2$:
- $(\mathbf{P}^2)_{11} = (0.2)(0.2) + (0.5)(0.4) + (0.3)(0.1) = 0.04 + 0.20 + 0.03 = 0.27$
- $(\mathbf{P}^2)_{12} = (0.2)(0.5) + (0.5)(0.4) + (0.3)(0.6) = 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.18 = 0.48$
- $(\mathbf{P}^2)_{13} = (0.2)(0.3) + (0.5)(0.2) + (0.3)(0.3) = 0.06 + 0.10 + 0.09 = 0.25$

Row 2 of $\mathbf{P}^2$: already computed key entries; continuing ŌĆö $(\mathbf{P}^2)_{21} = (0.4)(0.2) + (0.4)(0.4) + (0.2)(0.1) = 0.08 + 0.16 + 0.02 = 0.26$. By row-sum = 1, $(\mathbf{P}^2)_{22} = 1 - 0.26 - 0.26 = 0.48$.

Row 3 of $\mathbf{P}^2$: $(\mathbf{P}^2)_{31} = (0.1)(0.2) + (0.6)(0.4) + (0.3)(0.1) = 0.02 + 0.24 + 0.03 = 0.29$. By row-sum = 1, $(\mathbf{P}^2)_{32} = 1 - 0.29 - 0.27 = 0.44$.

Thus:
$$\mathbf{P}^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.27 & 0.48 & 0.25 \\ 0.26 & 0.48 & 0.26 \\ 0.29 & 0.44 & 0.27 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $\pi^{(2)} = \pi^{(0)} \mathbf{P}^2$ (2 marks):**

$$\pi^{(2)} = (0.5, 0.3, 0.2) \begin{bmatrix} 0.27 & 0.48 & 0.25 \\ 0.26 & 0.48 & 0.26 \\ 0.29 & 0.44 & 0.27 \end{bmatrix}$$

- $\pi_1^{(2)} = 0.5(0.27) + 0.3(0.26) + 0.2(0.29) = 0.135 + 0.078 + 0.058 = 0.271$
- $\pi_2^{(2)} = 0.5(0.48) + 0.3(0.48) + 0.2(0.44) = 0.240 + 0.144 + 0.088 = 0.472$
- $\pi_3^{(2)} = 0.5(0.25) + 0.3(0.26) + 0.2(0.27) = 0.125 + 0.078 + 0.054 = 0.257$

(Check: $0.271 + 0.472 + 0.257 = 1.000$ Ō£ō)

**Answer:** $\pi^{(2)} = (0.271,\ 0.472,\ 0.257)$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Periodicity (2 marks):** The diagonal entries of $\mathbf{P}^2$ are all strictly positive ($0.27, 0.48, 0.27$). Since $P_{ii}^{(2)} > 0$ for all $i$, the period of every state divides 2. Also $P_{11} = 0.2 > 0$, so the period divides 1. By $\gcd(1, 2) = 1$, the chain is **aperiodic**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** A two-state Markov chain has transition matrix $\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}$. Compute $\mathbf{P}^3$ and hence find $(\mathbf{P}^3)_{1,2}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define a stationary distribution. Solve $\pi \mathbf{P} = \pi$ with $\pi_1 + \pi_2 = 1$ for this chain. Verify your answer by computing $\pi \mathbf{P}^3$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $\mathbf{P}^2$ (3 marks):**
$$\mathbf{P}^2 = \mathbf{P} \cdot \mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}$$

- $(\mathbf{P}^2)_{11} = (0.6)(0.6) + (0.4)(0.3) = 0.36 + 0.12 = 0.48$
- $(\mathbf{P}^2)_{12} = (0.6)(0.4) + (0.4)(0.7) = 0.24 + 0.28 = 0.52$
- $(\mathbf{P}^2)_{21} = (0.3)(0.6) + (0.7)(0.3) = 0.18 + 0.21 = 0.39$
- $(\mathbf{P}^2)_{22} = (0.3)(0.4) + (0.7)(0.7) = 0.12 + 0.49 = 0.61$

$$\mathbf{P}^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.48 & 0.52 \\ 0.39 & 0.61 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $\mathbf{P}^3 = \mathbf{P}^2 \cdot \mathbf{P}$ (3 marks):**

- $(\mathbf{P}^3)_{11} = (0.48)(0.6) + (0.52)(0.3) = 0.288 + 0.156 = 0.444$
- $(\mathbf{P}^3)_{12} = (0.48)(0.4) + (0.52)(0.7) = 0.192 + 0.364 = 0.556$
- $(\mathbf{P}^3)_{21} = (0.39)(0.6) + (0.61)(0.3) = 0.234 + 0.183 = 0.417$
- $(\mathbf{P}^3)_{22} = (0.39)(0.4) + (0.61)(0.7) = 0.156 + 0.427 = 0.583$

$$\mathbf{P}^3 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.444 & 0.556 \\ 0.417 & 0.583 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Answer:** $(\mathbf{P}^3)_{1,2} = \boxed{0.556}$

**(b) Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Definition of stationary distribution (2 marks):** A stationary distribution $\pi = (\pi_1, \pi_2, \dots, \pi_n)$ is a probability row vector satisfying $\pi \mathbf{P} = \pi$ and $\sum_i \pi_i = 1$. It represents the long-run fraction of time the chain spends in each state.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Set up the system (2 marks):**
$$\pi_1 = 0.6 \pi_1 + 0.3 \pi_2$$
$$\pi_2 = 0.4 \pi_1 + 0.7 \pi_2$$
$$\pi_1 + \pi_2 = 1$$

From the first equation: $0.4 \pi_1 = 0.3 \pi_2 \implies \pi_2 = \frac{4}{3} \pi_1$.

Substituting: $\pi_1 + \frac{4}{3} \pi_1 = 1 \implies \frac{7}{3} \pi_1 = 1 \implies \pi_1 = \frac{3}{7}, \quad \pi_2 = \frac{4}{7}$.

**Answer:** $\pi = \left(\dfrac{3}{7},\ \dfrac{4}{7}\right)$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Verification using $\pi \mathbf{P}^3$ (3 marks):**

$$\pi \mathbf{P}^3 = \left(\frac{3}{7}, \frac{4}{7}\right) \begin{bmatrix} 0.444 & 0.556 \\ 0.417 & 0.583 \end{bmatrix}$$

First component:
$$\frac{3}{7}(0.444) + \frac{4}{7}(0.417) = \frac{1.332 + 1.668}{7} = \frac{3.000}{7} = \frac{3}{7} \checkmark$$

Second component:
$$\frac{3}{7}(0.556) + \frac{4}{7}(0.583) = \frac{1.668 + 2.332}{7} = \frac{4.000}{7} = \frac{4}{7} \checkmark$$

Hence $\pi \mathbf{P}^3 = \pi$, confirming $\pi$ is indeed the stationary distribution. As $n \to \infty$, $\mathbf{P}^n$ converges to a matrix with both rows equal to $\pi$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Mistakes (Penalty Marks Lost Here)**
> 1. **Row sums vs column sums confusion:** A stochastic matrix has **row sums = 1**, *not* column sums. Students often mistakenly verify column sums.
> 2. **Wrong multiplication order:** When finding distribution after $n$ steps, use $\pi^{(n)} = \pi^{(0)} \mathbf{P}^n$ (row vector times matrix), **not** $\mathbf{P}^n \pi^{(0)}$ (matrix times column vector). These yield **different** answers.
> 3. **Forgetting normalization:** After computing $\pi^{(n)}$, always verify $\sum_i \pi_i^{(n)} = 1$. Numerical roundoff can introduce drift; flag it explicitly in your answer.
> 4. **Confusing $P_{ij}$ with $P_{ji}$:** $P_{ij}$ is the probability of going *from* $i$ *to* $j$, not the reverse. The matrix is generally **not symmetric**.
> 5. **ChapmanŌĆōKolmogorov misuse:** Never write $\mathbf{P}^{(m+n)} = \mathbf{P}^{(m)} + \mathbf{P}^{(n)}$ ŌĆö it's a **product**, not a sum.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 2 / Introduction to Markov Chains**

### **Core Concepts (Must Memorize)**
- Ō£ģ **Markov Property** (memoryless): $P(\text{future} \mid \text{present, past}) = P(\text{future} \mid \text{present})$
- Ō£ģ **Stochastic matrix** has all entries $\geq 0$ and **row sums = 1**
- Ō£ģ **n-step transition** $P_{ij}^{(n)} = (\mathbf{P}^n)_{ij}$ ŌĆö *matrix power*, not element-wise power
- Ō£ģ **Distribution evolution:** $\pi^{(n)} = \pi^{(0)} \mathbf{P}^n$
- Ō£ģ **ChapmanŌĆōKolmogorov:** $\mathbf{P}^{m+n} = \mathbf{P}^m \mathbf{P}^n$ (matrix product)

### **Key Classifications**
- Ō£ģ **Discrete-time** vs **continuous-time**
- Ō£ģ **Finite** vs **infinite** state space
- Ō£ģ **Time-homogeneous** vs **non-homogeneous** (Module 2 focuses on homogeneous)
- Ō£ģ **Irreducible**: every state reachable from every other state
- Ō£ģ **Periodic** vs **aperiodic**: based on $\gcd$ of return times
- Ō£ģ **Ergodic** = irreducible **AND** aperiodic

### **Numerical Problem Solving Strategy**
- Ō£ģ **Step 1:** Identify state space $S$, write down $\mathbf{P}$, identify initial distribution $\pi^{(0)}$
- Ō£ģ **Step 2:** For 2-step probability ŌåÆ compute $(\mathbf{P}^2)_{ij}$ via matrix multiplication
- Ō£ģ **Step 3:** For distribution evolution ŌåÆ compute $\pi^{(n)} = \pi^{(0)} \mathbf{P}^n$
- Ō£ģ **Step 4:** For stationary distribution ŌåÆ solve $\pi \mathbf{P} = \pi$, $\sum \pi_i = 1$

### **Common Engineering Applications (For "Real-world Application" Sub-parts)**
- Ō£ģ **PageRank Algorithm** (Google Search)
- Ō£ģ **Hidden Markov Models** (Speech / DNA / NLP)
- Ō£ģ **MCMC** (Bayesian inference, AI/ML)
- Ō£ģ **Queueing Theory** (Network performance)
- Ō£ģ **Randomized Algorithm Analysis** (course PECST639 ŌĆö Las Vegas / Monte Carlo)

### **Mistakes to Avoid in KTU Exams**
- ŌØī Mixing up row vector vs column vector multiplication
- ŌØī Forgetting to verify row-stochasticity when given a matrix in a problem
- ŌØī Computing $\mathbf{P}^{(m+n)}$ as a sum instead of a product
- ŌØī Confusing periodicity with reducibility
- ŌØī Failing to mention the **memoryless** interpretation when asked about the Markov property

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Random Walks on Graphs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST639 - RANDOMIZED ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Random Walks on Graphs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Random Walk on a Graph** $G = (V, E)$ is a discrete-time stochastic process that describes a path consisting of a sequence of random vertices, where the walk starts at some initial vertex $v_0 \in V$, and at each subsequent step, the walker transitions from its current vertex to a **uniformly randomly chosen neighbor** of that vertex. Formally, if the walk is at vertex $v$ at time $t$, then the probability of moving to vertex $u$ at time $t+1$ is:

$$P_{vu} = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{\deg(v)} & \text{if } (v,u) \in E \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $\deg(v)$ is the degree of vertex $v$ in the graph $G$. The random walk is fundamentally a **time-homogeneous Markov chain** defined on the state space $V$, with transition probability matrix $P$ whose entries are given by the above rule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Random walks are the cornerstone of randomized algorithm design because they provide an elegant framework for **graph exploration**, **sampling**, and **symmetry-breaking**. The course module specifically covers: definition on undirected graphs, **hitting time**, **cover time**, **stationary distribution**, and the **electrical network analogy** relating commute times to effective resistance.

### 1.2 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Transition Matrix | $P$ | $\vert P \vert \times \vert V \vert$ matrix with $P_{vu} = 1/\deg(v)$ for edges |
| Hitting Time | $h_{ij}$ | Expected steps to first reach $j$ starting from $i$ |
| Commute Time | $C_{ij}$ | Expected steps from $i$ to $j$ and back to $i$ (i.e., $h_{ij} + h_{ji}$) |
| Cover Time | $C(G)$ | Expected steps to visit **every** vertex at least once |
| Stationary Distribution | $\pi$ | Long-run probability of being at each vertex |
| Mixing Time | $t_{mix}$ | Time to reach near-stationary distribution |
| Effective Resistance | $R_{uv}$ | Resistance between $u$ and $v$ when each edge = $1\,\Omega$ |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Drunkard's Walk

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation:** Imagine a **drunk ant** standing on a vertex of a graph drawn as a network of platforms connected by bridges. At each second, the ant is too dizzy to remember which way it came from, so it picks **one of the bridges connected to its current platform completely at random** and stumbles across. The question is: how long, on average, will it take this ant to reach a particular destination? How long to visit *every* platform at least once?

This ant is performing a **random walk**. The bridges define the graph edges, the platforms are the vertices, and the uniform choice at each step makes it a memoryless process ŌĆö precisely the Markov property.

### 1.4 Why Random Walks Matter in Algorithm Design

1. **Graph Exploration Without Memory:** Unlike DFS/BFS, the walker needs no stack or visited set.
2. **Probabilistic Guarantees:** Expected running time is well-bounded by combinatorial graph properties.
3. **Symmetry Breaking:** Randomness avoids worst-case adversary inputs.
4. **Natural Sampling Mechanism:** Long runs of the walk produce samples from the stationary distribution.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Random walks power **PageRank** (Google search ranking), **Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC)** sampling in machine learning, **bot avoidance** in web crawlers, **community detection** in social networks, and **network reliability analysis** in telecommunications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Transition Mechanism ŌĆö Step by Step

**Step 1: Initialization.** Choose a starting vertex $v_0 \in V$ (often chosen adversarially to upper-bound the worst case).

**Step 2: Look at Neighbors.** Let $N(v) = \{u \in V : (v,u) \in E\}$ denote the open neighborhood. The number of choices at vertex $v$ equals $\deg(v) = \vert N(v) \vert$.

**Step 3: Uniform Random Selection.** The walker picks a target $v_{t+1} \in N(v_t)$ such that $\Pr[v_{t+1} = u \mid v_t = v] = 1/\deg(v)$ for every $u \in N(v)$.

**Step 4: Repeat Step 2ŌĆō3** for as many steps as required by the algorithmic task.

### 2.2 The Markov Chain View

The sequence $(X_0, X_1, X_2, \ldots)$ where $X_t \in V$ is a **Markov chain** because:

$$\Pr[X_{t+1} = v_{t+1} \mid X_t = v_t, X_{t-1} = v_{t-1}, \ldots, X_0 = v_0] = \Pr[X_{t+1} = v_{t+1} \mid X_t = v_t]$$

This **memoryless property** is the *Why* behind the elegance: only the current position matters, not the path history.

### 2.3 Hitting Time ŌĆö Recurrence Relation

The expected number of steps to reach target $j$ from source $i$ satisfies the **fundamental recurrence**:

$$h_{ij} = 0 \quad \text{if } i = j$$

$$h_{ij} = 1 + \sum_{k \in N(i)} P_{ik} \cdot h_{kj} \quad \text{if } i \neq j$$

**Why this works:** The "1" accounts for the single step taken now, and we then condition on which neighbor $k$ was chosen, paying the expected remaining cost $h_{kj}$ from there.

### 2.4 Stationary Distribution of an Undirected Graph

For an **undirected, connected, non-bipartite** graph $G$, the random walk has a unique stationary distribution:

$$\pi(v) = \frac{\deg(v)}{2 \vert E \vert} = \frac{\deg(v)}{2m}$$

This is the **degree-proportional** distribution. Highly connected vertices are visited more often in the long run.

### 2.5 Cover Time ŌĆö Bounds

For any connected graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and $m$ edges:

$$2m \cdot \max_{u,v} R_{uv} \;\leq\; C(G) \;\leq\; 2m^2 \cdot \max_{u,v} R_{uv} \cdot (\ln n + \ln \ln n + \Theta(1))$$

where $R_{uv}$ is the effective resistance between $u$ and $v$ in the electrical network obtained by replacing each edge with a **1 ╬® resistor**.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| \# | Formula / Concept | Expression | When to Use |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------------|
| 1 | Transition probability | $P_{vu} = 1/\deg(v)$ | Setting up the Markov chain |
| 2 | Hitting time recurrence | $h_{ij} = 1 + \sum_{k \in N(i)} \frac{h_{kj}}{\deg(i)}$ | Computing $h_{ij}$ by solving linear system |
| 3 | Commute time identity | $C_{uv} = 2m \cdot R_{uv}$ | Converting walk to circuit problem |
| 4 | Stationary distribution | $\pi(v) = \deg(v)/(2m)$ | Long-run behavior, sampling |
| 5 | Cover time lower bound | $C(G) \geq 2m \cdot \max R_{uv}$ | Lower-bound proofs |
| 6 | Cover time upper bound | $C(G) \leq 2m^2 \cdot \max R_{uv} \cdot (\ln n + \ln \ln n + O(1))$ | Upper-bound proofs |
| 7 | Mixing time (lazy walk) | $t_{mix} = \Theta(1/\lambda_2)$ | Convergence analysis |
| 8 | Graph conductance | $\Phi(S) = \sum_{e \in \partial S} 1 / \min(\mathrm{vol}(S), \mathrm{vol}(\bar S))$ | Bottleneck detection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Pipe Replacement Note:** All vertical bars in formulas above are rendered with `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to avoid breaking the surrounding markdown tables. In actual equations, students should write $\deg(v)$ for the degree.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Web Search (PageRank)** | Random surfer model; stationary distribution = page importance |
| **Wireless Sensor Networks** | Random-walk-based data gathering to balance energy |
| **Social Network Analysis** | Community detection via short random walks |
| **Computer Networks** | Anonymous routing (Crowds protocol) |
| **Bioinformatics** | Random walk on protein interaction graphs for gene ranking |
| **Cryptography** | Walk-based hashing in zerocash/Zcash |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Hitting Time Recurrence from First Principles

**Goal:** Show that for a random walk on undirected graph $G$ with target $j$ and current vertex $i \neq j$:

$$h_{ij} = 1 + \frac{1}{\deg(i)} \sum_{k \in N(i)} h_{kj}$$

**Step-by-Step Proof:**

Let $T_{ij}$ be the random variable denoting the number of steps to reach $j$ from $i$. By the law of total expectation, conditioning on the first step:

$$h_{ij} = \mathbb{E}[T_{ij}] = \sum_{k \in N(i)} \mathbb{E}[T_{ij} \mid X_1 = k] \cdot \Pr[X_1 = k]$$

Since the walk chooses uniformly from $\deg(i)$ neighbors:

$$\Pr[X_1 = k \mid X_0 = i] = \frac{1}{\deg(i)}$$

Given the walk moves to $k$, it has already taken **one** step, and from $k$ the expected remaining steps to reach $j$ is $h_{kj}$. Therefore:

$$\mathbb{E}[T_{ij} \mid X_1 = k] = 1 + h_{kj}$$

Substituting into the law of total expectation:

$$h_{ij} = \sum_{k \in N(i)} \left(1 + h_{kj}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\deg(i)} = \frac{1}{\deg(i)} \sum_{k \in N(i)} \left(1 + h_{kj}\right)$$

Splitting the sum:

$$h_{ij} = \frac{1}{\deg(i)} \sum_{k \in N(i)} 1 \;+\; \frac{1}{\deg(i)} \sum_{k \in N(i)} h_{kj}$$

The first term simplifies to $\deg(i)/\deg(i) = 1$, giving the desired recurrence:

$$\boxed{h_{ij} = 1 + \frac{1}{\deg(i)} \sum_{k \in N(i)} h_{kj}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **system of linear equations** in $\vert V \vert - 1$ unknowns (one for each $i \neq j$), which can be solved in $O(n^3)$ by Gaussian elimination or $O(n \cdot m)$ by iterative methods.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Stationary Distribution for Undirected Graphs

**Goal:** Show $\pi(v) = \deg(v)/(2m)$ satisfies the stationarity equation $\pi P = \pi$.

**Step 1: Stationarity Condition.** A distribution $\pi$ is stationary if for every vertex $u$:

$$\pi(u) = \sum_{v \in V} \pi(v) \cdot P_{vu}$$

This says: probability mass flowing into $u$ from all neighbors $v$ must equal $\pi(u)$.

**Step 2: Substitute Candidate $\pi(v) = \deg(v)/(2m)$.** Then:

$$\sum_{v \in V} \pi(v) \cdot P_{vu} = \sum_{v \in N(u)} \frac{\deg(v)}{2m} \cdot \frac{1}{\deg(v)}$$

**Step 3: Simplify.** Each term $\deg(v)/\deg(v) = 1$, and we sum over exactly the neighbors of $u$:

$$\sum_{v \in N(u)} \frac{1}{2m} = \frac{\deg(u)}{2m} = \pi(u)$$

**Step 4: Uniqueness.** For a connected, non-bipartite undirected graph, the random walk is an **irreducible aperiodic** Markov chain, so the stationary distribution is unique. Thus the derivation is complete:

$$\boxed{\pi(v) = \frac{\deg(v)}{2 \vert E \vert}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why degree-proportional?** In a random walk, you spend more time at high-degree vertices because there are *more* outgoing edges to "trap" you there momentarily. Equivalently, a vertex with degree 10 is hit twice as often as a degree-5 vertex.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö The Commute-Time / Resistance Identity

**Goal:** Prove that for any two vertices $u, v$ in a connected undirected graph $G$:

$$C_{uv} = h_{uv} + h_{vu} = 2m \cdot R_{uv}$$

where $R_{uv}$ is the effective electrical resistance between $u$ and $v$ when each edge is a $1\,\Omega$ resistor.

**Step 1: Effective Resistance Definition.** Inject a current $I$ at $u$ and extract it at $v$. By Ohm's law, $R_{uv} = V/I$, where $V$ is the resulting potential difference. In particular, $R_{uv}$ equals the voltage drop from $u$ to $v$ when $I = 1$ ampere.

**Step 2: Define a Potential Function.** Set $V(u) = h_{uv}$ and $V(v) = 0$ (a canonical choice, since $h_{vv} = 0$). The potential at every other vertex $w$ is set to $h_{wv}$, the expected steps from $w$ to reach $v$.

**Step 3: Compute the Voltage Drop on Edge $(a, b)$.**

$$V(a) - V(b) = h_{av} - h_{bv}$$

Using the hitting time recurrence applied to vertex $a$:

$$h_{av} = 1 + \frac{1}{\deg(a)} \sum_{k \in N(a)} h_{kv}$$

**Step 4: Pair Up Neighbors.** By symmetry, $\sum_{k \in N(a)} h_{kv}$ can be regrouped to relate the sum to $\sum_{k \in N(a)} V(k)$. After algebraic manipulation (detailed in Doyle & Snell, *Random Walks and Electric Networks*), we obtain:

$$V(a) - V(b) = \frac{1}{\deg(a)} + \frac{1}{\deg(b)} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{current on edge} = \frac{V(a) - V(b)}{1\,\Omega} \text{ by Ohm's law}$$

**Step 5: Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law.** The total current entering $u$ equals the sum of currents on all edges from $u$:

$$I = \sum_{b \in N(u)} \frac{V(u) - V(b)}{1} = \sum_{b \in N(u)} (h_{uv} - h_{bv})$$

**Step 6: Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law at Every Other Vertex.** For all internal vertices $w \notin \{u, v\}$:

$$\sum_{k \in N(w)} (h_{wv} - h_{kv}) = 0$$

This is satisfied automatically because $h_{wv}$ satisfies the same recurrence (telescoping identity).

**Step 7: Compute Effective Resistance.** By definition, $R_{uv} = (V(u) - V(v))/I = h_{uv} / I$. By a similar argument, $h_{vu} = I$ as well. Adding:

$$h_{uv} + h_{vu} = 2 I \cdot R_{uv}$$

But $I$ turns out to be exactly $m$ (the number of edges) when the random-walk potentials are normalized correctly. Therefore:

$$\boxed{C_{uv} = h_{uv} + h_{vu} = 2m \cdot R_{uv}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This identity is the **most exam-relevant result** in KTU Module 2. It transforms a *combinatorial* problem (counting walk steps) into an *electrical* problem (computing resistance), which is often dramatically easier.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example ŌĆö Random Walk on a Path $P_3$

**Graph:** $V = \{1, 2, 3\}$, $E = \{(1,2), (2,3)\}$, $m = 2$.

**Step 1: Set up the recurrence for $h_{13}$ (hitting 3 from 1).** Since the only way out of 1 is to 2:

$$h_{13} = 1 + h_{23}$$

For $h_{23}$: neighbors of 2 are $\{1, 3\}$.

$$h_{23} = 1 + \frac{1}{2} h_{13} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0 = 1 + \frac{1}{2} h_{13}$$

**Step 2: Solve the system.** Substituting $h_{13} = 1 + h_{23}$:

$$h_{13} = 1 + \left(1 + \frac{1}{2} h_{13}\right) = 2 + \frac{1}{2} h_{13}$$

$$\frac{1}{2} h_{13} = 2 \;\Longrightarrow\; h_{13} = 4$$

Then $h_{23} = 1 + \frac{1}{2}(4) = 3$. By symmetry, $h_{31} = 4$ and $h_{32} = 1$. So:

$$C_{13} = h_{13} + h_{31} = 4 + 4 = 8$$

**Step 3: Verify via Resistance.** $R_{13} = 1 + 1 = 2\,\Omega$ (two resistors in series). Then:

$$2m \cdot R_{13} = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 = 8 \;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparing Standard Graphs

| Graph | $n$ | $m$ | $\max R_{uv}$ | $C(G)$ (asymptotic) | Algorithm Insight |
|-------|-----|-----|---------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Complete $K_n$ | $n$ | $n(n-1)/2$ | $2/n$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Coupon collector with $n-1$ neighbors |
| Path $P_n$ | $n$ | $n-1$ | $n-1$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Lollipop-like bottleneck |
| Cycle $C_n$ | $n$ | $n$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Resistance $\sim 1/4$ |
| Lollipop $L_n$ | $2n$ | $2n$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Worst case graph for cover time |
| Complete Binary Tree | $n$ | $n-1$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log^2 n)$ | $R_{uv} \sim 2 \cdot \text{depth}$ |
| Hypercube $Q_n$ | $2^n$ | $n \cdot 2^{n-1}$ | $\Theta(1/n)$ | $\Theta(n \cdot 2^n)$ | Dense but slow mixing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 State Transition Diagram ŌĆö Random Walk on a Triangle ($K_3$)

The following Mermaid diagram shows the random walk state machine on the triangle graph $K_3$ (vertices $A$, $B$, $C$, each pair connected).

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Vertex A"]:::stateNode
    nodeB["Vertex B"]:::stateNode
    nodeC["Vertex C"]:::stateNode

    nodeA -- "p equals 1 over 2" --> nodeB
    nodeA -- "p equals 1 over 2" --> nodeC
    nodeB -- "p equals 1 over 2" --> nodeA
    nodeB -- "p equals 1 over 2" --> nodeC
    nodeC -- "p equals 1 over 2" --> nodeA
    nodeC -- "p equals 1 over 2" --> nodeB

    classDef stateNode fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each directed edge is labeled with the transition probability. From any vertex of a regular graph like $K_3$, the walker has $\deg(v) = 2$ choices, each with probability $1/2$.

### 4.2 Hitting Time Computation Flow

The following diagram illustrates the workflow for computing hitting time $h_{ij}$ on an arbitrary graph.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Start at source vertex i"]:::processNode
    stepB["Identify neighbor set N of i"]:::processNode
    stepC["For each neighbor k in N compute transition probability 1 over deg i"]:::processNode
    stepD["Apply recurrence h_ij equals 1 plus sum of h_kj over deg i"]:::processNode
    stepE["Set h_jj equals 0 as base case"]:::processNode
    stepF["Solve the linear system in n minus 1 unknowns"]:::processNode
    stepG["Output h_ij expected hitting time"]:::processNode

    startA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG

    classDef processNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Electrical Network Analogy ŌĆö Block Architecture

The following diagram maps the random-walk / electrical-network correspondence as a block-level functional architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA["Graph G vertices V edges E"]:::inputNode
    blockB["Random Walk Process"]:::processNode
    blockC["Markov Chain with transition matrix P"]:::processNode
    blockD["Electrical Network each edge equals 1 Ohm"]:::altProcessNode
    blockE["Commute Time C_uv equals h_uv plus h_vu"]:::outputNode
    blockF["Effective Resistance R_uv"]:::outputNode
    blockG["Identity C_uv equals 2m times R_uv"]:::identityNode

    blockA --> blockB
    blockA --> blockD
    blockB --> blockC
    blockC --> blockE
    blockD --> blockF
    blockE --> blockG
    blockF --> blockG

    classDef inputNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef processNode fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef altProcessNode fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef identityNode fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Interpretation:** The same graph serves as input to two parallel analyses ŌĆö a **probabilistic** path (top row, leading to commute time) and an **electrical** path (bottom row, leading to effective resistance). The unifying identity $C_{uv} = 2m \cdot R_{uv}$ collapses them.

### 4.4 Cover Time Bound Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startN["Connected Graph G with n vertices and m edges"]:::initNode
    upperBox["Upper bound via max spanning tree path 2m times R_uv"]:::upperNode
    lowerBox["Lower bound via coupon collector style 2m times R_uv"]:::lowerNode
    chainBox["Combine into 2m R_uv leq C of G leq 2m squared R_uv log n log log n"]:::finalNode

    startN --> upperBox
    startN --> lowerBox
    upperBox --> chainBox
    lowerBox --> chainNode["chainNode placeholder"]:::unusedNode
    chainNode -.-> chainBox

    classDef initNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef upperNode fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lowerNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef finalNode fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef unusedNode fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#FFFFFF,color:#FFFFFF
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (2 ├Ś 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define a random walk on an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$. Write the transition probability $P_{uv}$ from vertex $u$ to neighbor $v$ in terms of $\deg(u)$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **random walk** on a graph $G = (V, E)$ is a stochastic process where a walk starts at some vertex $v_0 \in V$ and at each step moves from the current vertex to a neighbor chosen uniformly at random. The transition probability from $u$ to $v$ is:

$$P_{uv} = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{\deg(u)} & \text{if } (u,v) \in E \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The sequence of visited vertices forms a **time-homogeneous Markov chain** with state space $V$ and transition matrix $P$. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for transition formula, 1 mark for Markov chain property.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
*What is the **stationary distribution** of a random walk on a connected, undirected, non-bipartite graph? Justify the formula.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The stationary distribution is given by:

$$\pi(v) = \frac{\deg(v)}{2 \vert E \vert} \quad \text{for all } v \in V$$

**Justification:** Substituting into $\pi P = \pi$:

$$\sum_{u \in N(v)} \pi(u) P_{uv} = \sum_{u \in N(v)} \frac{\deg(u)}{2m} \cdot \frac{1}{\deg(u)} = \sum_{u \in N(v)} \frac{1}{2m} = \frac{\deg(v)}{2m} = \pi(v) \;\checkmark$$

The distribution is degree-proportional because highly connected vertices are revisited more often. *(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for verification step, 1 mark for intuition.)*

---

### ­¤ōĢ PART B ŌĆö Full-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (Module 2)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Module 2 question is an **internal choice** ŌĆö students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B, each carrying 14 marks (7 + 7).

---

#### Ō£ģ **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Style]*

**(a)** Define the **hitting time** $h_{ij}$ and the **commute time** $C_{ij}$ for a random walk on an undirected graph. Derive the recurrence relation for $h_{ij}$ when $i \neq j$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the path graph $P_4$ on vertices $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ with edges $\{(1,2), (2,3), (3,4)\}$. Compute the hitting time $h_{14}$ and the commute time $C_{14}$ using the recurrence relation. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (Q-A):**

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The **hitting time** $h_{ij}$ is the expected number of steps for a random walk starting at vertex $i$ to **first** reach vertex $j$. Formally:

$$h_{ij} = \mathbb{E}[T_{ij}], \quad \text{where } T_{ij} = \min\{t \geq 0 : X_t = j \mid X_0 = i\}$$

The **commute time** is the expected time to go from $i$ to $j$ and back to $i$:

$$C_{ij} = h_{ij} + h_{ji}$$

**Derivation of the recurrence (5 marks):** By the law of total expectation, conditioning on the first step:

$$h_{ij} = \sum_{k \in N(i)} \Pr[X_1 = k] \cdot \mathbb{E}[T_{ij} \mid X_1 = k]$$

Since the walk picks uniformly among $\deg(i)$ neighbors and has already taken one step before resuming from $k$:

$$h_{ij} = \sum_{k \in N(i)} \frac{1}{\deg(i)} \cdot \left(1 + h_{kj}\right) = 1 + \frac{1}{\deg(i)} \sum_{k \in N(i)} h_{kj}$$

Boundary condition: $h_{jj} = 0$ for all $j \in V$. This is a linear system of $\vert V \vert - 1$ equations in the unknowns $\{h_{ij}\}_{i \neq j}$.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Identify the equations. For $P_4$, the transition rules are: at vertex 2, move to 1 or 3 with probability 1/2 each; at vertex 3, move to 2 or 4 with probability 1/2 each. We need to solve for $h_{14}$, $h_{24}$, $h_{34}$:

$$h_{14} = 1 + h_{24}$$

$$h_{24} = 1 + \frac{1}{2} h_{14} + \frac{1}{2} h_{34}$$

$$h_{34} = 1 + \frac{1}{2} h_{24} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0 = 1 + \frac{1}{2} h_{24}$$

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Substitute the third equation into the second:

$$h_{24} = 1 + \frac{1}{2} h_{14} + \frac{1}{2} \left(1 + \frac{1}{2} h_{24}\right) = \frac{3}{2} + \frac{1}{2} h_{14} + \frac{1}{4} h_{24}$$

$$\frac{3}{4} h_{24} = \frac{3}{2} + \frac{1}{2} h_{14} \;\Longrightarrow\; h_{24} = 2 + \frac{2}{3} h_{14}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Use the first equation $h_{14} = 1 + h_{24} = 3 + \frac{2}{3} h_{14}$:

$$\frac{1}{3} h_{14} = 3 \;\Longrightarrow\; h_{14} = 9$$

Hence $h_{24} = 8$, $h_{34} = 5$, and by symmetry $h_{41} = 9$, $h_{42} = 5$, $h_{43} = 8$. So:

$$C_{14} = h_{14} + h_{41} = 9 + 9 = 18$$

**Verification via Resistance:** $R_{14}$ in $P_4$ is the series sum of three 1 ╬® resistors = $3\,\Omega$. Then $2m \cdot R_{14} = 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 3 = 18 \;\checkmark$

---

#### Ō£ģ **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Style]*

**(a)** State and prove the **commute-time / effective-resistance identity**: $C_{uv} = 2m \cdot R_{uv}$ for any two vertices $u, v$ in a connected undirected graph $G$ with $m$ edges. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compute the cover time of the **cycle graph** $C_n$ on $n$ vertices. Identify the maximum effective resistance and apply the cover-time bound. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (Q-B):**

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Statement (1 mark):** For any two vertices $u, v$ in a connected undirected graph $G$ with $m$ edges, the commute time of the random walk equals $2m$ times the effective resistance between $u$ and $v$ in the electrical network obtained by replacing each edge with a 1 ╬® resistor.

**Proof (6 marks):** Define the **potential function** $\phi(w) = h_{wv}$ (hitting time from $w$ to $v$). Set $\phi(v) = 0$ and inject a current of $I$ amperes at $u$, extracting at $v$.

**Step 1: Voltage Drop on Edge $(a, b)$:** By Ohm's law, the current on edge $(a, b)$ is $\phi(a) - \phi(b)$ (since each edge has resistance 1).

**Step 2: Apply KCL at Interior Vertices:** For any vertex $w \neq u, v$, the net current flow is zero:

$$\sum_{k \in N(w)} (\phi(w) - \phi(k)) = 0$$

Using the hitting-time recurrence, $\phi(w) = 1 + \frac{1}{\deg(w)} \sum_{k \in N(w)} \phi(k)$, this becomes:

$$\deg(w) \cdot \phi(w) - \sum_{k \in N(w)} \phi(k) = \deg(w)$$

Multiplying both sides appropriately, we confirm that $\phi$ is a valid electrical potential.

**Step 3: KCL at Source $u$:** The total injected current $I$ equals the sum of currents on all edges leaving $u$:

$$I = \sum_{k \in N(u)} (\phi(u) - \phi(k)) = \sum_{k \in N(u)} (h_{uv} - h_{kv})$$

By the same argument, $I = m$. By Ohm's law, $R_{uv} = (\phi(u) - \phi(v))/I = h_{uv}/I$, giving $h_{uv} = I \cdot R_{uv}$. Symmetrically, $h_{vu} = I \cdot R_{uv}$. Adding:

$$\boxed{C_{uv} = h_{uv} + h_{vu} = 2I \cdot R_{uv} = 2m \cdot R_{uv}}$$

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** For the cycle $C_n$, the effective resistance between any two vertices $a$ and $b$ at distance $d$ along the cycle is the parallel combination of the two arc paths:

$$R_{ab} = \frac{d \cdot (n - d)}{n}$$

The maximum occurs at $d = n/2$ (diametrically opposite vertices):

$$\max_{u,v} R_{uv} = R_{\max} = \frac{(n/2)^2}{n} = \frac{n}{4}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply the cover-time bounds with $m = n$ and $R_{\max} = n/4$:

$$2m \cdot R_{\max} = 2n \cdot \frac{n}{4} = \frac{n^2}{2}$$

$$2m^2 \cdot R_{\max} \cdot (\ln n + \ln \ln n + O(1)) = 2n^2 \cdot \frac{n}{4} \cdot (\ln n + \ln \ln n + O(1)) = \frac{n^3}{2} \cdot (\ln n + O(\log \log n))$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** For the cycle, the cover time is known to be $\Theta(n^2)$. The lower bound $C(C_n) \geq n^2/2$ is achieved because the walker's expected motion behaves like a random walk on $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$ with variance growing linearly. Therefore:

$$\boxed{C(C_n) = \Theta(n^2)}$$

---

### ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the boundary condition** $h_{jj} = 0$. Many students write the recurrence but omit the base case, losing 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing hitting time with commute time.** Hitting time $h_{ij}$ is from $i$ to $j$ *only*; commute time $C_{ij} = h_{ij} + h_{ji}$ includes the return trip. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Wrong resistance formula.** The effective resistance of a path of length $\ell$ is $\ell$ (series), not $1/\ell$. Remember resistors in series add, in parallel use $1/R = \sum 1/R_i$.
> 4. **Mixing directed and undirected graphs.** The $C_{uv} = 2m R_{uv}$ identity holds **only for undirected** graphs. For directed walks, use a different framework (e.g., absorbing Markov chains).
> 5. **Not verifying Markov chain properties.** When asked to "show" something is a random walk, explicitly state the **memoryless property** $\Pr[X_{t+1} \mid X_t, X_{t-1}, \ldots] = \Pr[X_{t+1} \mid X_t]$.
> 6. **Off-by-one in cover time.** The cover time counts the number of *steps*, not the number of *vertices visited*. Don't confuse $C(G)$ with $n$.

---

### ­¤Ä» Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Ō£ģ **Random walk on $G$** = Markov chain with transition $P_{uv} = 1/\deg(u)$ for each edge $(u,v)$.
- Ō£ģ **Hitting time** $h_{ij}$ satisfies $h_{ij} = 1 + \frac{1}{\deg(i)} \sum_{k \in N(i)} h_{kj}$ with $h_{jj} = 0$.
- Ō£ģ **Commute time** $C_{ij} = h_{ij} + h_{ji}$.
- Ō£ģ **Cover time** $C(G)$ = expected steps to visit *all* vertices.
- Ō£ģ **Stationary distribution** for undirected $G$: $\pi(v) = \deg(v)/(2m)$.
- Ō£ģ **Electrical identity** (THE key theorem): $C_{uv} = 2m \cdot R_{uv}$ for undirected connected $G$.
- Ō£ģ **Cover-time sandwich**: $2m \cdot R_{\max} \leq C(G) \leq 2m^2 \cdot R_{\max} \cdot (\ln n + \ln \ln n + O(1))$.
- Ō£ģ **Lollipop graph** $L_n$ has worst-case cover time $\Theta(n^2)$.
- Ō£ģ **Complete graph** $K_n$ has cover time $\Theta(n \log n)$ (coupon collector).
- Ō£ģ **Mixing time** is governed by the **spectral gap** $1 - \lambda_2$ of the transition matrix.
- Ō£ģ **Undirected non-bipartite** assumption is needed for unique stationary distribution.
- Ō£ģ **Lazy random walk** (stay in place with prob 1/2) is used to make the chain aperiodic when $G$ is bipartite.
- Ō£ģ **Master formula** for resistance of a path of length $d$ in cycle $C_n$: $R = d(n-d)/n$.
- Ō£ģ **Random walks are algorithmically powerful** because: (i) no memory of path needed, (ii) cover time is polynomial for most natural graphs, (iii) commute time is computable via Kirchhoff's laws.
- Ō£ģ **Applications to remember for exam**: 2-SAT, s-t connectivity, PageRank, graph sampling, undirected connectivity in log-space.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications in Randomized Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST639 - RANDOMIZED ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications in Randomized Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of Randomized Algorithms, **Applications** refer to the deployment of probabilistic decision-making (randomized choices, random bits, or hashing) to solve fundamental computational problems across sorting, selection, graph theory, cryptography, data structures, and number theory. These applications leverage the **Las Vegas** (always correct, random running time) and **Monte Carlo** (randomized correctness, bounded running time) paradigms to achieve provable expected-time bounds that are often simpler, faster, or more elegant than their deterministic counterparts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Applications of randomized algorithms span sorting (Quicksort), order statistics (Selection), graph cuts (Min-Cut), hashing (Universal Hashing), primality (MillerŌĆōRabin), and data structures (Skip Lists, Treaps). Every application must be analyzed using **Expected Running Time**, **Probability of Failure**, and **Success Probability Amplification** via repetition.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Coin-Flip Surgeon"

Imagine a surgeon who needs to locate a diseased cell in a million healthy cells.

- **Deterministic Approach:** Examine every single cell sequentially ŌĆö predictable but slow ($O(n)$).
- **Randomized Approach:** Toss a coin to randomly sample **k** cells, then check those.

If the diseased cell exists, the probability of catching it in one sample is $\frac{k}{n}$. Repeating the sampling amplifies the success probability exponentially. This is the **core intuition** behind randomized applications ŌĆö trade a tiny failure probability for a massive speed-up.

### 1.3 Classification of Applications

| Paradigm | Correctness | Running Time | Typical Application |
|----------|-------------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Las Vegas** | Always correct | Random (bounded expected) | Randomized Quicksort, Selection |
| **Monte Carlo (one-sided error)** | Correct OR always "yes" | Deterministic bound | Min-Cut, Primality |
| **Monte Carlo (two-sided error)** | Probabilistic | Deterministic bound | Polynomial Identity Testing |
| **Universal Hashing** | Deterministic (worst-case per key) | Expected $O(1)$ | Dictionary, Symbol Tables |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics used in KTU Valuation:**
> - **Expected Time** $\mathbb{E}[T(n)]$
> - **Probability of Bad Event** $\Pr[\text{failure}] \leq \frac{1}{n^c}$
> - **Tail Bound** (Chernoff / Markov / Chebyshev)
> - **Random Bits Used / Sample Complexity**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Application 1 ŌĆö Randomized Quicksort

**Operational Steps:**
1. Pick a pivot uniformly at random from the array of $n$ elements.
2. Partition the array around the pivot ŌĆö $O(n)$.
3. Recurse on the two sub-arrays independently.

**Why Randomized?**
