**(a)** Define the moment generating function. Prove that for independent Bernoulli random variables, $M_X(t) \le \exp(\mu(e^t - 1))$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a randomized algorithm for graph coloring where each vertex is assigned one of $k$ colors uniformly at random, independently. For a graph with maximum degree $\Delta$, bound the probability that **any edge** is monochromatic. Use Chernoff-style reasoning and the union bound. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) MGF Definition and Bound (7 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The moment generating function of a random variable $X$ is $M_X(t) = \mathbb{E}[e^{tX}]$ for $t \in \mathbb{R}$.

For independent Bernoulli $X_i$ with $\Pr[X_i = 1] = p_i$:

$$
M_X(t) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[e^{t \sum X_i}\right] = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[e^{tX_i}] = \prod_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(p_i e^{t} + (1-p_i)\bigr) = \prod_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(1 + p_i(e^{t} - 1)\bigr)
$$

Using $1 + x \le e^x$ for all real $x$:

$$
\prod_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(1 + p_i(e^{t} - 1)\bigr) \le \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{p_i(e^{t} - 1)} = e^{(e^{t} - 1) \sum p_i} = e^{\mu(e^{t} - 1)}
$$

**Mark split:** 2 marks for definition; 3 marks for independence factorization; 2 marks for applying $1 + x \le e^x$.

**(b) Graph Coloring Application (7 marks):**

Let $G = (V, E)$ have $|V| = n$ and max degree $\Delta$. For each edge $e = (u, v)$, define indicator $Y_e = 1$ if both endpoints share the same color, else $0$. Since each vertex picks a color uniformly from $k$ colors, independently:

$$
\Pr[Y_e = 1] = \sum_{c=1}^{k} \Pr[\text{both get color } c] = \sum_{c=1}^{k} \frac{1}{k^{2}} = \frac{1}{k}
$$

Let $Y = \sum_{e \in E} Y_e$ be the total count of monochromatic edges. Then $\mathbb{E}[Y] = \vert E \vert / k \le n\Delta / (2k)$ since $|E| \le n\Delta/2$.

We want $\Pr[Y \ge 1] \le \Pr[Y \ge \mathbb{E}[Y] \cdot 2k]$ when $k$ is large. Setting $\delta = 2k - 1$ in the upper tail bound... **a cleaner approach:**

By **Markov's inequality directly** (no Chernoff needed for this basic bound):

$$
\Pr[Y \ge 1] \le \mathbb{E}[Y] \le \frac{n\Delta}{2k}
$$

For a **Chernoff-style** sharper bound on a single edge being monochromatic:

$$
\Pr[Y_e = 1] = \frac{1}{k} \le \exp\!\left(-\frac{(k-1)^{2}}{3k}\right) \approx e^{-k/3} \text{ for large } k
$$

Union bound over all $|E|$ edges:

$$
\Pr[\text{some edge monochromatic}] \le \frac{n\Delta}{2k}
$$

Choosing $k = c \cdot \ln n$ for constant $c$ gives a vanishing failure probability.

**Mark split:** 2 marks for setting up indicators; 2 marks for computing $\Pr[Y_e = 1] = 1/k$; 3 marks for applying union bound and choosing $k$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the validity condition on $\delta$** ŌĆö The simplified form $\exp(-\mu\delta^2/3)$ requires $0 \le \delta \le 1$. Using it for $\delta > 1$ is incorrect (and **deducts 1ŌĆō2 marks**).
> 2. **Confusing Hoeffding with Chernoff** ŌĆö Hoeffding's inequality applies to bounded variables $X_i \in [a_i, b_i]$, not just Bernoulli. State which one you are using.
> 3. **Skipping the optimization step** ŌĆö Writing the bound directly without showing $t^* = \ln(1+\delta)$ means **losing at least 2 marks** on derivation questions.
> 4. **Misapplying the Union Bound** ŌĆö Chernoff bounds apply to a *single* random variable. To bound events across multiple items (e.g., all $n$ bins), you **must** apply the union bound explicitly: $\Pr[\bigcup A_i] \le \sum \Pr[A_i]$.
> 5. **Forgetting independence** ŌĆö Chernoff bounds require independent $X_i$. If your problem has dependent variables, you need **Azuma's inequality** instead.
> 6. **Using `|` for absolute value in tables** ŌĆö Markdown will break; examiners may deduct for sloppy presentation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Chernoff Bounds**

- [x] **Definition:** Bound on $\Pr[X \ge (1+\delta)\mu]$ or $\Pr[X \le (1-\delta)\mu]$ for $X = \sum X_i$ where $X_i$ are independent Bernoulli.
- [x] **Pre-requisite tools:** (1) Markov's inequality, (2) MGF, (3) $1 + x \le e^x$, (4) calculus optimization.
- [x] **Five-step proof template:** Exponential trick ŌåÆ Markov ŌåÆ MGF bound ŌåÆ Optimize $t$ ŌåÆ Simplify.
- [x] **Optimal parameter:** $t^* = \ln(1+\delta)$.
- [x] **Canonical upper tail:** $\left(\dfrac{e^{\delta}}{(1+\delta)^{1+\delta}}\right)^{\mu}$.
- [x] **Canonical lower tail:** $\left(\dfrac{e^{-\delta}}{(1-\delta)^{1-\delta}}\right)^{\mu}$.
- [x] **Simplified forms (for $0 \le \delta \le 1$):** Upper $\exp(-\mu\delta^2/3)$, Lower $\exp(-\mu\delta^2/2)$.
- [x] **Validity:** Upper tail for $\delta > -1$; Lower tail for $0 \le \delta < 1$.
- [x] **Use cases:** Load balancing, packet routing, sampling, hashing, PAC learning.
- [x] **Don't forget:** Union bound when extending to multiple items.
- [x] **Related inequalities:** Hoeffding (bounded r.v.), Azuma (martingales), Bernstein (variance-aware).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Concentration Inequalities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST639 - RANDOMIZED ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Applications of Concentration Inequalities # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Concentration Inequalities (Formal Definition):** > Concentration inequalities are a family of mathematical bounds that quantify how a random variable (or function of multiple random variables) is *concentrated* around its expected value (mean). Formally, for a random variable $X$ with $\mathbb{E}[X] = \mu$, a concentration inequality provides an upper bound on $\Pr[|X - \mu| \geq t]$ that decays **exponentially** in $t$, in contrast to Markov/Chebyshev bounds which only decay polynomially. In the context of **Randomized Algorithms (PECST639, Module 3)**, these inequalities are the principal tool used to prove that a probabilistic algorithm's *bad* events occur with negligible probability, enabling the derivation of **high-probability guarantees** (e.g., "with probability $\geq 1 - 1/n^c$, the algorithm succeeds"). ### 1.2 The Key Inequalities Recalled The hierarchy of concentration inequalities, from weakest to strongest, is: | Inequality | Strength | Type of Bound | |---|---|---| | **Markov's Inequality** | Weak | $\Pr[X \geq t] \leq \mathbb{E}[X]/t$ (only non-negative RVs) | | **Chebyshev's Inequality** | Weak-Medium | $\Pr[|X-\mu| \geq t] \leq \sigma^2/t^2$ | | **ChernoffŌĆōHoeffding Bound** | Strong | Exponential decay for sums of independent bounded RVs | | **Azuma's Inequality** | Strong | Bounded differences / martingales | | **McDiarmid's Inequality** | Strong | Bounded differences for general functions | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The "Coin Toss Exam" Intuition:** > Imagine you are a teacher and you give a student 1000 true/false questions. The student guesses each answer by a fair coin toss. The expected score is $500$. Using Markov's inequality, you can only claim "the probability the student scores above $900$ is at most $500/900 \approx 0.55$" ŌĆö useless. Chebyshev gives "at most $0.0025$" ŌĆö better but still loose. **Chernoff's bound** crushes this down to "at most $10^{-89}$". This is the *magic* of concentration: as the number of independent trials grows, deviations from the mean become **exponentially rare**, exactly like how the bell curve's tails shrink when averaged over many coin flips. **Engineering Significance:** For a randomized routing protocol on an internet router, we do **not** want a "probably correct" bound like $0.99$. We need $1 - 10^{-30}$. Concentration inequalities deliver precisely this. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Natural logarithm base:** $e \approx 2.71828$ - **Constant of integration identity:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-x^2/2}\,dx = \sqrt{2\pi}$ - **Universal Chernoff exponential base:** $\delta^2/2$ (for Hoeffding) or $\delta^2/(2+\delta)$ (for the standard additive Chernoff form). - **Standard failure probability:** $\delta = 1/n^c$ where $c$ is a small positive integer (typically $c=1$ or $c=2$). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Mechanics ŌĆö How Concentration Bounds Work The fundamental architecture of *why* concentration inequalities give exponential tails can be decomposed as follows: - **Step 1 ŌĆö Moment Generation:** Compute the **Moment Generating Function (MGF)** of $X$, defined as $\mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X}]$ for a tunable parameter $\lambda > 0$. - **Step 2 ŌĆö Markov's Trick:** Apply Markov's inequality to the non-negative random variable $e^{\lambda X}$. This yields $\Pr[X \geq t] \leq e^{-\lambda t} \cdot \mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X}]$. - **Step 3 ŌĆö Optimization:** Choose the parameter $\lambda$ that **minimizes** the right-hand side. For sums of independent bounded RVs, the MGF factorizes into a product, and the optimized bound becomes exponential. - **Step 4 ŌĆö Symmetrization:** To bound both tails $\Pr[X \geq \mu + t]$ and $\Pr[X \leq \mu - t]$ simultaneously, apply the same trick to $e^{-\lambda X}$, yielding two-sided bounds. > [!TIP] > **The "Why" ŌĆö Engineering Insight:** Concentration inequalities are essentially *Markov's inequality applied to a clever exponential transformation* $e^{\lambda X}$. The exponential weighting magnifies deviations from the mean, while the freedom to choose $\lambda$ lets us tilt the bound optimally. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Inequality Name | Statement | When to Apply | |---|---|---| | **Markov** | $\Pr[X \geq t] \leq \mathbb{E}[X]/t$ | Non-negative RV, no variance info | | **Chebyshev** | $\Pr[\vert X - \mu \vert \geq t] \leq \mathrm{Var}(X)/t^2$ | Finite second moment | | **Chernoff (Upper Tail)** | $\Pr[X \geq (1+\delta)\mu] \leq \left(\frac{e^{\delta}}{(1+\delta)^{1+\delta}}\right)^{\mu}$ | Sum of independent Bernoullis, $0<\delta$ | | **Chernoff Simplified** | $\Pr[X \geq (1+\delta)\mu] \leq e^{-\mu \delta^2/3}$ for $0 < \delta \leq 1$ | Same, simplified bound | | **Chernoff (Lower Tail)** | $\Pr[X \leq (1-\delta)\mu] \leq e^{-\mu \delta^2/2}$ for $0 < \delta < 1$ | Sum of independent Bernoullis | | **Hoeffding** | $\Pr\left[\vert \overline{X} - \mu \vert \geq t\right] \leq 2\exp\left(-2nt^2\right)$ | Sum of $n$ i.i.d. RVs in $[a,b]$ | | **Azuma** | $\Pr[\vert S_n - \mathbb{E}[S_n]\vert \geq t\right] \leq 2\exp\left(-t^2 / \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i^2\right)$ | Martingale $S_n$ with $\vert S_i - S_{i-1}\vert \leq c_i$ | | **McDiarmid** | $\Pr[\vert f(X_1,\dots,X_n) - \mathbb{E}[f]\vert \geq t\right] \leq 2\exp\left(-2t^2 / \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i^2\right)$ | Bounded-difference function | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Note on KTU Valuations:** Examiners *always* test whether you know **which inequality applies** to which setting. Writing Azuma when Hoeffding is needed costs full marks. Memorize the *trigger conditions*. ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Network Packet Routing:** Bound the queue length at a router to prove low latency. - **Cryptographic Hashing:** Prove that universal hash families have low collision probability. - **Load Balancing (Balls into Bins):** Show that the maximum bin load is $O(\log n / \log\log n)$ w.h.p. - **PAC-Learning & DNF Counting:** Bound the sample complexity of learning algorithms. - **Sparse Recovery / Compressed Sensing:** Bound the reconstruction error under RIP conditions. - **Stochastic Gradient Descent:** Bound the deviation of the empirical risk from the true risk in ML. ## 2.4 Why Concentration is the Backbone of Randomized Algorithms > [!IMPORTANT] > A randomized algorithm typically runs in expected polynomial time. To strengthen this to *worst-case high probability*, we use **concentration inequalities** to show that the "bad" tail mass vanishes faster than any polynomial $1/n^c$. This converts expectation guarantees into rigorous w.h.p. certificates ŌĆö the gold standard required for production-grade systems. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Multiplicative Chernoff Bound We derive the upper-tail Chernoff bound from scratch, since this is the *most exam-relevant* inequality in PECST639. ### Setup Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be **independent Bernoulli random variables** with $\Pr[X_i = 1] = p_i$. Let $X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ with $\mu = \mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$. We want to bound $\Pr[X \geq (1+\delta)\mu]$ for $\delta > 0$. ### Derivation For any $\lambda > 0$: $$ \Pr[X \geq (1+\delta)\mu] = \Pr[e^{\lambda X} \geq e^{\lambda(1+\delta)\mu}] $$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Markov's inequality** to the non-negative RV $e^{\lambda X}$: $$ \Pr[e^{\lambda X} \geq e^{\lambda(1+\delta)\mu}] \leq \frac{\mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X}]}{e^{\lambda(1+\delta)\mu}} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Factorize the MGF** using independence of $X_i$: $$ \mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X}] = \mathbb{E}\left[\prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{\lambda X_i}\right] = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X_i}] $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Bound each factor.** Since $X_i \in \{0, 1\}$ and $\mathbb{E}[X_i] = p_i$: $$ \mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X_i}] = p_i e^{\lambda} + (1-p_i) = 1 + p_i(e^{\lambda}-1) $$ Using the universal inequality $1 + x \leq e^{x}$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$: $$ \mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X_i}] \leq e^{p_i(e^{\lambda}-1)} $$ Therefore: $$ \mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X}] \leq \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{p_i(e^{\lambda}-1)} = e^{\mu(e^{\lambda}-1)} $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute back:** $$ \Pr[X \geq (1+\delta)\mu] \leq \frac{e^{\mu(e^{\lambda}-1)}}{e^{\lambda(1+\delta)\mu}} = e^{\mu(e^{\lambda}-1 - \lambda(1+\delta))} $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Optimize over $\lambda$.** Set the derivative of the exponent to zero: $$ \frac{d}{d\lambda}\left[e^{\lambda}-1 - \lambda(1+\delta)\right] = e^{\lambda} - (1+\delta) = 0 $$ This gives $\lambda^* = \ln(1+\delta)$. **Step 6 ŌĆö Substitute $\lambda^*$:** $$ e^{\lambda^*} - 1 - \lambda^*(1+\delta) = (1+\delta) - 1 - (1+\delta)\ln(1+\delta) = \delta - (1+\delta)\ln(1+\delta) $$ So the bound becomes: $$ \Pr[X \geq (1+\delta)\mu] \leq e^{\mu[\delta - (1+\delta)\ln(1+\delta)]} $$ Rewriting using the standard form: $$ \Pr[X \geq (1+\delta)\mu] \leq \left(\frac{e^{\delta}}{(1+\delta)^{(1+\delta)}}\right)^{\mu} $$ This is the **exact Chernoff upper-tail bound**. $\blacksquare$ ### Simplified Forms Used in Practice For $0 \leq \delta \leq 1$: $$ \Pr[X \geq (1+\delta)\mu] \leq e^{-\mu \delta^2 / 3} $$ For $\delta > 1$: $$ \Pr[X \geq (1+\delta)\mu] \leq e^{-\mu \delta / 3} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Azuma's Inequality (Bounded Differences Version) Azuma applies to **martingales** $S_0, S_1, \ldots, S_n$ where $\mathbb{E}[S_i \mid S_{i-1}] = S_{i-1}$ and the bounded-difference condition $\vert S_i - S_{i-1}\vert \leq c_i$. **Step 1:** For any $\lambda > 0$, apply Markov to $e^{\lambda(S_n - S_0)}$: $$ \Pr[S_n - S_0 \geq t] \leq e^{-\lambda t} \cdot \mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda(S_n - S_0)}] $$ **Step 2:** Factorize the MGF via the tower property of conditional expectation: $$ \mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda(S_n - S_0)}] = \mathbb{E}_{S_0}\mathbb{E}_{S_1\mid S_0}\cdots\mathbb{E}_{S_n\mid S_{n-1}}\left[\prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{\lambda(S_i - S_{i-1})}\right] $$ **Step 3:** Use Hoeffding's lemma for bounded RVs: if $\vert Y \vert \leq c$, then $\mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda Y}] \leq e^{\lambda^2 c^2 / 2}$. Combining everything: $$ \Pr[S_n - S_0 \geq t] \leq \exp\left(-\lambda t + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\lambda^2 c_i^2}{2}\right) $$ **Step 4:** Optimize over $\lambda$: $$ \lambda^* = \frac{t}{\sum c_i^2} $$ Plugging back: $$ \Pr[S_n - S_0 \geq t] \leq \exp\left(-\frac{t^2}{2\sum c_i^2}\right) $$ Symmetrizing for the two-sided bound gives Azuma's inequality. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.3 Application 1 ŌĆö Balls into Bins (Occupancy Bound) **Problem:** Throw $n$ balls into $n$ bins uniformly at random. Let $M$ be the maximum number of balls in any bin. **Step 1:** For a fixed bin $i$, let $X_i$ be the indicator that ball $j$ lands in bin $i$. Then $X = \sum_{j=1}^{n} X_{ij}$ has $\mu = 1$. **Step 2:** Apply Chernoff with $\delta = k-1$: $$ \Pr[X \geq k] \leq \left(\frac{e^{k-1}}{k^k}\right)^1 \leq \frac{e^{k-1}}{k^k} $$ For $k = \frac{3\ln n}{\ln\ln n}$: $$ \Pr[X \geq k] \leq \frac{1}{n^2} $$ **Step 3:** Union bound over $n$ bins: $$ \Pr[M \geq k] \leq n \cdot \frac{1}{n^2} = \frac{1}{n} $$ Hence $M = O(\log n / \log\log n)$ with high probability. --- ## 3.4 Application 2 ŌĆö MAX-CUT Randomized Algorithm **Problem:** Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ with $|V| = n$, $|E| = m$, find a cut $(S, \bar{S})$ maximizing cut edges. **Algorithm:** Place each vertex in $S$ independently with probability $1/2$. **Step 1:** For each edge $e_i$, let $X_i = 1$ if $e_i$ is cut. Then $\mathbb{E}[X_i] = 1/2$. **Step 2:** Total cut size $X = \sum X_i$ has $\mathbb{E}[X] = m/2$. **Step 3:** Apply Chernoff lower-tail bound with $\delta = 1/2$: $$ \Pr[X \leq m/4] \leq e^{-m/8} $$ So with probability at least $1 - e^{-m/8}$, the cut has at least $m/2$ edges ŌĆö a **$1/2$-approximation** guaranteed w.h.p. --- ## 3.5 Application 3 ŌĆö Set Balancing via Discrepancy **Problem:** Given $n$ sets over a ground set of size $m$, color each element Red/Blue to *minimize* the max imbalance. Let $\chi_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ be independent Rademacher RVs. For set $A_j$, the discrepancy is $D_j = \sum_{i \in A_j} \chi_i$. **Step 1:** Apply McDiarmid's inequality with bounded differences $c_i = 2$: $$ \Pr[\vert D_j \vert \geq t] \leq 2\exp\left(-\frac{t^2}{2m}\right) $$ **Step 2:** Setting $t = c\sqrt{m\log n}$ for appropriate $c$, then union-bounding over all $n$ sets yields a balanced coloring with discrepancy $O(\sqrt{m \log n})$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Applying Concentration Inequalities ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Identify Random Process X"] --> B["Compute Expectation mu"] B --> C{"Independent
Bounded RVs?"} C -- Yes --> D["Use Chernoff-Hoeffding Bound"] C -- No, but martingale --> E["Use Azuma's Inequality"] C -- No, but bounded-difference fcn --> F["Use McDiarmid's Inequality"] D --> G["Pick delta and t for tail event"] E --> G F --> G G --> H["Derive Exponential Decay Bound"] H --> I["Union Bound over n events"] I --> J["Conclude w.h.p. Success"] ``` ## 4.2 Architecture of Chernoff's Bound Derivation ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Sum X = Sum X_i"] --> B["MGF: E e to the lambda X"] B --> C["Factorize via Independence"] C --> D["Hoeffding Lemma / 1+x <= e to x"] D --> E["Optimize lambda"] E --> F["Exponential Tail Bound"] F --> G["Union Bound for w.h.p."] ``` ## 4.3 Application Landscape Map ```mermaid flowchart TD Root["Concentration Inequalities"] Root --> C["Chernoff Hoeffding"] Root --> A["Azuma"] Root --> M["McDiarmid"] C --> C1["Balls into Bins"] C --> C2["MAX CUT"] C --> C3["DNF Counting"] C --> C4["Hashing Collision"] A --> A1["Packet Routing"] A --> A2["Online Algorithms"] A --> A3["Stochastic Gradient"] M --> M1["Set Balancing"] M --> M2["Discrepancy Theory"] M --> M3["PAC Learning"] ``` ## 4.4 Comparison Flow ŌĆö Strength vs Use Case ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Choose Inequality"] --> Q1{"Need only
polynomial decay?"} Q1 -- Yes --> Q1a["Chebyshev / Markov"] Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Independent
sum of bounded RVs?"} Q2 -- Yes --> Q2a["Chernoff Hoeffding"] Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Martingale structure?"} Q3 -- Yes --> Q3a["Azuma"] Q3 -- No --> Q4{"Bounded-difference function
of independent RVs?"} Q4 -- Yes --> Q4a["McDiarmid"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024] **State Markov's inequality and Chebyshev's inequality. Why are they called "weak" concentration inequalities?** **Model Answer:** **Markov's Inequality:** For any non-negative random variable $X$ and $t > 0$, $$ \Pr[X \geq t] \leq \frac{\mathbb{E}[X]}{t} $$ **Chebyshev's Inequality:** For any RV $X$ with finite variance $\sigma^2$ and $t > 0$, $$ \Pr[\vert X - \mu \vert \geq t] \leq \frac{\sigma^2}{t^2} $$ **Why weak:** Both bounds only decay **polynomially** in $t$ (i.e., $1/t$ and $1/t^2$). They do not exploit higher moments. Concentration inequalities like Chernoff give **exponential** decay $e^{-\Omega(t^2)}$, which is vastly tighter for large $t$. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023] **When would you use Hoeffding's inequality instead of Chebyshev's inequality? State the exact form of Hoeffding's inequality.** **Model Answer:** Hoeffding is preferred when we have a sum of **independent bounded random variables**, because it exploits the boundedness to obtain **exponential decay**. Chebyshev only uses variance and gives polynomial decay. **Hoeffding's Inequality:** Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be independent RVs with $X_i \in [a_i, b_i]$. Let $S_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$. Then for any $t > 0$, $$ \Pr[\vert S_n - \mathbb{E}[S_n] \vert \geq t] \leq 2\exp\left(-\frac{2t^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(b_i - a_i)^2}\right) $$ --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö Module Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** State and prove the **Chernoff upper-tail bound** for a sum $X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ of independent Bernoulli random variables. Give the simplified form valid for $0 < \delta \leq 1$. **(b) [7 Marks]** Apply the Chernoff bound to prove that when $n$ balls are thrown uniformly into $n$ bins, the maximum load is $O(\log n / \log\log n)$ with high probability. --- #### Part (a) ŌĆö Solution **Statement:** Let $X_i$ be independent Bernoullis with $\Pr[X_i = 1] = p_i$, $X = \sum X_i$, $\mu = \mathbb{E}[X]$. For any $\delta > 0$: $$ \Pr[X \geq (1+\delta)\mu] \leq \left(\frac{e^{\delta}}{(1+\delta)^{(1+\delta)}}\right)^{\mu} $$ **Proof:** Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes. Key steps: 1. $\Pr[X \geq (1+\delta)\mu] = \Pr[e^{\lambda X} \geq e^{\lambda(1+\delta)\mu}] \leq e^{-\lambda(1+\delta)\mu} \mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X}]$ (Markov) 2. $\mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X}] = \prod \mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X_i}] \leq \prod e^{p_i(e^{\lambda}-1)} = e^{\mu(e^{\lambda}-1)}$ (independence + $1+x \leq e^x$) 3. Optimize $\lambda$: $\lambda^* = \ln(1+\delta)$. 4. Substituting back yields the bound. $\blacksquare$ **Simplified form** for $0 < \delta \leq 1$: $$ \Pr[X \geq (1+\delta)\mu] \leq e^{-\mu \delta^2 / 3} $$ --- #### Part (b) ŌĆö Solution Let $M = \max_{j} X_j$ where $X_j$ is the load of bin $j$. For a fixed bin, $\mu = 1$. For integer $k$, the probability that bin $j$ has $\geq k$ balls is bounded by Chernoff with $\delta = k-1$: $$ \Pr[X_j \geq k] \leq \frac{e^{k-1}}{k^k} $$ For $k = \frac{3\ln n}{\ln\ln n}$, we have $k^k \geq n^3 \cdot e^{k-1}$, so $\Pr[X_j \geq k] \leq 1/n^3$. Union bound over $n$ bins: $$ \Pr[M \geq k] \leq n \cdot \frac{1}{n^3} = \frac{1}{n^2} $$ So $M = O(\log n / \log\log n)$ with probability $\geq 1 - 1/n^2$. $\blacksquare$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** State **Azuma's inequality** for martingales and explain the *bounded differences* condition. Mention two real-world applications. **(b) [7 Marks]** A randomized routing algorithm sends $n$ packets along $n$ random paths. Let $T$ be the maximum queue length at any router. Using a martingale construction, show that $T = O(\sqrt{n \log n})$ with high probability. --- #### Part (a) ŌĆö Solution **Statement:** Let $S_0, S_1, \ldots, S_n$ be a martingale with bounded differences, i.e., $\vert S_i - S_{i-1} \vert \leq c_i$ for all $i$. Then for any $t > 0$: $$ \Pr[\vert S_n - S_0 \vert \geq t] \leq 2\exp\left(-\frac{t^2}{2\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i^2}\right) $$ **Bounded differences condition:** The change in the martingale from step $i-1$ to $i$ must be bounded by some constant $c_i$ regardless of history. This restricts how violently the random process can jump at each step. **Applications:** 1. **Packet routing in networks** (queue-length bounding) 2. **Online algorithms analysis** (e.g., paging, ski-rental) 3. **Stochastic gradient descent** (generalization bounds) 4. **Randomized smoothing** in non-smooth optimization --- #### Part (b) ŌĆö Solution Construct a martingale $S_i$ representing the maximum queue length after $i$ packets are routed. Each new packet changes the load by at most $1$, so $c_i = 1$. By Azuma: $$ \Pr[\vert S_n - \mathbb{E}[S_n] \vert \geq t] \leq 2\exp\left(-\frac{t^2}{2n}\right) $$ Setting $t = c\sqrt{n \log n}$ for constant $c$: $$ \Pr[T \geq c\sqrt{n\log n}] \leq 2\exp\left(-\frac{c^2 \log n}{2}\right) = \frac{2}{n^{c^2/2}} $$ Choosing $c = 2$ gives failure probability $2/n^2$, hence $T = O(\sqrt{n \log n})$ w.h.p. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting union-bound factor:** When bounding the maximum of $n$ events, multiply the single-event bound by $n$ (or $n^c$). Skipping this loses 2-3 marks. > 2. **Wrong Chernoff exponent:** Writing $\exp(-\mu\delta^2/2)$ instead of $\exp(-\mu\delta^2/3)$ for $0<\delta<1$ is a common error. Memorize: **lower tail uses $\delta^2/2$, upper tail uses $\delta^2/3$**. > 3. **Conflating Hoeffding with Chernoff:** Hoeffding applies to **bounded continuous** RVs in $[a_i,b_i]$; Chernoff is specifically for **sums of independent Bernoullis (or 0-1 RVs)**. > 4. **Missing independence assumption:** All concentration bounds require either independence or a structured martingale. Examiners deduct 2 marks if this assumption is not explicitly stated. > 5. **Choosing wrong $\lambda$ in derivations:** Always set derivative to zero and substitute back. Skipping the optimization step loses 1-2 marks in derivations. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - [x] **Markov's inequality** applies only to **non-negative** RVs. - [x] **Chebyshev** uses only the **second moment** and gives $1/t^2$ decay. - [x] **Chernoff bound** gives **exponential** decay $e^{-\Omega(\mu \delta^2)}$ for sums of independent bounded RVs. - [x] **Upper-tail Chernoff:** $\Pr[X \geq (1+\delta)\mu] \leq e^{-\mu\delta^2/3}$ for $0 < \delta \leq 1$. - [x] **Lower-tail Chernoff:** $\Pr[X \leq (1-\delta)\mu] \leq e^{-\mu\delta^2/2}$ for $0 < \delta < 1$. - [x] **Hoeffding** generalizes Chernoff to bounded RVs in $[a_i, b_i]$: $\Pr[\vert S_n - \mu \vert \geq t] \leq 2e^{-2t^2/\sum(b_i-a_i)^2}$. - [x] **Azuma's inequality** applies to martingales with **bounded differences** $c_i$. - [x] **McDiarmid's inequality** applies to functions with **bounded differences** in their arguments. - [x] **Balls into Bins:** Maximum load is $O(\log n / \log\log n)$ w.h.p. using Chernoff + union bound. - [x] **MAX-CUT:** Random cut gives expected $m/2$ edges; Chernoff gives $\geq m/4$ w.h.p. - [x] **Always use union bound** when extending from single-event to multi-event analyses. - [x] **Derivation recipe:** (1) Markov on $e^{\lambda X}$, (2) factorize via independence, (3) optimize $\lambda$, (4) symmetrize if two-sided. - [x] **Engineering applications:** routing, hashing, load balancing, learning theory, discrepancy, PAC-learning, online algorithms. - [x] **Failure probability convention:** w.h.p. means probability $\geq 1 - 1/n^c$ for some constant $c > 0$. - [x] **Key parameter:** Always set Chernoff's $\delta$ based on the *target value* of $X$, e.g., $\delta = (t/\mu) - 1$. - [x] **Marks distribution tip:** In 14-mark questions, expect either full derivation (7 marks) or application to a known problem (7 marks). Practice both.

topics.title: Randomized Rounding and Martingales: Randomized Rounding Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST639 - RANDOMIZED ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Randomized Rounding and Martingales: Randomized Rounding Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Randomized Rounding

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Randomized Rounding** is a probabilistic technique introduced by **Raghavan and Thompson (1987)** that converts the fractional solution of a Linear Programming (LP) or Semi-Definite Programming (SDP) relaxation into an integral (discrete) solution by treating each fractional variable $x_i \in [0,1]$ as the probability of including the corresponding discrete element in the final rounded solution.

Mathematically, given an LP relaxation solution $\{x_i^* \in [0,1]\}$, randomized rounding independently sets:

$$\tilde{x}_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{with probability } x_i^* \\ 0 & \text{with probability } 1 - x_i^* \end{cases}$$

The rounded vector $\tilde{x} = (\tilde{x}_1, \tilde{x}_2, \ldots, \tilde{x}_n)$ becomes a feasible (or near-feasible) 0/1 solution for the original combinatorial problem.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Explanation for First-Time Learners**

Imagine you are organizing a college fest and the LP solver tells you that the *ideal fractional budget allocation* for a stall is **0.7 units** of resources. You cannot literally spend "0.7 of a stall" ŌĆö you either open the stall (1) or you do not (0). 

**Randomized rounding** is like flipping a weighted coin: a coin that lands *Heads* with probability **0.7** and *Tails* with probability **0.3**. Whichever way the coin lands, you make a hard binary decision. When you repeat this for thousands of independent decisions and take the *expected outcome*, you get back to roughly 0.7 ŌĆö the LP's perfect fractional solution ŌĆö but with concrete yes/no choices.

In short: **use probability to snap soft fractional answers into crisp integer answers, while preserving the average behavior.**

---

## 1.3 Role of Martingales in Randomized Rounding

> [!NOTE]
> **Martingale Definition (KTU 2024 Module 4)**
> A sequence of random variables $X_0, X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ is a **martingale** with respect to the filtration $\mathcal{F}_0 \subseteq \mathcal{F}_1 \subseteq \ldots \subseteq \mathcal{F}_n$ if:
> $$\mathbb{E}[\vert X_i \vert] < \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \mathbb{E}[X_{i+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_i] = X_i$$

A martingale has the property that *tomorrow's expected value equals today's value* ŌĆö i.e., it is a **fair game** at every step. Martingales are used in conjunction with **Azuma-Hoeffding inequality** to prove that randomized rounding solutions are tightly concentrated around their expectation, enabling rigorous approximation guarantees.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants Used

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Number of variables | $n$ | Problem size |
| Failure probability | $\delta$ | $0 < \delta < 1$ |
| Deviation parameter | $\varepsilon$ | $0 < \varepsilon < 1$ |
| Number of trials | $N$ | Polynomial in $n$ |
| Chernoff constant | $e$ | **Euler's number $\approx 2.71828$** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Randomized Rounding Pipeline (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard 5-Stage Pipeline Used in KTU-style Problems**

### Stage 1 ŌĆö Formulate the Integer Program (IP)
Express the combinatorial optimization problem as an IP with binary variables $x_i \in \{0,1\}$ and a linear objective.

### Stage 2 ŌĆö Solve the LP Relaxation
Replace $x_i \in \{0,1\}$ with $x_i \in [0,1]$ and solve the resulting LP optimally in polynomial time, yielding the fractional optimum $x^*$.

### Stage 3 ŌĆö Independent Randomized Rounding
For each $i$, flip an independent coin: include $i$ in the solution with probability $x_i^*$. Generate the rounded 0/1 vector $\tilde{x}$.

### Stage 4 ŌĆö Bound the Expectation
Show that the expected value of the rounded objective equals (or closely approximates) the LP optimum, i.e., $\mathbb{E}[\text{Objective}(\tilde{x})] \approx \text{OPT}_{LP} \geq \text{OPT}_{IP}$.

### Stage 5 ŌĆö Apply Concentration (Chernoff / Azuma)
Use **Chernoff bounds** (independent case) or **Azuma-Hoeffding inequality** (martingale case) to show that with high probability ($1 - 1/n^c$), the rounded solution deviates from its expectation by at most a small multiplicative factor.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why LP Relaxation?** Because integer programs are NP-hard, but LPs are solvable in polynomial time. Relaxation gives an **upper bound** on the optimum (for minimization) or **lower bound** (for maximization).
- **Why random rounding and not greedy rounding?** Greedy rounding may lose multiplicative factors; randomized rounding preserves the **expected** objective value exactly (for linear objectives).
- **Why Chernoff/Azuma?** Random variables concentrate exponentially around their mean, allowing us to convert a *probabilistic guarantee* into a *worst-case* approximation guarantee.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Randomized Rounding & Martingales**

| # | Formula / Theorem | Statement |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linear Expectation** | $\mathbb{E}[\tilde{x}_i] = x_i^*$ |
| 2 | **Linearity of Expectation (Rounded Objective)** | $\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_i c_i \tilde{x}_i\right] = \sum_i c_i x_i^* = \text{OPT}_{LP}$ |
| 3 | **Chernoff Lower Tail** | $\Pr[X \leq (1-\varepsilon)\mu] \leq \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\varepsilon^2 \mu}{2}\right)$ |
| 4 | **Chernoff Upper Tail** | $\Pr[X \geq (1+\varepsilon)\mu] \leq \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\varepsilon^2 \mu}{3}\right)$ for $0<\varepsilon \leq 1$ |
| 5 | **Azuma-Hoeffding Inequality** | $\Pr[\vert X_n - X_0 \vert \geq \lambda] \leq 2 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\lambda^2}{2 \sum_{i=1}^n c_i^2}\right)$ |
| 6 | **Martingale Property** | $\mathbb{E}[X_{i+1} \mid X_i] = X_i$ |
| 7 | **Doob Martingale Construction** | $Y_i = \mathbb{E}[Z \mid X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_i]$ |
| 8 | **Expected Approximation Ratio (MAX-SAT)** | $\mathbb{E}[\text{SAT clauses}] \geq \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{e}\right)\text{OPT}$ |
| 9 | **Set Cover Approximation** | $\mathbb{E}[\text{Covered elements}] \geq \text{OPT} - \ln n$ |
| 10 | **Discrepancy Bound** | $\Pr[\vert D(X) \vert \geq \lambda] \leq 2 e^{-2\lambda^2/n}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** In the table above, the absolute-value bars use $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation (not the markdown pipe character) to avoid breaking table syntax.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Network Design (VLSI, Telecom):** Randomized rounding converts fractional routing probabilities into actual routing decisions for **packet switching** and **circuit placement**.
- **Cloud Resource Allocation:** LP relaxation gives fractional VM allocations; rounding produces discrete VM assignments with provable load-balancing guarantees.
- **Operations Research (Supply Chain):** Used in **vehicle routing** and **facility location** problems where exact solutions are computationally infeasible.
- **Combinatorial Auctions:** Determines winning bids from LP-derived fractional allocations in real-time bidding systems.
- **Machine Learning (Hyperparameter Tuning):** Probabilistic rounding techniques influence stochastic gradient methods.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Expected Value of a Rounded Linear Program

**Problem Setup.** Let $x^* = (x_1^*, x_2^*, \ldots, x_n^*)$ be the fractional optimum of the LP relaxation:

$$\text{Maximize } \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i x_i \quad \text{subject to } x_i \in [0,1].$$

Define the rounded variables $\tilde{x}_i$ independently as Bernoulli($x_i^*$).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express the expected value of a single rounded variable.**

$$\mathbb{E}[\tilde{x}_i] = 1 \cdot \Pr[\tilde{x}_i = 1] + 0 \cdot \Pr[\tilde{x}_i = 0] = 1 \cdot x_i^* + 0 \cdot (1 - x_i^*) = x_i^*.$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the linearity of expectation to the objective function.**

By linearity of expectation:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \tilde{x}_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \, \mathbb{E}[\tilde{x}_i].$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the per-variable expectation.**

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \tilde{x}_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i x_i^* = \text{OPT}_{LP}.$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Connect to the IP optimum.**

Since LP relaxation is an upper bound on the IP optimum for maximization:

$$\text{OPT}_{LP} \geq \text{OPT}_{IP} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mathbb{E}[\text{Rounded Objective}] \geq \text{OPT}_{IP}.$$

Thus, the rounded solution has an **expected value at least as good as the integer optimum**, which is the foundation of all randomized rounding approximation guarantees.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Chernoff Bound (Upper Tail) for Independent Bernoulli Variables

**Setup.** Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be independent Bernoulli($p_i$) random variables with $X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$, $\mu = \mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Markov's inequality on the moment generating function.**

For any $t > 0$:

$$\Pr[X \geq (1+\varepsilon)\mu] = \Pr[e^{tX} \geq e^{t(1+\varepsilon)\mu}] \leq \frac{\mathbb{E}[e^{tX}]}{e^{t(1+\varepsilon)\mu}}.$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Factor the expectation (independence).**

$$\mathbb{E}[e^{tX}] = \mathbb{E}\left[\prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{tX_i}\right] = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[e^{tX_i}].$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute per-term expectation.**

For Bernoulli($p_i$):

$$\mathbb{E}[e^{tX_i}] = (1 - p_i) \cdot 1 + p_i \cdot e^t = 1 + p_i(e^t - 1) \leq e^{p_i(e^t - 1)}.$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute and collect exponents.**

$$\prod_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[e^{tX_i}] \leq \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{p_i(e^t - 1)} = e^{(e^t - 1)\sum_i p_i} = e^{(e^t - 1)\mu}.$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Combine.**

$$\Pr[X \geq (1+\varepsilon)\mu] \leq \frac{e^{(e^t - 1)\mu}}{e^{t(1+\varepsilon)\mu}} = \exp\!\left[\mu\left(e^t - 1 - t(1+\varepsilon)\right)\right].$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Minimize over $t$.** Setting $t = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$ (for $0 < \varepsilon \leq 1$):

$$e^t - 1 - t(1+\varepsilon) = (1+\varepsilon) - 1 - (1+\varepsilon)\ln(1+\varepsilon) \leq -\frac{\varepsilon^2}{3}.$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Final bound.**

$$\boxed{\Pr[X \geq (1+\varepsilon)\mu] \leq \exp\!\left(-\frac{\varepsilon^2 \mu}{3}\right)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Azuma-Hoeffding Inequality for Martingales

**Setup.** Let $X_0, X_1, \ldots, X_n$ be a martingale with bounded differences:

$$\vert X_i - X_{i-1} \vert \leq c_i \quad \text{for all } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n.$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Bound the moment generating function of $X_i - X_{i-1}$.**

For any $t > 0$:

$$\mathbb{E}[e^{t(X_i - X_{i-1})} \mid X_0, \ldots, X_{i-1}] \leq \frac{e^{tc_i} + e^{-tc_i}}{2} \leq e^{t^2 c_i^2 / 2}.$$

The last step uses the convexity of $e^x$ combined with the Hoeffding lemma.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Iteratively apply the MGF bound.**

$$\mathbb{E}[e^{t(X_n - X_0)}] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{t(X_i - X_{i-1})}\right] \leq \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{t^2 c_i^2 / 2} = \exp\!\left(\frac{t^2}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i^2\right).$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Markov's inequality.**

$$\Pr[X_n - X_0 \geq \lambda] \leq e^{-t\lambda} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{t^2}{2}\sum_i c_i^2\right).$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Optimize over $t$.** Setting $t = \lambda / \sum_i c_i^2$:

$$\boxed{\Pr[\vert X_n - X_0 \vert \geq \lambda] \leq 2\exp\!\left(-\frac{\lambda^2}{2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i^2}\right)}$$

---

## 3.4 Application Derivation ŌĆö MAX-SAT via Randomized Rounding

**Problem.** Given clauses $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m$ on Boolean variables $y_1, \ldots, y_n$, maximize the number of simultaneously satisfied clauses.

**LP Relaxation.** Let $y_i \in [0,1]$ for each variable (interpreted as $\Pr[y_i = \text{True}]$). For each clause $C_j$ with variables indexed by $S_j$, the LP constraint is:

$$\sum_{i \in S_j^+} y_i + \sum_{i \in S_j^-} (1 - y_i) \geq z_j,$$

where $z_j$ is the fractional contribution of clause $C_j$. Solve the LP to get $y^*$ and $z^*$.

**Expected Satisfied Clauses (Linear Objective Case).**

For a single clause of size $k$ (e.g., $y_1 \lor y_2 \lor \ldots \lor y_k$), after randomized rounding:

$$\Pr[\text{Clause unsatisfied}] = \prod_{i=1}^{k}(1 - y_i^*) \leq \left(1 - \frac{1}{k}\right)^k \leq \frac{1}{e}.$$

**Summation.**

$$\mathbb{E}[\text{Satisfied clauses}] = \sum_{j=1}^{m} (1 - \Pr[C_j \text{ unsat}]) \geq \left(1 - \frac{1}{e}\right) \sum_{j} z_j^* = \left(1 - \frac{1}{e}\right) \text{OPT}_{LP} \geq \left(1 - \frac{1}{e}\right) \text{OPT}.$$

**Conclusion.** Randomized rounding yields a $\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{e}\right) \approx 0.632$-approximation for MAX-SAT.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Randomized Rounding Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Combinatorial Optimization Problem] --> B[Formulate Integer Program IP]
    B --> C[Relax to Linear Program LP]
    C --> D[Solve LP Polynomial Time]
    D --> E[Obtain Fractional Optimum x star]
    E --> F[Independent Randomized Rounding]
    F --> G[Rounded 0 or 1 Solution tilde x]
    G --> H[Apply Chernoff or Azuma Hoeffding]
    H --> I[High Probability Approximation Guarantee]
    I --> J[Output: Integral Approximate Solution]
```

## 4.2 Martingale Sequence Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X0[X0 initial value] -->|random step 1| X1[X1]
    X1 -->|random step 2| X2[X2]
    X2 -->|random step 3| X3[X3]
    X3 -->|random step n| Xn[Xn final value]
    X0 -.expected equals.-> X1
    X1 -.expected equals.-> X2
    X2 -.expected equals.-> X3
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Rounding Strategy Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Have LP solution x star] --> S1{Is objective linear?}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2[Use Independent Randomized Rounding]
    S1 -- No --> S3[Use Dependent Rounding or Alteration Method]
    S2 --> S4{Need derandomization?}
    S3 --> S4
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Apply Method of Conditional Expectations]
    S4 -- No --> S6[Output Probabilistic Solution]
    S5 --> S7[Output Deterministic Approximation]
```

## 4.4 Concentration Inequality Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[Have sum of random variables] --> Q1{Are variables independent?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Bounded by 0 1?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Construct Martingale]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4[Use Chernoff Bound]
    Q2 -- No --> Q5[Use Hoeffding Inequality]
    Q3 --> Q6[Use Azuma Hoeffding Inequality]
    Q4 --> Q7[Output Concentration Bound]
    Q5 --> Q7
    Q6 --> Q7
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**Define randomized rounding. State the Chernoff bound for upper tail deviations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Randomized Rounding (1 mark):** A probabilistic technique where each fractional LP variable $x_i^* \in [0,1]$ is independently rounded to $\tilde{x}_i \in \{0,1\}$ with $\Pr[\tilde{x}_i = 1] = x_i^*$.

**Chernoff Upper Tail (2 marks):** Let $X_1, \ldots, X_n$ be independent Bernoulli($p_i$) with $\mu = \sum p_i$. Then for $0 < \varepsilon \leq 1$:

$$\Pr[X \geq (1+\varepsilon)\mu] \leq \exp\!\left(-\frac{\varepsilon^2 \mu}{3}\right).$$

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`
**What is a martingale? Give the statement of Azuma-Hoeffding inequality.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Martingale (1.5 marks):** A sequence $\{X_i\}$ is a martingale with respect to filtration $\{\mathcal{F}_i\}$ if $\mathbb{E}[\vert X_i \vert] < \infty$ and $\mathbb{E}[X_{i+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_i] = X_i$.

**Azuma-Hoeffding Inequality (1.5 marks):** If $\vert X_i - X_{i-1} \vert \leq c_i$, then:

$$\Pr[\vert X_n - X_0 \vert \geq \lambda] \leq 2 \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lambda^2}{2 \sum_i c_i^2}\right).$$

---

## ­¤ōŚ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the randomized rounding technique in detail. Discuss its application in solving the **MAX-SAT problem** and prove that it achieves a $\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{e}\right)$-approximation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and prove the **Chernoff bound** (both upper and lower tail). Show its use in proving the approximation guarantee of randomized rounding for **Set Cover**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition (1 mark):** Randomized rounding maps fractional LP solutions $x_i^* \in [0,1]$ to discrete 0/1 solutions by independent Bernoulli trials with $\Pr[\tilde{x}_i = 1] = x_i^*$.

**MAX-SAT Formulation (1 mark):** Write each clause $C_j$ with positive literals indexed by $P_j$ and negative literals indexed by $N_j$. The LP relaxation sets:

$$\sum_{i \in P_j} y_i + \sum_{i \in N_j} (1 - y_i) \geq z_j, \quad 0 \leq y_i, z_j \leq 1.$$

**Probability that clause $C_j$ is unsatisfied (2 marks):** Independent rounding gives:

$$\Pr[C_j \text{ unsat}] = \prod_{i \in P_j}(1 - y_i^*) \cdot \prod_{i \in N_j} y_i^*.$$

Using AM-GM inequality and the LP constraint $\sum_i z_{ij}^* \geq z_j^*$, one shows $\Pr[C_j \text{ unsat}] \leq 1 - z_j^*$, hence:

$$\Pr[C_j \text{ sat}] \geq z_j^*.$$

**Summation and approximation (2 marks):**

$$\mathbb{E}[\text{Satisfied clauses}] = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \Pr[C_j \text{ sat}] \geq \sum_j z_j^* \geq \text{OPT}.$$

For clauses of size $k$, tighter analysis yields $\Pr[\text{unsat}] \leq (1-1/k)^k \leq 1/e$, giving the $\left(1 - 1/e\right)$ bound.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Therefore randomized rounding yields a $(1 - 1/e) \approx 0.632$-approximation for MAX-SAT.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Chernoff Upper Tail Statement (1 mark):** For $X = \sum X_i$ with $\mu = \mathbb{E}[X]$ and $0 < \varepsilon \leq 1$:

$$\Pr[X \geq (1+\varepsilon)\mu] \leq \exp\!\left(-\frac{\varepsilon^2 \mu}{3}\right).$$

**Proof (3 marks):** Apply Markov's inequality to $e^{tX}$, use independence to factor the MGF, compute the per-term expectation via Bernoulli, optimize over $t = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$, yielding the stated bound. (See Section 3.2 for full derivation.)

**Chernoff Lower Tail (1 mark):** Similarly,

$$\Pr[X \leq (1-\varepsilon)\mu] \leq \exp\!\left(-\frac{\varepsilon^2 \mu}{2}\right).$$

**Set Cover Application (2 marks):** Let each element $e$ be covered with probability $p_e$ by the LP. Define indicator $X_e = 1$ if covered after rounding. Then $X = \sum X_e$ has $\mu = \sum p_e \geq \text{OPT}$. Using Chernoff lower tail with $\varepsilon = 1/2$, with high probability $X \geq \mu/2 \geq \text{OPT}/2$, giving a 2-approximation (and $O(\ln n)$ using alteration method).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Define a **martingale**. Construct the **Doob martingale** for the randomized rounding process and show that it satisfies the martingale property. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and prove the **Azuma-Hoeffding inequality** for martingales. Apply it to analyze the **discrepancy of set systems** under randomized rounding. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Martingale Definition (1 mark):** A sequence $\{X_i\}_{i=0}^{n}$ is a martingale if $\mathbb{E}[\vert X_i \vert] < \infty$ and $\mathbb{E}[X_{i+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_i] = X_i$.

**Doob Martingale Construction (3 marks):** Let $Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_n$ be the independent Bernoulli random variables used in rounding. Define $Z$ to be the function we want to track (e.g., objective function value). The Doob martingale is:

$$M_i = \mathbb{E}[Z \mid Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_i], \quad M_0 = \mathbb{E}[Z].$$

**Verification of Martingale Property (3 marks):** For $i \geq 1$:

$$\mathbb{E}[M_{i+1} \mid Y_1, \ldots, Y_i] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\mathbb{E}[Z \mid Y_1, \ldots, Y_{i+1}] \,\Big|\, Y_1, \ldots, Y_i\right] = \mathbb{E}[Z \mid Y_1, \ldots, Y_i] = M_i.$$

(The first equality is the definition; the second uses the tower property of conditional expectation.) Thus $\{M_i\}$ is a martingale with respect to the filtration $\mathcal{F}_i = \sigma(Y_1, \ldots, Y_i)$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Azuma-Hoeffding Statement (1 mark):** If $\{M_i\}$ is a martingale with $\vert M_i - M_{i-1} \vert \leq c_i$, then:

$$\Pr[\vert M_n - M_0 \vert \geq \lambda] \leq 2 \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lambda^2}{2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i^2}\right).$$

**Proof Outline (3 marks):** Apply Hoeffding's lemma to bound $\mathbb{E}[e^{t(M_i - M_{i-1})} \mid \mathcal{F}_{i-1}] \leq e^{t^2 c_i^2/2}$. Iterate, apply Markov's inequality, and optimize over $t$. (See Section 3.3 for full derivation.)

**Discrepancy Application (3 marks):** Given a set system $\mathcal{S} = \{S_1, \ldots, S_n\}$ on ground set $[m]$, randomly color each element with $\pm 1$. The discrepancy of $S_j$ is:

$$D_j = \left\vert \sum_{i \in S_j} \chi_i \right\vert, \quad D = \max_j D_j.$$

Construct a martingale $M_k = \mathbb{E}[D \mid \chi_1, \ldots, \chi_k]$ with bounded differences $c_k \leq 1$. Azuma-Hoeffding gives:

$$\Pr[D - \mathbb{E}[D] \geq \lambda] \leq 2\exp\!\left(-\frac{\lambda^2}{2n}\right).$$

With $\mathbb{E}[D_j] \leq \sqrt{2 \vert S_j \vert \ln(2m)}$, we obtain $D = O(\sqrt{n \log m})$ w.h.p.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - **Do NOT confuse Chernoff with Azuma-Hoeffding.** Chernoff applies to *independent* Bernoulli sums; Azuma-Hoeffding applies to *martingales* with bounded differences.
> - **Direction of inequality matters.** Upper tail and lower tail have different constants ($1/3$ vs $1/2$); using the wrong one costs full marks.
> - **Filtration dependency.** When verifying the martingale property, students often forget to write $\mathbb{E}[\vert X_i \vert] < \infty$. Always state both conditions.
> - **Approximation ratio sign.** For maximization problems, $\mathbb{E}[\text{Alg}] \geq (1 - 1/e)\text{OPT}$; students sometimes invert the inequality.
> - **Notation clash with markdown.** Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX ŌĆö never the bare pipe symbol in tables.

---

## ­¤¦Ā Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Randomized Rounding & Martingales**

- Ō£ģ Randomized rounding converts LP fractional solutions $x_i^* \in [0,1]$ to integral $\tilde{x}_i \in \{0,1\}$ via independent Bernoulli trials.
- Ō£ģ The **expected value** of any linear objective is preserved exactly: $\mathbb{E}[\sum c_i \tilde{x}_i] = \sum c_i x_i^* = \text{OPT}_{LP}$.
- Ō£ģ **Chernoff bound** controls deviation for independent sums; constants are $\varepsilon^2\mu/3$ (upper) and $\varepsilon^2\mu/2$ (lower).
- Ō£ģ A **martingale** is a fair game: $\mathbb{E}[X_{i+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_i] = X_i$. Always check finiteness of expectation first.
- Ō£ģ **Doob martingale**: $M_i = \mathbb{E}[Z \mid Y_1, \ldots, Y_i]$ ŌĆö used to analyze any function of random bits.
- Ō£ģ **Azuma-Hoeffding inequality**: $\Pr[\vert M_n - M_0 \vert \geq \lambda] \leq 2 e^{-\lambda^2 / (2\sum c_i^2)}$.
- Ō£ģ **MAX-SAT** via randomized rounding: $\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{e}\right)$-approximation (Ōēł 0.632).
- Ō£ģ **Set Cover** via randomized rounding: $O(\ln n)$-approximation (and 2-approximation with alteration method).
- Ō£ģ **Discrepancy theory**: bounded by $O(\sqrt{n \log m})$ for $m$ sets over $n$ elements.
- Ō£ģ **Derandomization**: Method of Conditional Expectations converts probabilistic algorithms to deterministic ones.
- Ō£ģ Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ in LaTeX, never the markdown pipe `|` inside tables.
- Ō£ģ Always state **both** the conditional expectation equality and the integrability condition for martingales.
- Ō£ģ Mermaid node IDs must be alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`), never reserved words like `end` or `graph`.
- Ō£ģ KTU 2024 scheme stresses **martingale-based analysis** for non-independent rounding schemes ŌĆö be prepared.

---

*End of Notes ŌĆö Module 4: Randomized Rounding and Martingales*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications in Approximation Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST639 - RANDOMIZED ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications in Approximation Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Approximation Algorithm (KTU Definition):**
> For an NP-hard optimization problem $\Pi$ with objective function $c : \mathcal{S} \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$, an **approximation algorithm** $\mathcal{A}$ is a polynomial-time algorithm that, for every instance $I$, outputs a feasible solution $S \in \mathcal{S}(I)$ whose cost satisfies:
> $$\rho_{\mathcal{A}} \;=\; \sup_{I}\, \frac{c_{\mathcal{A}}(I)}{c_{\text{OPT}}(I)} \quad \text{(minimization)} \quad\text{or}\quad \rho_{\mathcal{A}} \;=\; \sup_{I}\, \frac{c_{\text{OPT}}(I)}{c_{\mathcal{A}}(I)} \quad \text{(maximization)}$$
> The value $\rho_{\mathcal{A}} \geq 1$ is the **approximation ratio**. A **randomized approximation algorithm** achieves this ratio *in expectation* (or with high probability).

### Key Complexity-Theoretic Classes

| Class | Definition | Significance in KTU Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **APX** | Problems admitting a constant-factor polynomial-time approximation | Most NP-hard optimization problems lie here |
| **PTAS** | Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme: $(1+\varepsilon)$-approx in time $n^{O(1/\varepsilon)}$ | Stronger than APX |
| **FPTAS** | Fully PTAS: $(1+\varepsilon)$-approx in time $f(1/\varepsilon) \cdot n^{O(1)}$ | Strongest tractable class |
| **APX-hard** | No PTAS unless $P = NP$ | Lower bound on approximability |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy ŌĆö "The Travelling Salesman on a Rainy Day"**
> Imagine you must visit **20 cities** in Kerala starting from Kochi and returning home, minimizing travel cost. The optimal tour requires checking $19! \approx 10^{17}$ permutations ŌĆö impossible.
> A **deterministic approximation** might use the nearest-neighbor heuristic (greedy). A **randomized approximation**, however, **samples a few permutations uniformly at random** and keeps the cheapest one. The randomness lets us *probe the solution space efficiently* and provably guarantees a tour no worse than a fixed factor of optimum.
>
> **The deep insight:** When exact computation is intractable ($NP$-hardness), randomness acts as a *statistical lens* ŌĆö it does not find the optimum, but it produces a **certifiably good** solution with provable bounds.

---

## 1.3 Why Randomization Helps in Approximation

Three principal reasons appear repeatedly in KTU-level treatment of Module 4:

1. **Symmetry breaking** ŌĆö random assignments balance objective terms (used in MAX-CUT, MAX-SAT).
2. **Probabilistic existence proofs** ŌĆö method of conditional expectations converts random solutions to deterministic ones (derandomization).
3. **Concentration** ŌĆö Chernoff/Hoeffding bounds sharpen expected guarantees to high-probability guarantees.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU-Style Memory Hook:** *"Randomness doesn't solve NP-hardness ŌĆö it **certifies** the price we pay for not solving it."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Core Algorithmic Patterns

Randomized approximation algorithms in KTU Module 4 fall into **three canonical patterns**:

### Pattern A ŌĆö Random Assignment (Expectation Bound)
Assign each decision variable independently and uniformly. Use **linearity of expectation** to derive the average cost.

### Pattern B ŌĆö Randomized Rounding
Solve a Linear Programming (LP) relaxation, then round fractional variables randomly to integers.

### Pattern C ŌĆö Greedy + Random Sampling
Make locally optimal choices, occasionally perturbed by randomness (e.g., random-priority greedy for Set Cover).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Result | Formula / Bound | Conditions | Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MAX-CUT random expectation | $\mathbb{E}[\vert C \vert] \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \vert E \vert$ | Random $x_v \in \{0,1\}$ independently | **High (10M)** |
| 2 | MAX-SAT 3-CNF | $\mathbb{E}[\#\text{clauses}] \geq \tfrac{7}{8} m$ | Random truth assignment | **High (10M)** |
| 3 | Set Cover (greedy) | $C_{\text{ALG}} \leq H_n \cdot C_{\text{OPT}}$ | $H_n = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2} + \cdots + \tfrac{1}{n}$ | **High (14M)** |
| 4 | Vertex Cover random | $\mathbb{E}[\text{cover}] \leq 2 \cdot \text{OPT}$ | Pick each vertex with prob $\tfrac{1}{2}$ | **Medium (7M)** |
| 5 | Chernoff Bound | $\Pr[\vert X - \mu \vert \geq \delta\mu] \leq 2e^{-\delta^{2}\mu/3}$ | $X = \sum X_i$ sum of independent indicators | **High (10M)** |
| 6 | MAX-CUT GoemansŌĆōWilliamson | SDP relaxation gives $\alpha_{GW} \approx 0.8785$ | Requires SDP rounding | **Optional** |
| 7 | Derandomization | Conditional expectation $\Phi_0 \leq \Phi_1 \leq \cdots$ | Yields deterministic algorithm | **Medium** |
| 8 | Discrepancy Theory | $\mathbb{E}[\text{cut}(A)] = \tfrac{m}{2}$ | $A$ random subset of vertices | **Low (3M)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** The bound $\mathbb{E}[\vert C \vert] = \tfrac{m}{2}$ for MAX-CUT is *deceptively simple* ŌĆö but its derivation via indicator random variables and linearity of expectation is **frequently asked for 7ŌĆō14 marks**. Master it cold.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Problem | Approximation Used |
|---|---|---|
| **VLSI Circuit Design** | MAX-CUT | Partitioning for chip layout |
| **Wireless Sensor Networks** | Set Cover / Vertex Cover | Minimum relay placement |
| **Bioinformatics** | MAX-SAT | Protein structure prediction |
| **Logistics / Ride-sharing** | TSP | Route optimization at scale |
| **Cloud Computing** | Load Balancing / Bin Packing | Server consolidation |
| **Database Query Optimization** | Join ordering | Cost-based approximation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Mandate:** Marks are awarded for *each algebraic step*. Skipping a line of derivation costs **1ŌĆō2 marks per gap**. Every expectation below is fully expanded.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I ŌĆö MAX-CUT Random Assignment (Expected Cut Size)

**Problem.** Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ with $\vert V \vert = n$ vertices and $\vert E \vert = m$ edges, partition $V$ into two sets $S, \bar{S}$ so as to **maximize** the number of edges crossing the cut $\delta(S) = \{uv \in E : u \in S,\, v \notin S\}$.

**Randomized Algorithm.**
For each vertex $v \in V$, independently toss a fair coin. Set
$$x_v = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{with probability } \tfrac{1}{2} \\ 0 & \text{with probability } \tfrac{1}{2} \end{cases}$$
Let $S = \{v : x_v = 1\}$. Return $(S, V \setminus S)$.

**Derivation.** For each edge $e = uv \in E$, define the indicator random variable
$$X_{uv} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } uv \in \delta(S) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
The total cut size is $\vert C \vert = \sum_{e \in E} X_e$. By linearity of expectation:
$$\mathbb{E}[\vert C \vert] \;=\; \sum_{e \in E} \mathbb{E}[X_e]$$

For a fixed edge $uv$, the edge crosses the cut **iff** $x_u \neq x_v$. The four equally likely outcomes of $(x_u, x_v)$ are $(0,0), (0,1), (1,0), (1,1)$, so
$$\Pr[x_u \neq x_v] \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}$$

Therefore $\mathbb{E}[X_e] = \tfrac{1}{2}$ for every edge, giving
$$\mathbb{E}[\vert C \vert] \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \sum_{e \in E} 1 \;=\; \tfrac{m}{2}$$

Since $\text{OPT} \leq m$, we conclude
$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{\text{OPT}}{\vert C \vert}\right] \;\geq\; \frac{m}{m/2} \;=\; 2$$

$$\boxed{\;\mathbb{E}[\text{cut size}] \;\geq\; \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \text{OPT}\;}$$

This is a **$\tfrac{1}{2}$-approximation** in expectation (a $2$-approximation factor for the ratio $\text{OPT}/\text{ALG}$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation II ŌĆö MAX-SAT for 3-CNF Formulas (The $\tfrac{7}{8}$ Bound)

**Problem.** Given a 3-CNF formula $\varphi = C_1 \wedge C_2 \wedge \cdots \wedge C_m$ on $n$ Boolean variables, find an assignment maximizing the number of **satisfied clauses**.

**Randomized Algorithm.** For each variable $x_i$, set $x_i = 1$ independently with probability $\tfrac{1}{2}$, and $x_i = 0$ with probability $\tfrac{1}{2}$.

**Derivation.** A clause of size $k$ is unsatisfied **only if all $k$ of its literals are false**. Since each literal is independently true with probability $\tfrac{1}{2}$, the probability a $k$-clause is unsatisfied is $\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{k}$.

For a clause $C_j$ of size $k_j$, define indicator $Y_j = \mathbf{1}[C_j \text{ satisfied}]$. Then:
$$\mathbb{E}[Y_j] \;=\; 1 - \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{k_j}$$

By linearity of expectation over all $m$ clauses:
$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{j=1}^{m} Y_j\right] \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left[1 - \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{k_j}\right]$$

For **3-CNF**, every clause has $k_j = 3$, so $\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^3 = \tfrac{1}{8}$. Therefore:
$$\mathbb{E}[\text{\# satisfied}] \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left(1 - \tfrac{1}{8}\right) \;=\; \tfrac{7}{8}\, m$$

Since $\text{OPT} \leq m$:
$$\mathbb{E}[\text{ALG}] \;=\; \tfrac{7}{8}\, m \;\geq\; \tfrac{7}{8} \cdot \text{OPT}$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{Expected } 3\text{-SAT approximation ratio} \;\geq\; \tfrac{7}{8}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **H├źstad's Theorem (1997):** Unless $P = NP$, the $\tfrac{7}{8}$ factor cannot be improved. This is the **optimal inapproximability** result and is frequently referenced in KTU seminars.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III ŌĆö Set Cover via Randomized Greedy ($\ln n$ Bound)

**Problem.** Given a universe $\mathcal{U}$ of $n$ elements and a family of sets $\mathcal{S} = \{S_1, \dots, S_k\}$ with $\bigcup S_i = \mathcal{U}$, find the minimum-cardinality sub-family covering $\mathcal{U}$.

**Randomized Greedy Sketch.** While elements remain uncovered, pick a set $S_i$ with probability proportional to the number of uncovered elements it contains.

**Expected-Cost Bound.** Let $C^*$ be the optimum cover with $\vert C^* \vert = k^*$. Order the OPT sets arbitrarily as $O_1, O_2, \dots, O_{k^*}$. Let $r_i$ be the number of elements newly covered when $O_i$ is added.

Standard analysis (MotwaniŌĆōRaghavanŌĆōTompa) gives:
$$\mathbb{E}[\text{greedy cost}] \;\leq\; H_n \cdot k^* \;=\; \left(1 + \tfrac{1}{2} + \cdots + \tfrac{1}{n}\right) \cdot \text{OPT}$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{Set Cover randomized greedy} \;\leq\; H_n \cdot \text{OPT}\;}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV ŌĆö Vertex Cover via Random Sampling (2-Approximation)

**Problem.** Given $G = (V, E)$, find minimum $\vert C \vert \subseteq V$ such that every edge has at least one endpoint in $C$.

**Algorithm.** Include each vertex $v \in V$ in the cover independently with probability $\tfrac{1}{2}$. For any edge $uv$ not covered, add both $u$ and $v$.

**Expected cost:**
$$\mathbb{E}[\vert \text{final cover} \vert] \;\leq\; \mathbb{E}[\text{initial sample}] + \Pr[\text{edge uncovered}]
\leq \tfrac{n}{2} + \Pr[uv \text{ uncovered}]$$

Since each edge is uncovered only if **both** endpoints are missed (probability $\tfrac{1}{4}$), the correction term is small, and the final bound is
$$\mathbb{E}[\vert C_{\text{ALG}} \vert] \;\leq\; 2 \cdot \text{OPT}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Side-by-Side Comparison

| Problem | Random Method | Expected Ratio | Tight? | Derandomizable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| MAX-CUT | Random 2-coloring | $\tfrac{1}{2}$ | Yes (trivial graphs) | Ō£ģ Conditional expectation |
| MAX-3-SAT | Random assignment | $\tfrac{7}{8}$ | Yes (H├źstad) | Ō£ģ Conditional expectation |
| Set Cover | Randomized greedy | $H_n \approx \ln n$ | Yes (Feige) | Ō£ģ Deterministic greedy |
| Vertex Cover | Random sampling | $2$ | Yes (trivial) | Ō£ģ LP rounding |
| TSP (metric) | Christofides | $1.5$ | No (open) | ŌØī Not randomized |
| MAX-CUT (SDP) | GoemansŌĆōWilliamson | $0.8785$ | Conjectured | Ō£ģ Deterministic |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow ŌĆö Generic Randomized Approximation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Instance I] --> B[Identify Optimization Problem P]
    B --> C[Choose Randomization Strategy]
    C --> D1[Strategy 1: Random Assignment]
    C --> D2[Strategy 2: Randomized Rounding]
    C --> D3[Strategy 3: Random Sampling / Greedy]
    D1 --> E[Generate Candidate Solution S]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Compute Cost c S]
    F --> G{Apply Expectation Analysis}
    G --> H[Linearity of Expectation]
    G --> I[Chernoff Hoeffding Concentration]
    H --> J[Derive Approximation Ratio rho]
    I --> J
    J --> K{rho Acceptable?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Return Expected Bound]
    K -- No --> M[Refine via Derandomization]
    M --> N[Method of Conditional Expectations]
    N --> O[Deterministic Final Algorithm]
    L --> P[Output: Certified Approximation]
    O --> P
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block ŌĆö MAX-CUT Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Input Stage]
        I1[Graph G with n vertices and m edges]
    end

    subgraph Randomization[Randomization Stage]
        R1[For each vertex v in V]
        R2[Toss fair coin independently]
        R3[Set x_v = 0 or 1 with prob 1/2]
    end

    subgraph CutConstruction[Cut Construction Stage]
        C1[Partition V into S and V minus S]
        C2[Compute crossing edges delta S]
    end

    subgraph Analysis[Analytical Stage]
        A1[Indicator X_e per edge]
        A2[Linearity of expectation]
        A3[E cut = m divided by 2]
    end

    I1 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block ŌĆö Set Cover Randomized Greedy Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[Universe U of n elements] --> F[Family of sets S1 to Sk]
    F --> Loop{All elements covered?}
    Loop -- No --> Pick[Sample set Si with probability proportional to uncovered elements it contains]
    Pick --> Add[Add Si to Cover C]
    Add --> Loop
    Loop -- Yes --> End[Output Cover C]
    End --> Bound[Compute E C less than equal to H_n times OPT]
```

## 4.4 Derandomization Pipeline (Conditional Expectation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Random Solution X with E Phi X] --> F[Initialize assignment empty]
    F --> V[For each variable xi in order]
    V --> Q{Compute Phi conditional expectations}
    Q --> Phi0[Phi = E Phi vert xi = 0]
    Q --> Phi1[Phi = E Phi vert xi = 1]
    Phi0 --> Cmp[Choose value with smaller conditional expectation]
    Phi1 --> Cmp
    Cmp --> Fix[Fix xi to chosen value]
    Fix --> V
    V -- All variables fixed --> Final[Deterministic solution with Phi less than equal to E Phi X]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define approximation ratio for a maximization problem. State the approximation ratio achieved by the randomized algorithm for MAX-CUT.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
For a maximization problem with optimum value $\text{OPT}(I)$ and algorithm value $\text{ALG}(I)$, the **approximation ratio** is defined as
$$\rho \;=\; \min_{I}\, \frac{\text{ALG}(I)}{\text{OPT}(I)}$$
For MAX-CUT, a random 2-coloring achieves $\mathbb{E}[\text{cut}] = \tfrac{m}{2}$, so since $\text{OPT} \leq m$,
$$\rho_{\text{MAX-CUT}}^{\text{random}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write $\rho = 2$ (for minimization convention). For **maximization**, $\rho \leq 1$. Marks lost if confused.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the expected fraction of clauses satisfied by a random assignment for a 3-CNF formula? State the corresponding inapproximability result.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Random assignment satisfies each 3-clause with probability $1 - (1/2)^3 = 7/8$, so the expected number of satisfied clauses is $\tfrac{7}{8} m$, giving
$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{\text{ALG}}{\text{OPT}}\right] \;\geq\; \tfrac{7}{8}$$
By **H├źstad's theorem (1997)**, no polynomial-time algorithm can achieve ratio exceeding $7/8 + \varepsilon$ for any $\varepsilon > 0$, unless $P = NP$.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: each Part-B question carries **14 marks** split into sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). **Internal choice between Question A and Question B** is mandatory.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** *Describe a randomized algorithm for the MAX-CUT problem. Prove rigorously that the expected size of the cut produced is at least $\tfrac{m}{2}$, where $m$ is the number of edges.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Using Chernoff bounds, show how to amplify the success probability so that the algorithm returns a cut of size at least $\tfrac{m}{2}(1 - \delta)$ with probability at least $1 - \tfrac{1}{n^c}$ for any constant $c$.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Algorithm Description (3 Marks)**
Given $G = (V, E)$ with $\vert V \vert = n$ and $\vert E \vert = m$:
1. For each vertex $v_i$, independently assign $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ uniformly at random.
2. Define $S = \{v_i : x_i = 1\}$ and $\bar{S} = V \setminus S$.
3. Return cut $\delta(S) = \{uv \in E : x_u \neq x_v\}$.

**Proof of Expectation Bound (4 Marks):**
For each edge $e = uv$, define indicator $X_{uv} = \mathbf{1}[x_u \neq x_v]$.
$$\Pr[X_{uv} = 1] \;=\; \Pr[(x_u, x_v) \in \{(0,1), (1,0)\}] \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}$$

By linearity of expectation over all $m$ edges:
$$\mathbb{E}[\vert \delta(S) \vert] \;=\; \sum_{e \in E} \mathbb{E}[X_e] \;=\; m \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \;=\; \tfrac{m}{2}$$

Since $\text{OPT} \leq m$, we obtain the **$\tfrac{1}{2}$-approximation** in expectation. ŌłÄ

**(b) Chernoff Amplification (7 Marks)**
Let $X = \sum_{e \in E} X_e$ with $\mu = \mathbb{E}[X] = m/2$. The $X_e$ are independent Bernoulli with parameter $1/2$.

Apply the **multiplicative Chernoff bound** for upper deviation:
$$\Pr[X \leq (1 - \delta)\mu] \;\leq\; \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{\delta^2 \mu}{2}\right)$$

Substituting $\mu = m/2$:
$$\Pr[X \leq (1-\delta)\tfrac{m}{2}] \;\leq\; \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{\delta^2 m}{4}\right)$$

To achieve failure probability $\leq \tfrac{1}{n^c}$, we require
$$\exp\!\left(-\tfrac{\delta^2 m}{4}\right) \;\leq\; n^{-c} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad m \;\geq\; \tfrac{4c \ln n}{\delta^2}$$

Thus for any constant $c > 0$, choosing $\delta$ such that $m \geq 4c\ln n / \delta^2$ (which holds for any dense enough graph), we obtain
$$\Pr\!\left[\vert \delta(S) \vert \;\geq\; \tfrac{m}{2}(1-\delta)\right] \;\geq\; 1 - \tfrac{1}{n^c}$$

This amplifies the constant-probability guarantee into an *overwhelmingly high probability* one. ŌłÄ

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** *State and prove the randomized $\tfrac{7}{8}$-approximation algorithm for MAX-3-SAT. Show all steps using linearity of expectation.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the method of conditional expectations to derandomize the MAX-SAT algorithm. Show that the deterministic algorithm achieves at least $\tfrac{7}{8}$ of the clauses.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) MAX-3-SAT Randomized Algorithm (7 Marks)**

**Algorithm:**
1. Input: 3-CNF formula $\varphi$ with $m$ clauses over variables $x_1, \dots, x_n$.
2. For each $x_i$, independently set $x_i \leftarrow 1$ with probability $1/2$ (else $0$).
3. Return the random assignment.

**Proof of $\tfrac{7}{8}$ Bound:**

A clause $C_j$ of size $k_j$ is unsatisfied only when all its $k_j$ literals evaluate to false. Since each literal is independently true with probability $\tfrac{1}{2}$:
$$\Pr[C_j \text{ unsatisfied}] \;=\; \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{k_j}$$

Define indicator $Y_j = \mathbf{1}[C_j \text{ satisfied}]$. Then
$$\mathbb{E}[Y_j] \;=\; 1 - \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{k_j}$$

By linearity of expectation:
$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{j=1}^{m} Y_j\right] \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left[1 - \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{k_j}\right]$$

For 3-CNF, $k_j = 3$ for all $j$:
$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\#\text{satisfied}\right] \;=\; m \cdot \left(1 - \tfrac{1}{8}\right) \;=\; \tfrac{7m}{8}$$

Since $\text{OPT} \leq m$:
$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{\text{ALG}}{\text{OPT}}\right] \;\geq\; \tfrac{7}{8}$$

Hence the algorithm achieves a $\tfrac{7}{8}$-approximation in expectation. ŌłÄ

**(b) Derandomization via Conditional Expectations (7 Marks)**

Let $\Phi(x_1, \dots, x_n) = \sum_{j=1}^{m} Y_j$ be the number of satisfied clauses (a *potential function*).

**Key Property:** For any partial assignment $(a_1, \dots, a_{k-1})$,
$$\min\!\left\{\mathbb{E}[\Phi \mid x_k = 0],\; \mathbb{E}[\Phi \mid x_k = 1]\right\} \;\leq\; \mathbb{E}[\Phi \mid \text{partial}]$$

**Deterministic Algorithm:**
1. Set $\Phi_0 = \mathbb{E}[\Phi]$ over all $2^n$ assignments.
2. For $k = 1$ to $n$:
   - Compute $A_k = \mathbb{E}[\Phi \mid x_1, \dots, x_{k-1}, x_k = 0]$
   - Compute $B_k = \mathbb{E}[\Phi \mid x_1, \dots, x_{k-1}, x_k = 1]$
   - Fix $x_k = 0$ if $A_k \geq B_k$, else fix $x_k = 1$.
3. After $n$ steps, return the deterministic assignment.

**Correctness:** At each step, the conditional expectation is **non-increasing**. After fixing all $n$ variables, the conditional expectation equals the actual value at the chosen assignment:
$$\Phi(\text{final}) \;\geq\; \mathbb{E}[\Phi] \;\geq\; \tfrac{7m}{8} \;\geq\; \tfrac{7}{8} \cdot \text{OPT}$$

Thus the deterministic algorithm achieves at least $\tfrac{7}{8}$ of the optimum ŌĆö without any randomness! ŌłÄ

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Module 4 ŌĆö Approximation Algorithms):**
> 1. **Sign confusion in MAX-CUT ratio** ŌĆö students write the ratio as $2$ instead of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ for maximization. **ŌłÆ2 marks**.
> 2. **Skipping linearity-of-expectation expansion** ŌĆö the sentence "by linearity of expectation, $E[X] = m/2$" alone is **insufficient**. You must write the explicit sum $\sum_{e} \mathbb{E}[X_e]$ and justify independence if used. **ŌłÆ1 to ŌłÆ2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting $\text{OPT} \leq m$** for MAX-CUT, MAX-SAT ŌĆö the bound is on $\text{ALG}/\text{OPT}$, not on $m$ alone. **ŌłÆ1 mark**.
> 4. **Derandomization question** ŌĆö students often *describe* the method without showing the **non-increasing invariant**. Examiners want the formal chain $\Phi_0 \geq \Phi_1 \geq \cdots \geq \Phi_n$. **ŌłÆ3 marks** if missing.
> 5. **Chernoff bound exponent** ŌĆö the constant in $e^{-\delta^2 \mu / c}$ varies ($c = 2$ or $3$ depending on the form). Always state which version you are using. **ŌłÆ1 mark** otherwise.
> 6. **H├źstad's inapproximability theorem** is *bonus credit* if cited. **+1 mark** in valuation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 ŌĆö Applications in Approximation Algorithms):**

- [x] **Approximation ratio** for maximization is $\leq 1$; for minimization is $\geq 1$.
- [x] **MAX-CUT** random 2-coloring gives $\mathbb{E}[\text{cut}] = m/2$ ŌåÆ **1/2-approximation**.
- [x] **MAX-3-SAT** random assignment gives $\mathbb{E} = 7m/8$ ŌåÆ **7/8-approximation**, tight by H├źstad.
- [x] **Vertex Cover** randomized gives **2-approximation** in expectation.
- [x] **Set Cover** randomized greedy gives **$H_n$-approximation** where $H_n \approx \ln n + \gamma$.
- [x] **Linearity of expectation** is the workhorse ŌĆö always expand $\sum \mathbb{E}[X_i]$ explicitly.
- [x] **Chernoff bound**: $\Pr[X \leq (1-\delta)\mu] \leq e^{-\delta^2\mu/2}$; use for amplification.
- [x] **Derandomization via conditional expectation**: maintains invariant $\Phi_k \leq \Phi_{k-1}$.
- [x] **GoemansŌĆōWilliamson SDP** achieves **0.8785** for MAX-CUT ŌĆö non-trivial improvement.
- [x] **PTAS, FPTAS, APX, APX-hard** are the four classes to recognize in MCQs.
- [x] **H├źstad (1997)** = optimal 7/8 inapproximability for MAX-3-SAT (cite for +1 mark).
- [x] Always state: **"OPT $\leq m$"** for MAX-CUT, **"OPT $\leq m$"** for MAX-SAT before concluding ratio.
- [x] For KTU Part B, **derive** the bound, then **state** the ratio ŌĆö order matters for marks.

---

**End of Module 4 ŌĆö Applications in Approximation Algorithms Notes** Ō£ģ

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Martingales
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST639 - RANDOMIZED ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Martingales

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Martingale

Let $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$ be a probability space and let $\mathcal{F}_1 \subseteq \mathcal{F}_2 \subseteq \dots \subseteq \mathcal{F}$ be a filtration (an increasing sequence of $\sigma$-algebras representing the information available up to time $n$). A sequence of random variables $Z_0, Z_1, Z_2, \dots$ is called a **martingale** with respect to the filtration $\{\mathcal{F}_n\}$ if, for every $n \geq 0$:

$$E \left[ \, \mid Z_n \mid \, \right] < \infty$$

$$E[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = Z_n$$

The first condition guarantees that the expectation exists and is finite, while the second condition captures the essence of a **fair game**: given all the information up to time $n$, the best prediction (conditional expectation) of tomorrow's value $Z_{n+1}$ is exactly today's value $Z_n$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST639 Module 4):** A martingale is the probabilistic abstraction of a fair game. The defining identity $E[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = Z_n$ is the central object used to derive concentration inequalities like **AzumaŌĆōHoeffding**, which are direct exam favourites.

### 1.2 Related Notions ŌĆö Supermartingale and Submartingale

| Concept | Conditional Expectation Identity | Intuition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Martingale** | $E[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = Z_n$ | A fair game; expected fortune does not change. |
| **Supermartingale** | $E[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] \leq Z_n$ | A losing game; expected fortune decreases. |
| **Submartingale** | $E[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] \geq Z_n$ | A winning game; expected fortune increases. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Casino Gambler

Imagine a gambler playing **roulette** at a fair casino. At every step $n$, the gambler bets $\$1$ on red:

- With probability $\tfrac{1}{2}$: gambler wins $\$1$ ŌåÆ fortune becomes $Z_{n+1} = Z_n + 1$.
- With probability $\tfrac{1}{2}$: gambler loses $\$1$ ŌåÆ fortune becomes $Z_{n+1} = Z_n - 1$.

Given the entire past history of wins and losses (which is what $\mathcal{F}_n$ encodes), the expected next fortune is:

$$E[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = \tfrac{1}{2}(Z_n + 1) + \tfrac{1}{2}(Z_n - 1) = Z_n$$

The fortune process $\{Z_n\}$ is therefore a martingale. **No betting strategy, however clever, can change this expected value** ŌĆö this is the famous *no free lunch* theorem that martingales make rigorous.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Martingales Matter in Algorithm Design:** Randomized algorithms analyse quantities like "deviation of running time" or "error probability" using martingales because a martingale automatically removes drift, leaving only the noise term. This is the gateway to **tail bounds** (AzumaŌĆōHoeffding, Chernoff) that quantify *how unlikely* a randomized algorithm is to deviate from its expected behaviour.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Definition

Let us dissect the two requirements of a martingale step by step.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Integrability Condition.** $E[\vert Z_n \vert] < \infty$. This guarantees that the conditional expectation $E[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n]$ is well-defined (by the RadonŌĆōNikodym theorem). In KTU problems this is rarely violated because the random variables we encounter (Bernoulli sums, bounded quantities) are almost always bounded.
