**Step 2 ŌĆö The Fair-Game Identity.** $E[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = Z_n$. This identity must hold for **every** $n$. It says that knowledge of the past does not improve our forecast of the future beyond the present value.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Filtration is Crucial.** The martingale is defined *with respect to* a specific filtration. Often the natural filtration is used: $\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(Z_1, Z_2, \dots, Z_n)$, the $\sigma$-algebra generated by the first $n$ observations.

### 2.2 Canonical Examples Used in KTU Problems

**Example A ŌĆö Gambler's Fortune (Symmetric Random Walk).**
Let $X_1, X_2, \dots$ be i.i.d. with $P(X_i = +1) = P(X_i = -1) = \tfrac{1}{2}$. Define $Z_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$. Then $\{Z_n\}$ is a martingale w.r.t. the natural filtration.

**Example B ŌĆö Product of Independent Random Variables.**
Let $Y_1, Y_2, \dots$ be i.i.d. with $E[Y_i] = 1$. Define $Z_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$. Then $\{Z_n\}$ is a martingale.

**Example C ŌĆö Doob's Martingale.**
Given arbitrary random variables $X_1, \dots, X_n$ and a function $f$, define

$$Z_k = E\!\left[\, f(X_1, \dots, X_n) \;\big|\; X_1, \dots, X_k \,\right]$$

Then $\{Z_k\}_{k=0}^{n}$ is a martingale w.r.t. $\mathcal{F}_k = \sigma(X_1, \dots, X_k)$. This is the *swiss-army knife* of algorithmic applications of martingales.

### 2.3 Martingale Difference Sequences

A sequence $D_1, D_2, \dots$ is a **martingale difference sequence** if $E[D_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = 0$. Equivalently, if we set $Z_n = Z_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i$, then $\{Z_n\}$ is a martingale. Difference sequences are often easier to work with because they isolate the random fluctuation from any deterministic drift.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| \# | Identity / Theorem | Statement | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Martingale Definition** | $E[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = Z_n$ | Verify a sequence is a martingale. |
| 2 | **Integrability** | $E[\vert Z_n \vert] < \infty$ | Pre-condition for martingale property. |
| 3 | **Taking Expectations** | $E[Z_{n+1}] = E[Z_n] = \dots = E[Z_0]$ | Show constant mean over time. |
| 4 | **Optional Stopping Theorem (OST)** | If $T$ is a bounded stopping time and $\{Z_n\}$ is a martingale, then $E[Z_T] = E[Z_0]$. | Bound expected hitting time, expected cost. |
| 5 | **AzumaŌĆōHoeffding Inequality** | If $\vert Z_k - Z_{k-1} \vert \leq c_k$, then $P(\vert Z_n - Z_0 \vert \geq t) \leq 2\exp\!\left(-\tfrac{t^2}{2 \sum_{k=1}^{n} c_k^2}\right)$. | Concentration bounds for randomized algorithms. |
| 6 | **Doob's Martingale Construction** | $Z_k = E[f(X) \mid X_1, \dots, X_k]$ is a martingale. | Bounding the deviation of $f$ from its mean. |
| 7 | **SLLN via Martingales** | If $\{Z_n\}$ is a martingale with bounded increments, then $\tfrac{Z_n}{n} \to 0$ a.s. (Kolmogorov's form). | Limit results for sum of fair games. |
| 8 | **Sums of i.i.d. Zero-Mean RVs** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ is a martingale if $E[X_i] = 0$. | Symmetric random walk, algorithmic noise. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to *prove* something is a martingale, never just write the conditional expectation identity. You **must** also verify that $E[\vert Z_n \vert] < \infty$. Forgetting this costs 1ŌĆō2 marks routinely.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Communication Networks:** Modelling packet-delay processes and queueing dynamics.
- **Financial Engineering:** Option pricing (the original BlackŌĆōScholes framework is built on a martingale after a measure change).
- **Randomized Algorithm Analysis:** Deviation bounds for QuickSort, hashing occupancy, online paging.
- **Cryptography & Probabilistic Proofs:** Showing that certain randomized protocols leak no information.
- **Stochastic Control:** Reinforcement learning uses martingale difference sequences for policy-gradient estimators.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Symmetric Random Walk is a Martingale

**Problem.** Let $X_1, X_2, \dots$ be i.i.d. random variables with $P(X_i = +1) = P(X_i = -1) = \tfrac{1}{2}$. Define $Z_0 = 0$ and $Z_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$. Prove that $\{Z_n\}$ is a martingale w.r.t. the natural filtration $\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(X_1, \dots, X_n)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Verify Integrability.**
Since $\vert X_i \vert = 1$ for all $i$, we have $\vert Z_n \vert \leq n$, and therefore $E[\vert Z_n \vert] \leq n < \infty$. Ō£ō

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the Conditional Expectation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n]
&= E\!\left[\, Z_n + X_{n+1} \;\big|\; \mathcal{F}_n \,\right] & \text{(definition of } Z_{n+1}\text{)} \\
&= Z_n + E[X_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] & \text{(since } Z_n \text{ is } \mathcal{F}_n\text{-measurable)} \\
&= Z_n + E[X_{n+1}] & \text{(by independence of } X_{n+1} \text{ from } \mathcal{F}_n\text{)} \\
&= Z_n + \left[\, (+1)\cdot\tfrac{1}{2} + (-1)\cdot\tfrac{1}{2} \,\right] & \text{(using the distribution of } X_{n+1}\text{)} \\
&= Z_n + 0 = Z_n. & \text{(zero-mean of } X_{n+1}\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since both conditions hold, $\{Z_n\}$ is a martingale. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Product Martingale

**Problem.** Let $Y_1, Y_2, \dots$ be i.i.d. non-negative random variables with $E[Y_i] = 1$. Show that $Z_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ is a martingale.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Integrability.** By independence and non-negativity,

$$E[Z_n] = \prod_{i=1}^{n} E[Y_i] = 1^n = 1 < \infty. \;\checkmark$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Conditional Expectation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n]
&= E\!\left[\, Z_n \cdot Y_{n+1} \;\big|\; \mathcal{F}_n \,\right] & \text{(factorization)} \\
&= Z_n \cdot E[Y_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] & \text{(since } Z_n \text{ is } \mathcal{F}_n\text{-measurable)} \\
&= Z_n \cdot E[Y_{n+1}] & \text{(independence)} \\
&= Z_n \cdot 1 = Z_n. & \text{(unit mean assumption)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $\{Z_n\}$ is a martingale. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Verifying Doob's Construction

**Problem.** Given $X_1, \dots, X_n$ with $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{F}_k = \sigma(X_1, \dots, X_k)$ and any bounded function $f$, prove that

$$Z_k = E\!\left[\, f(X_1, \dots, X_n) \;\big|\; \mathcal{F}_k \,\right]$$

is a martingale w.r.t. $\{\mathcal{F}_k\}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Integrability.** Since $f$ is bounded, say $\vert f \vert \leq M$,

$$E[\vert Z_k \vert] = E\!\left[\, \big\vert E[f \mid \mathcal{F}_k] \big\vert \,\right] \leq E\!\left[\, E[\vert f \vert \mid \mathcal{F}_k] \,\right] \leq M < \infty. \;\checkmark$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Conditional Expectation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[Z_{k+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_k]
&= E\!\left[\, E\!\left[\, f \;\big|\; \mathcal{F}_{k+1} \,\right] \;\Big|\; \mathcal{F}_k \,\right] & \text{(definition of } Z_{k+1}\text{)} \\
&= E\!\left[\, f \;\big|\; \mathcal{F}_k \,\right] & \text{(tower property of conditional expectation)} \\
&= Z_k. \;\blacksquare & \text{(definition of } Z_k\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Optional Stopping Theorem (Bounded Case)

**Theorem (OST ŌĆö Bounded Stopping Time).** Let $\{Z_n\}$ be a martingale and let $T$ be a stopping time with $P(T \leq N) = 1$ for some fixed $N$. Then $E[Z_T] = E[Z_0]$.

**Proof Sketch.**

Define $Z_{T \wedge n}$, where $\wedge$ denotes $\min$. Using the *fundamental martingale identity* on the stopped process, one shows $E[Z_{T \wedge N}] = E[Z_0]$. Because $T \leq N$ a.s., $Z_{T \wedge N} = Z_T$ a.s., giving $E[Z_T] = E[Z_0]$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.5 Derivation 5 ŌĆö Application: Expected Hitting Time of $\pm a$

**Problem.** A symmetric random walk $\{Z_n\}$ starts at $0$. Find $E[T_a]$, where $T_a = \min\{n : \vert Z_n \vert = a\}$.

**Step 1.** $Z_0 = 0$ and $\{Z_{T_a \wedge n}\}$ is a bounded martingale because $\vert Z_{T_a \wedge n} \vert \leq a$.

**Step 2.** Apply Optional Stopping with bounded stopping time argument:

$$
E[Z_{T_a}] = E[Z_0] = 0.
$$

**Step 3.** By symmetry, $P(Z_{T_a} = a) = P(Z_{T_a} = -a) = \tfrac{1}{2}$, so

$$
E[Z_{T_a}] = a \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} + (-a)\cdot\tfrac{1}{2} = 0. \;\checkmark
$$

For the *time* itself, apply OST to the martingale $Z_n^2 - n$ (which has $E[Z_n^2 - n] = 0$ by the variance computation):

$$
E[Z_{T_a}^2 - T_a] = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; E[T_a] = E[Z_{T_a}^2] = a^2.
$$

Hence the expected hitting time of $\pm a$ is $a^2$. $\blacksquare$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map ŌĆö Martingale and Related Families

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Discrete-time Stochastic Process Z_n] --> B{Is E[Z_n+1 | F_n] = Z_n?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Martingale - Fair Game]
    B -- Less or equal --> D[Supermartingale - Losing Game]
    B -- Greater or equal --> E[Submartingale - Winning Game]
    C --> F[Symmetric Random Walk]
    C --> G[Sum of i.i.d. zero-mean RVs]
    C --> H[Doobs Martingale Construction]
    C --> I[Product of i.i.d. unit-mean RVs]
    H --> J[Used in Bounded Difference Inequalities]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    J --> K[Azuma Hoeffding Inequality]
    K --> L[Concentration Bounds for Randomized Algorithms]
    L --> M[Quicksort Running Time]
    L --> N[Occupancy in Hashing]
    L --> O[Online Paging Competitive Ratio]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture ŌĆö How Martingales Enter Algorithm Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Randomized Algorithm A] --> TRACE[Execution Trace X_1, X_2 ... X_n]
    TRACE --> F1[Define filtration F_k from X_k]
    TRACE --> F2[Choose target function f X]
    F1 --> F3[Construct Doobs martingale Z_k]
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> F4[Verify martingale property]
    F4 --> F5{Bounded differences c_k?}
    F5 -- Yes --> F6[Apply Azuma Hoeffding]
    F5 -- No --> F7[Use Kolmogorov or Freedman]
    F6 --> OUTPUT[Tail Bound on f X - Ef X]
    F7 --> OUTPUT
    OUTPUT --> RESULT[Deviation Probability Guarantee]
```

### 4.3 Information Flow in the Fair-Game Identity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[History Z_1 ... Z_n] --> COND[Conditioning on F_n]
    COND --> EXP[Take Conditional Expectation]
    EXP --> PRED[Predicted Next Value]
    PRED --> CMP{Equals Z_n?}
    CMP -- Yes --> M[Process is a Martingale]
    CMP -- No --> NM[Process is NOT a Martingale]
```

### 4.4 Hitting-Time Analysis Architecture (Symmetric Random Walk)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start at Z_0 = 0] --> STEP[Each step +1 or -1 with prob 1/2]
    STEP --> BOUND{Hit level +a or -a?}
    BOUND -- No --> STEP
    BOUND -- Yes --> STOP[T_a is the stopping time]
    STOP --> APPLY[Apply OST to Z_Ta and Z_Ta^2 - n]
    APPLY --> RES1[E Z_Ta = 0]
    APPLY --> RES2[E T_a = a^2]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper, Randomized Algorithms]`
Define a martingale. State the two conditions a stochastic process $\{Z_n\}$ must satisfy to be a martingale with respect to a filtration $\{\mathcal{F}_n\}$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A sequence of random variables $Z_0, Z_1, Z_2, \dots$ is a martingale with respect to the filtration $\{\mathcal{F}_n\}$ if for every $n \geq 0$:

(i) $E[\vert Z_n \vert] < \infty$ *(integrability, 1 mark)*
(ii) $E[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = Z_n$ *(fair-game identity, 2 marks)*

The first condition ensures the conditional expectation exists and is finite; the second asserts that given all past information, the expected future value equals the present value.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper, Randomized Algorithms]`
Give one example of a martingale arising from independent random variables. State why your example satisfies the martingale property.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Example (2 marks):* Let $X_1, X_2, \dots$ be i.i.d. with $P(X_i = +1) = P(X_i = -1) = \tfrac{1}{2}$. Define $Z_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ with $Z_0 = 0$.

*Justification (1 mark):* Since $X_{n+1}$ is independent of $\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(X_1, \dots, X_n)$ and $E[X_{n+1}] = 0$, we get $E[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = Z_n + E[X_{n+1}] = Z_n$. Also $E[\vert Z_n \vert] \leq n < \infty$. Hence $\{Z_n\}$ is a martingale.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

#### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Mandatory Choice 1

**Q.A.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** *Define* a martingale, a supermartingale, and a submartingale. Show that if $X_1, X_2, \dots$ are i.i.d. random variables with $E[X_i] = 0$, then $S_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ is a martingale w.r.t. the natural filtration. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** *State* the Optional Stopping Theorem for bounded stopping times. Use it to prove that the expected hitting time of levels $\pm a$ for a symmetric random walk starting at $0$ is $a^2$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution.**

**(a)** Definitions (2 marks):

- *Martingale:* $E[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = Z_n$.
- *Supermartingale:* $E[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] \leq Z_n$.
- *Submartingale:* $E[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] \geq Z_n$.

*Proof that $S_n$ is a martingale (5 marks):*

Let $\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(X_1, \dots, X_n)$.

(i) Integrability: $E[\vert S_n \vert] \leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} E[\vert X_i \vert] < \infty$ since each $X_i$ has finite expectation.

(ii) Fair-game identity:

$$E[S_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = E\!\left[\, S_n + X_{n+1} \;\big|\; \mathcal{F}_n \,\right] = S_n + E[X_{n+1}] = S_n + 0 = S_n.$$

Both conditions hold, so $\{S_n\}$ is a martingale.

**(b)** *OST Statement (2 marks):* If $\{Z_n\}$ is a martingale and $T$ is a stopping time bounded by some constant $N$, then $E[Z_T] = E[Z_0]$.

*Expected Hitting Time Computation (5 marks):*

Let $T_a = \min\{n : \vert Z_n \vert = a\}$ for the symmetric random walk starting at $Z_0 = 0$.

**Step 1.** Apply OST to the martingale $\{Z_{T_a \wedge n}\}$, which is bounded by $a$:
$$E[Z_{T_a}] = E[Z_0] = 0.$$

**Step 2.** Apply OST to the martingale $W_n = Z_n^2 - n$. To verify, note

$$E[W_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = E[Z_{n+1}^2 - (n+1) \mid \mathcal{F}_n].$$

Since $Z_{n+1} = Z_n + X_{n+1}$ with $X_{n+1} \in \{-1, +1\}$ and $E[X_{n+1}] = 0$, $E[X_{n+1}^2] = 1$, we get

$$E[Z_{n+1}^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = Z_n^2 + 1.$$

Therefore $E[W_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = (Z_n^2 + 1) - (n+1) = Z_n^2 - n = W_n$. Ō£ō

**Step 3.** Apply OST to $W_{T_a}$:

$$E[W_{T_a}] = E[W_0] = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; E[Z_{T_a}^2] - E[T_a] = 0.$$

Since $\vert Z_{T_a} \vert = a$, $E[Z_{T_a}^2] = a^2$, giving $E[T_a] = a^2$. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Mandatory Choice 2

**Q.B.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** *Define* a martingale difference sequence. Construct Doob's martingale from a sequence $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$ and a real function $f$, and verify that it is indeed a martingale. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** *State* the AzumaŌĆōHoeffding inequality. Apply it to bound the deviation of a $\{-1, +1\}$-valued sum from its mean by $t$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution.**

**(a)** *Definition (2 marks):* A sequence $\{D_n\}$ is a **martingale difference sequence** (MDS) w.r.t. $\{\mathcal{F}_n\}$ if $E[D_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = 0$ and $E[\vert D_{n+1} \mid] < \infty$.

*Doob's Construction (2 marks):* Given bounded $f$ and $\mathcal{F}_k = \sigma(X_1, \dots, X_k)$, define

$$Z_k = E[f(X_1, \dots, X_n) \mid \mathcal{F}_k], \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, n.$$

*Verification (3 marks):*

(i) Integrability: $\vert Z_k \vert \leq E[\vert f \vert \mid \mathcal{F}_k]$, so $E[\vert Z_k \vert] \leq E[\vert f \vert] < \infty$.

(ii) Fair-game identity, using the **tower property** of conditional expectation:

$$
E[Z_{k+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_k] = E\!\left[\, E[f \mid \mathcal{F}_{k+1}] \mid \mathcal{F}_k \,\right] = E[f \mid \mathcal{F}_k] = Z_k.
$$

Hence $\{Z_k\}$ is a martingale.

**(b)** *AzumaŌĆōHoeffding Inequality (3 marks):* Let $\{Z_n\}$ be a martingale with $Z_0 = 0$ and differences satisfying $\vert Z_k - Z_{k-1} \vert \leq c_k$ for each $k$. Then for any $t > 0$:

$$P\!\left(\, \big\vert Z_n \big\vert \geq t \,\right) \leq 2 \exp\!\left(\, -\,\dfrac{t^2}{2\, \sum_{k=1}^{n} c_k^2} \,\right).$$

*Application to $\{-1, +1\}$-sum (4 marks):* Let $Y_1, \dots, Y_n$ be i.i.d. with $P(Y_i = +1) = P(Y_i = -1) = \tfrac{1}{2}$ and let $S_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$. Note $E[S_n] = 0$.

We apply AzumaŌĆōHoeffding with $Z_k = S_k$, so $Z_0 = 0$ and $\vert Z_k - Z_{k-1} \vert = \vert Y_k \vert = 1$. Hence $c_k = 1$ and $\sum c_k^2 = n$. Therefore:

$$P\!\left(\, \big\vert S_n \big\vert \geq t \,\right) \leq 2\, \exp\!\left(\, -\,\dfrac{t^2}{2n} \,\right).$$

This is the classical Hoeffding bound for fair coin flips. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Skipping the integrability check** in martingale proofs costs 1ŌĆō2 marks. Always write $E[\vert Z_n \vert] < \infty$ explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing sub/super:** Supermartingales are *losing games* (inequality $\leq$); submartingales are *winning games* (inequality $\geq$). Reversing these is a 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting the boundedness condition** in OST. Apply OST only to martingales that remain bounded at the stopping time, or quote the specific theorem version.
> 4. **Using AzumaŌĆōHoeffding without verifying bounded differences.** If $\vert Z_k - Z_{k-1} \vert$ is unbounded, the inequality does not apply directly.
> 5. **Misapplying independence:** In $E[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n]$, only independence of the *new* random variable is needed, not of the entire sequence.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **martingale** is a discrete-time stochastic process $\{Z_n\}$ satisfying integrability and the fair-game identity $E[Z_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = Z_n$.
- **Supermartingales** ($\leq$) and **submartingales** ($\geq$) are the natural generalizations where the game is unfair.
- Three **canonical examples** to memorize: (i) sum of i.i.d. zero-mean RVs, (ii) product of i.i.d. unit-mean RVs, (iii) Doob's martingale $E[f \mid \mathcal{F}_k]$.
- A **martingale difference sequence** has $E[D_{n+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_n] = 0$; sums of MDS yield martingales.
- **Doob's construction** is the bridge between arbitrary functions of random variables and martingale theory.
- The **Optional Stopping Theorem (OST)** lets us exchange expectation and evaluation at a stopping time ŌĆö central for hitting-time analysis.
- The **AzumaŌĆōHoeffding inequality** converts bounded martingale differences into exponential concentration bounds and is the workhorse for analyzing randomized algorithms.
- For the **symmetric random walk**, $Z_n$ is a martingale, $Z_n^2 - n$ is a martingale, and $E[T_a] = a^2$.
- **Algorithm-design applications:** Quicksort running time, hashing occupancy bounds, occupancy in cuckoo hashing, online paging competitive ratios, and randomized routing.
- **Exam rule of thumb:** Whenever you see "expected deviation" or "tail probability" of a sum in a randomized algorithm, **think martingale ŌåÆ AzumaŌĆōHoeffding**.
- **Filter tip:** Always state the filtration explicitly; the natural filtration $\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(X_1, \dots, X_n)$ is the default unless stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Azuma's Inequality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST639 - RANDOMIZED ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Azuma's Inequality # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Azuma's Inequality** (also known as the **AzumaŌĆōHoeffding Inequality**) is a fundamental concentration inequality in probability theory that bounds the deviation of a **martingale** from its expected value. It is the martingale generalization of Hoeffding's inequality and is one of the most powerful tools in the analysis of randomized algorithms. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECST639 ŌĆö Module 4)** > Let $X_0, X_1, \ldots, X_n$ be a martingale with respect to the filtration $\mathcal{F}_0 \subseteq \mathcal{F}_1 \subseteq \cdots \subseteq \mathcal{F}_n$ such that for each $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$, the **bounded difference condition** holds: > $$X_i - X_{i-1} \leq c_i \quad \text{(almost surely)}$$ > Then, for any $t > 0$, Azuma's Inequality states: > $$\Pr\!\left[\,\left\vert X_n - \mathbb{E}[X_n] \right\vert \geq t\,\right] \leq 2 \exp\!\left(\,-\frac{t^2}{2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i^2}\,\right)$$ Equivalently, for a martingale with **uniformly bounded differences** $\vert X_i - X_{i-1} \vert \leq c_i$, the result holds with the **two-sided** bound. ## 1.2 Special Case: Hoeffding's Inequality When $X_i$ are **independent random variables** with $X_i \in [a_i, b_i]$, defining the **Doob martingale** $Z_k = \mathbb{E}[X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_n \mid X_1, \ldots, X_k]$ recovers **Hoeffding's Inequality** as a corollary. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Snowball Rolling Downhill" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Picture (Plain English)** > Imagine a snowball rolling down a hill where the path is governed by chance ŌĆö each gust of wind (random choice) pushes it either left or right. You start at the expected landing point (the mean), and at every step the push is **bounded** ŌĆö say, never more than 1 meter in either direction. After $n$ steps, **Azuma's Inequality tells you how likely the snowball is to have drifted far from the average path**. The bound says: the probability of being a long way off decays **exponentially** in the square of the deviation, divided by the total "wiggle room" $2 \sum c_i^2$. **Real-world metaphor for engineering students:** - $X_0, X_1, \ldots, X_n$ is the **cumulative score** of an algorithm after each random decision. - Each $\pm c_i$ is the **maximum impact** of one random choice. - Azuma tells us: **most runs land close to the expected score** ŌĆö this is the mathematical heart of "derandomization" via concentration. ## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance | Term | Meaning | KTU Relevance | |------|---------|---------------| | **Martingale** | A fair game: $\mathbb{E}[X_i \mid \mathcal{F}_{i-1}] = X_{i-1}$ | Foundation of the inequality | | **Filtration** $\mathcal{F}_i$ | History of randomness up to step $i$ | Defines the conditioning | | **Bounded Difference** | $\vert X_i - X_{i-1} \vert \leq c_i$ | Crucial hypothesis | | **Doob Martingale** | Conditional-expectation construction | Links to i.i.d. sums | | **Concentration** | Decay of tail probabilities | Central to algorithm analysis | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown ### Step 1 ŌĆö The Setup A **discrete-time martingale** is a sequence of random variables $(X_i)$ such that: $$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\left\vert X_i \right\vert\,\right] < \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \mathbb{E}[X_i \mid \mathcal{F}_{i-1}] = X_{i-1}$$ The filtration $\mathcal{F}_i$ represents "all information revealed by time $i$." ### Step 2 ŌĆö The Bounded Difference Condition There exist **non-negative constants** $c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n$ such that for every $i$: $$X_i - X_{i-1} \leq c_i \quad \text{a.s.}$$ The **two-sided** version requires $\vert X_i - X_{i-1} \vert \leq c_i$. ### Step 3 ŌĆö Exponential Moment via Chernoff For any $\lambda > 0$, **Markov's inequality** applied to $e^{\lambda X_n}$ gives: $$\Pr[X_n - \mathbb{E}[X_n] \geq t] = \Pr\!\left[\,e^{\lambda(X_n - \mathbb{E}[X_n])} \geq e^{\lambda t}\,\right] \leq e^{-\lambda t}\, \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{\lambda(X_n - \mathbb{E}[X_n])}\,\right]$$ ### Step 4 ŌĆö Bounding the MGF Using the bounded difference and conditional expectation, one shows: $$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{\lambda(X_i - X_{i-1})} \mid \mathcal{F}_{i-1}\,\right] \leq e^{\lambda^2 c_i^2 / 2}$$ This is the key technical inequality ŌĆö it follows from the **Hoeffding lemma** applied conditionally. ### Step 5 ŌĆö Telescoping and Optimizing The MGF factors: $$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{\lambda(X_n - X_0)}\,\right] \leq \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{\lambda^2 c_i^2 / 2} = \exp\!\left(\,\frac{\lambda^2}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i^2\,\right)$$ Optimizing $\lambda$ gives $\lambda^\star = t / \sum c_i^2$, yielding the final bound. ### Step 6 ŌĆö Two-Sided via Union Apply the one-sided bound to both $+t$ and $-t$ and use the **union bound**: $$\Pr\!\left[\,\left\vert X_n - \mathbb{E}[X_n] \right\vert \geq t\,\right] \leq 2 \exp\!\left(\,-\frac{t^2}{2 \sum c_i^2}\,\right)$$ ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Theorem > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Big Idea** > The martingale framework lets us analyze **dependent** random variables as long as each step has bounded conditional effect. This is far more general than Hoeffding's inequality, which requires **independence**. This is why Azuma is the workhorse of modern randomized algorithm analysis. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All the formulas you must memorize for Module 4** | # | Formula / Statement | Condition | When to Use | |---|---------------------|-----------|-------------| | 1 | $\Pr[X_n - \mathbb{E}[X_n] \geq t] \leq \exp\!\left(\,-\dfrac{t^2}{2\sum c_i^2}\,\right)$ | Bounded diff. martingale | One-sided upper tail | | 2 | $\Pr\!\left[\left\vert X_n - \mathbb{E}[X_n] \right\vert \geq t\right] \leq 2\exp\!\left(\,-\dfrac{t^2}{2\sum c_i^2}\,\right)$ | $\vert X_i - X_{i-1}\vert \leq c_i$ | **Standard form** | | 3 | $\Pr\!\left[\left\vert X_n - \mathbb{E}[X_n] \right\vert \geq t\right] \leq 2\exp\!\left(\,-\dfrac{t^2}{2n}\right)$ | Uniform $\vert c_i \vert = 1$ | Cleanest case | | 4 | $\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{\lambda(X_i - X_{i-1})} \mid \mathcal{F}_{i-1}\,\right] \leq e^{\lambda^2 c_i^2 / 2}$ | Hoeffding's lemma (cond.) | Proof step | | 5 | $\lambda^\star = t / \sum c_i^2$ | Optimal Chernoff parameter | Optimization step | | 6 | $X_k = \mathbb{E}[S_n \mid Y_1, \ldots, Y_k]$ | Doob martingale construction | Reducing to sums | | 7 | $\Pr[\text{load} \geq (1+\delta)\mu] \leq \exp\!\left(\,-\dfrac{\delta^2 \mu}{3}\,\right)$ | Balls-into-bins | Application | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Load balancing** (balls-into-bins): bounding the maximum load of a hash table. - **Packet routing**: bounding delay in randomized network protocols. - **Online algorithms**: analyzing competitive ratio deviations. - **PAC-learning / ML**: bounding generalization error with bounded-dependency data. - **QuickSelect / QuickSort**: bounding the depth of recursion and runtime. - **Cryptographic primitives**: analyzing randomized reductions. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Full Proof of Azuma's Inequality (One-Sided Form) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theorem (Azuma, 1967).** Let $(X_0, X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ be a martingale w.r.t. filtration $(\mathcal{F}_i)$ with $X_0 = 0$ w.l.o.g. and $X_i - X_{i-1} \leq c_i$ a.s. Then for every $t > 0$: > $$\Pr[X_n \geq t] \leq \exp\!\left(\,-\frac{t^2}{2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i^2}\,\right)$$ ### Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Chernoff / Markov to $e^{\lambda X_n}$ By **Markov's inequality**, for any $\lambda > 0$: $$ \Pr[X_n \geq t] = \Pr\!\left[\,e^{\lambda X_n} \geq e^{\lambda t}\,\right] $$ Multiplying both sides and applying Markov: $$ \Pr[X_n \geq t] \leq e^{-\lambda t}\, \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{\lambda X_n}\,\right] $$ > **Logic:** Markov's inequality states $\Pr[Y \geq a] \leq \mathbb{E}[Y]/a$ for non-negative $Y$. We set $Y = e^{\lambda X_n}$ and $a = e^{\lambda t}$, which is positive. ### Step 2 ŌĆö Factor the MGF Using the Martingale Property We split the MGF via the tower property of conditional expectation: $$ \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{\lambda X_n}\,\right] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{\lambda X_{n-1}}\, \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{\lambda(X_n - X_{n-1})} \mid \mathcal{F}_{n-1}\,\right]\,\right] $$ > **Logic:** $X_n = X_{n-1} + (X_n - X_{n-1})$, so $e^{\lambda X_n} = e^{\lambda X_{n-1}} \cdot e^{\lambda(X_n - X_{n-1})}$. Taking the conditional expectation given $\mathcal{F}_{n-1}$ isolates the increment. ### Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Conditional Hoeffding's Lemma The conditional Hoeffding lemma states: if $Z$ is a random variable with $Z \leq c$ a.s. and $\mathbb{E}[Z \mid \mathcal{F}] = 0$ a.s., then for any $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$: $$ \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{\lambda Z} \mid \mathcal{F}\,\right] \leq e^{\lambda^2 c^2 / 2} $$ We apply it with $Z = X_n - X_{n-1}$ and $\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{F}_{n-1}$. The condition $\mathbb{E}[X_n - X_{n-1} \mid \mathcal{F}_{n-1}] = 0$ is exactly the **martingale property**. > **Logic:** Hoeffding's lemma says bounded zero-mean variables have sub-Gaussian MGFs. Applied pointwise (a.s.) to the conditional distribution of the increment. Thus: $$ \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{\lambda(X_n - X_{n-1})} \mid \mathcal{F}_{n-1}\,\right] \leq e^{\lambda^2 c_n^2 / 2} $$ ### Step 4 ŌĆö Iterate Pulling the bound out of the inner conditional expectation: $$ \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{\lambda X_n}\,\right] \leq e^{\lambda^2 c_n^2 / 2}\, \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{\lambda X_{n-1}}\,\right] $$ Applying the same argument recursively for $i = n-1, n-2, \ldots, 1$: $$ \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{\lambda X_n}\,\right] \leq \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{\lambda^2 c_i^2 / 2} = \exp\!\left(\,\frac{\lambda^2}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i^2\,\right) $$ > **Logic:** Each iteration peels off one increment and applies Step 3. The product collapses because exponents add. ### Step 5 ŌĆö Combine and Optimize Putting it all together: $$ \Pr[X_n \geq t] \leq \exp\!\left(\,\frac{\lambda^2}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i^2 - \lambda t\,\right) $$ To get the **tightest** bound, minimize the exponent over $\lambda > 0$: $$ \frac{\partial}{\partial \lambda}\!\left(\,\frac{\lambda^2}{2}\sum c_i^2 - \lambda t\,\right) = \lambda \sum c_i^2 - t = 0 $$ So: $$ \lambda^\star = \frac{t}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i^2} $$ Substituting $\lambda^\star$ back: $$ \Pr[X_n \geq t] \leq \exp\!\left(\,\frac{t^2}{2(\sum c_i^2)^2}\sum c_i^2 - \frac{t^2}{\sum c_i^2}\,\right) = \exp\!\left(\,\frac{t^2}{2\sum c_i^2} - \frac{t^2}{\sum c_i^2}\,\right) = \exp\!\left(\,-\frac{t^2}{2\sum c_i^2}\,\right) $$ > **Logic:** First term: $\frac{\lambda^{\star\,2}}{2}\sum c_i^2 = \frac{t^2}{2(\sum c_i^2)^2}\cdot \sum c_i^2 = \frac{t^2}{2\sum c_i^2}$. Second term: $\lambda^\star t = \frac{t^2}{\sum c_i^2}$. The difference is $-\frac{t^2}{2\sum c_i^2}$. ### Step 6 ŌĆö Two-Sided Form via Symmetry and Union Bound For the absolute value, apply the one-sided result to both $X_n$ and $-X_n$ (which is also a martingale with bounded differences $c_i$): $$ \Pr\!\left[\,\left\vert X_n \right\vert \geq t\,\right] = \Pr[X_n \geq t] + \Pr[X_n \leq -t] \leq 2\exp\!\left(\,-\frac{t^2}{2\sum c_i^2}\,\right) $$ > **Logic:** $-X_n$ satisfies the same hypotheses; the events $\{X_n \geq t\}$ and $\{X_n \leq -t\}$ are disjoint. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Worked Example: Occupancy Problem (Balls into Bins) > [!NOTE] > **Setup:** Throw $n$ balls uniformly at random into $n$ bins. Let $M$ = maximum number of balls in any bin. Show $M = O(\log n / \log\log n)$ with high probability. ### Construction of the Martingale Let $X_0 = 0$. For $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ define: $$X_k = \mathbb{E}[M \mid \text{first } k \text{ balls}]$$ This is a **Doob martingale** because $\mathbb{E}[X_k \mid \mathcal{F}_{k-1}] = X_{k-1}$ a.s. ### Bounding the Differences Changing the location of ball $k$ changes the load of **at most one** bin by $\pm 1$ and possibly changes $M$ by **at most 1**. Hence: $$\vert X_k - X_{k-1} \vert \leq 1 \quad \text{a.s.}$$ So $c_i = 1$ for all $i$, giving $\sum c_i^2 = n$. ### Applying Azuma Setting $t = c\sqrt{n \log n}$ for suitable $c$: $$ \Pr\!\left[\,\left\vert M - \mathbb{E}[M] \right\vert \geq c\sqrt{n \log n}\,\right] \leq 2\exp\!\left(\,-\frac{c^2 n \log n}{2n}\,\right) = 2 n^{-c^2/2} $$ > **Logic:** With $c$ large enough this is polynomially small, so $M$ concentrates tightly around its mean. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Logical Flow of the Azuma Proof ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Martingale X_0, X_1, ..., X_n
with bounded diffs vert X_i - X_{i-1} vert le c_i"] --> B["Apply Markov / Chernoff bound
Pr X_n ge t le e^{-lambda t} E e^{lambda X_n}"] B --> C["Factor MGF via tower property
E e^{lambda X_n} = E e^{lambda X_{n-1}} E e^{lambda dX_n} vert F_{n-1}"] C --> D["Apply Conditional Hoeffding Lemma
E e^{lambda dX_n} vert F_{n-1} le e^{lambda^2 c_n^2 / 2}"] D --> E["Iterate for i = n, n-1, ..., 1
E e^{lambda X_n} le exp lambda^2 sum c_i^2 / 2"] E --> F["Combine with Markov bound
Pr X_n ge t le exp lambda^2 sum c_i^2 / 2 - lambda t"] F --> G["Optimize lambda: lambda star = t / sum c_i^2"] G --> H["Final one-sided bound
Pr X_n ge t le exp -t^2 / 2 sum c_i^2"] H --> I["Union bound on -X_n
vert X_n vert le t implies two-sided form"] I --> J["Final two-sided Azuma bound
Pr vert X_n - E X_n vert ge t le 2 exp -t^2 / 2 sum c_i^2"] node1[Start]:::startNode nodeB[Chernoff]:::procNode nodeC[Factor]:::procNode nodeD[Hoeffding Lemma]:::keyNode nodeE[Iterate]:::procNode nodeF[Combine]:::procNode nodeG[Optimize]:::keyNode nodeH[One-Sided]:::resultNode nodeI[Union]:::procNode nodeJ[Two-Sided Final]:::resultNode classDef startNode fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef procNode fill:#7fb3d5,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef keyNode fill:#f39c12,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef resultNode fill:#27ae60,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture ŌĆö Applying Azuma to a Randomized Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart LR R["Random Source"] --> A["Algorithm with
random choices Y_1, ..., Y_n"] A --> S["Output / Cost C(Y_1, ..., Y_n)"] S --> D["Doob Martingale
X_k = E C vert Y_1, ..., Y_k"] D --> B["Bounded Diff Check
vert X_k - X_{k-1} vert le c_k"] B --> AZ["Apply Azuma Inequality
Pr vert X_n - E X_n vert ge t le 2 exp -t^2 / 2 sum c_k^2"] AZ --> OUT["Tail Bound on Runtime /
Load / Quality"] R2[Random Source]:::srcNode A2[Algorithm]:::procNode S2[Cost]:::procNode D2[Doob Martingale]:::keyNode B2[Bounded Diff]:::keyNode AZ2[Azuma]:::resultNode OUT2[Concentration Bound]:::resultNode classDef srcNode fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef procNode fill:#7fb3d5,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef keyNode fill:#f39c12,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef resultNode fill:#27ae60,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Relationship Map: Azuma Ōåö Hoeffding Ōåö Chernoff Ōåö Markov ```mermaid flowchart TD M["Markov Inequality
Pr Y ge a le E Y / a"] --> C["Chernoff Bound
Pr sum X_i - mu ge t le exp -t^2 / 2n"] C --> H["Hoeffding Inequality
Pr vert sum X_i - mu vert ge t le 2 exp -t^2 / 2 sum b_i^2"] M --> H H --> AZ["Azuma Inequality
Martingale version of Hoeffding"] AZ --> DA["Doob Martingale Construction"] DA --> H AZ --> AP["Applications:
Balls-into-bins, QuickSelect, Routing"] n1[Markov]:::baseNode n2[Chernoff]:::procNode n3[Hoeffding]:::procNode n4[Azuma]:::keyNode n5[Doob]:::keyNode n6[Apps]:::resultNode classDef baseNode fill:#34495e,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef procNode fill:#7fb3d5,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef keyNode fill:#f39c12,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef resultNode fill:#27ae60,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Summary Table | Block | Role | Output | |-------|------|--------| | **Randomness** | Sequence of choices $Y_1, \ldots, Y_n$ | Filtration $\mathcal{F}_i$ | | **Algorithm** | Function of randomness | Cost / quality metric $Z$ | | **Doob Martingale** | Conditional expectations | Sequence $X_0, \ldots, X_n$ | | **Bounded-Diff Check** | Verify per-step impact | Constants $c_i$ | | **Azuma Engine** | Plug into inequality | Exponential tail bound | | **Engineering Decision** | Convert bound to WHP guarantee | Algorithm is efficient | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model]** State and explain Azuma's Inequality for martingales with bounded differences. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Azuma's Inequality states that if $X_0, X_1, \ldots, X_n$ is a martingale with respect to a filtration $\mathcal{F}_0, \mathcal{F}_1, \ldots, \mathcal{F}_n$ satisfying the **bounded difference condition** $\vert X_i - X_{i-1}\vert \leq c_i$ a.s. for all $i$, then for any $t > 0$: $$\Pr\!\left[\,\left\vert X_n - \mathbb{E}[X_n] \right\vert \geq t\,\right] \leq 2 \exp\!\left(\,-\frac{t^2}{2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i^2}\,\right)$$ It generalizes Hoeffding's inequality to dependent sequences and is widely used in analyzing randomized algorithms where individual random choices have bounded impact on the overall cost. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model]** How does Azuma's Inequality differ from Hoeffding's Inequality? When is Azuma preferred? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Hoeffding's Inequality requires the random variables to be **mutually independent**, while Azuma's Inequality applies to **martingales** (a much broader class, including dependent sequences). Azuma is preferred when: 1. The algorithm's cost depends on **adaptively chosen** random values. 2. We construct a **Doob martingale** of conditional expectations. 3. Each step's impact on the overall quantity is **bounded** but the variables are not independent. Azuma is strictly more general: setting $X_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} Y_i$ for independent $Y_i$ recovers Hoeffding. --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### ŌŁÉ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper, Module 4]** **(a)** Derive Azuma's Inequality in detail. State all hypotheses clearly and show every step of the proof. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Apply Azuma's Inequality to analyze the **occupancy problem**: when $n$ balls are thrown independently and uniformly into $n$ bins, show that the maximum load $M$ satisfies $M = O(\sqrt{n \log n})$ with high probability. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a) ŌĆö 7 marks **Step 1 ŌĆö Hypotheses (1 mark):** Let $X_0, X_1, \ldots, X_n$ be a martingale w.r.t. filtration $\mathcal{F}_0 \subseteq \mathcal{F}_1 \subseteq \cdots \subseteq \mathcal{F}_n$ with $\vert X_i - X_{i-1}\vert \leq c_i$ a.s. **Step 2 ŌĆö Chernoff / Markov setup (1 mark):** For any $\lambda > 0$: $$\Pr[X_n \geq t] \leq e^{-\lambda t}\, \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{\lambda X_n}\,\right]$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Tower factorization (1 mark):** $$ \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{\lambda X_n}\,\right] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{\lambda X_{n-1}}\, \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{\lambda(X_n - X_{n-1})} \mid \mathcal{F}_{n-1}\,\right]\,\right] $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Conditional Hoeffding lemma (2 marks):** For $Z = X_n - X_{n-1}$ with $\mathbb{E}[Z \mid \mathcal{F}_{n-1}] = 0$ and $\vert Z \vert \leq c_n$: $$ \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{\lambda Z} \mid \mathcal{F}_{n-1}\,\right] \leq e^{\lambda^2 c_n^2 / 2} $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Iteration and optimization (2 marks):** Iterating $n$ times and optimizing $\lambda^\star = t/\sum c_i^2$: $$ \Pr[X_n \geq t] \leq \exp\!\left(\,-\frac{t^2}{2\sum c_i^2}\,\right) $$ The **two-sided** form follows from the union bound. #### Model Solution to (b) ŌĆö 7 marks **Construction of the Doob martingale (2 marks):** Let $M$ = maximum load. Define: $$X_k = \mathbb{E}[M \mid \text{locations of first } k \text{ balls}]$$ Then $X_0, X_1, \ldots, X_n$ is a martingale. **Bounded differences (2 marks):** Ball $k$ changes only **one bin's** count by $\pm 1$. Hence $M$ can change by **at most 1**, so $\vert X_k - X_{k-1}\vert \leq 1$ a.s. Thus $c_i = 1$ and $\sum c_i^2 = n$. **Apply Azuma (2 marks):** For $t = c\sqrt{n \log n}$: $$ \Pr\!\left[\,\left\vert M - \mathbb{E}[M] \right\vert \geq c\sqrt{n \log n}\,\right] \leq 2\exp\!\left(\,-\frac{c^2 \log n}{2}\,\right) = 2 n^{-c^2/2} $$ **Conclusion (1 mark):** For $c \geq 3$, this is $O(1/n^{4.5})$, giving $M = \mathbb{E}[M] + O(\sqrt{n \log n})$ with high probability. --- ### ŌŁÉ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper, Module 4]** **(a)** With the help of Chernoff bound and the Doob martingale construction, prove Hoeffding's Inequality as a special case of Azuma's Inequality. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a randomized QuickSelect algorithm, define the **rank tree** $T$ as a binary tree where the root stores the rank of the pivot. Show, using Azuma's Inequality, that the **depth of any leaf** in $T$ is $O(\log n)$ with high probability. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a) ŌĆö 7 marks **Step 1 ŌĆö Setting up the Doob martingale (2 marks):** Let $Y_1, \ldots, Y_n$ be independent with $Y_i \in [a_i, b_i]$. Define: $$X_k = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i \;\Big|\; Y_1, \ldots, Y_k\,\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{k} Y_i + \sum_{i=k+1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[Y_i]$$ This is a martingale with $X_0 = \sum \mathbb{E}[Y_i]$ and $X_n = \sum Y_i$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Bounded differences (1 mark):** $$\vert X_k - X_{k-1} \vert = \vert Y_k - \mathbb{E}[Y_k] \vert \leq \max(\vert a_k - \mathbb{E}[Y_k]\vert, \vert b_k - \mathbb{E}[Y_k]\vert) \leq b_k - a_k$$ So $c_k = b_k - a_k$. **Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Azuma (2 marks):** With $\sum c_k^2 = \sum (b_k - a_k)^2$: $$ \Pr\!\left[\,\left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[Y_i] \right\vert \geq t\,\right] \leq 2\exp\!\left(\,-\frac{t^2}{2 \sum (b_k - a_k)^2}\,\right) $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Verify (2 marks):** This is precisely Hoeffding's Inequality. The proof is complete, demonstrating Azuma $\supseteq$ Hoeffding. #### Model Solution to (b) ŌĆö 7 marks **Step 1 ŌĆö Define the cost (2 marks):** Let $D$ be the depth of a specific leaf in the rank tree $T$. The depth equals the number of recursive calls needed to find the order statistic. **Step 2 ŌĆö Construct the martingale (2 marks):** Let $X_0 = \mathbb{E}[D]$. Define $X_k = \mathbb{E}[D \mid \text{first } k \text{ pivots}]$, a Doob martingale. **Step 3 ŌĆö Bound the difference (1 mark):** Each new pivot reduces the remaining problem size by **at least 1** (since the pivot is removed from further consideration). So the depth can change by at most 1 per step: $c_k = 1$ and $\sum c_k^2 \leq n$. **Step 4 ŌĆö Apply Azuma (2 marks):** For $t = c\sqrt{n \log n}$: $$ \Pr\!\left[\,\left\vert D - \mathbb{E}[D] \right\vert \geq c\sqrt{n \log n}\,\right] \leq 2\exp\!\left(\,-\frac{c^2 \log n}{2}\,\right) $$ Taking $c$ large enough, this is $o(1)$, so $D = O(\log n)$ w.h.p. (after tightening the analysis using the **geometric decrease** of the remaining subproblem). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Module 4 (Azuma's Inequality)** > > 1. **Forgetting the union bound** ŌĆö The two-sided form has the factor of **2**. Students who write $\exp(-t^2/2\sum c_i^2)$ instead of $2\exp(-t^2/2\sum c_i^2)$ lose **1 mark** instantly. > > 2. **Wrong identification of $c_i$** ŌĆö Many students confuse **bounded difference** with **bounded variable**. The condition is $\vert X_i - X_{i-1}\vert \leq c_i$, **not** $\vert X_i \vert \leq c_i$. Misreading loses up to **2 marks**. > > 3. **Skipping the martingale property** ŌĆö For Azuma to apply, you MUST verify $\mathbb{E}[X_i \mid \mathcal{F}_{i-1}] = X_{i-1}$. A Doob martingale construction must be **explicitly written out**, not just assumed. > > 4. **Wrong Chernoff parameter** ŌĆö The optimal $\lambda = t/\sum c_i^2$ must come from **differentiating the exponent**. Plugging in arbitrary $\lambda$ and missing the optimization step costs the **key technical mark**. > > 5. **Mixing up Hoeffding vs. Azuma hypotheses** ŌĆö Hoeffding needs **independence**; Azuma needs only the **martingale property** with bounded differences. Mark down for any such confusion. > > 6. **Not stating that the filtration is increasing** ŌĆö This is part of the formal definition; examiners often deduct for missing it. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Azuma's Inequality** - **Core statement (two-sided, memorize verbatim):** $$\Pr\!\left[\,\left\vert X_n - \mathbb{E}[X_n] \right\vert \geq t\,\right] \leq 2\exp\!\left(\,-\frac{t^2}{2 \sum c_i^2}\,\right)$$ - **Three required hypotheses** of Azuma: 1. $(X_i)$ is a **martingale** w.r.t. $(\mathcal{F}_i)$. 2. Filtration $(\mathcal{F}_i)$ is **increasing** (more info over time). 3. **Bounded differences:** $\vert X_i - X_{i-1}\vert \leq c_i$ a.s. - **Optimal Chernoff parameter:** $\lambda^\star = \dfrac{t}{\sum_{i=1}^n c_i^2}$. - **Proof recipe (in order):** Markov ŌåÆ Chernoff ŌåÆ Tower Property ŌåÆ Conditional Hoeffding Lemma ŌåÆ Iterate ŌåÆ Optimize ŌåÆ Union Bound. - **Hoeffding is a special case** of Azuma via the **Doob martingale** $X_k = \mathbb{E}[\sum Y_i \mid Y_1, \ldots, Y_k]$. - **Cleanest form (uniform $c_i = 1$):** $\Pr[\vert X_n \vert \geq t] \leq 2\exp(-t^2/2n)$. - **Tail-decay is sub-Gaussian** ŌĆö the bound is $\exp(-t^2)$, not $\exp(-t)$ (which is Markov) or $\exp(-t^3)$ (which is Bernstein). - **Two-sided bound always carries the factor of 2** (union bound). - **Standard applications to remember:** - **Balls-into-bins** ŌåÆ max load via Doob martingale with $c_i = 1$. - **QuickSelect / QuickSort** ŌåÆ recursion depth. - **Randomized routing** ŌåÆ packet delay. - **Online algorithms** ŌåÆ competitive ratio deviation. - **Sub-Gaussian intuition:** bounded-martingale increments behave like independent bounded variables for tail-probability purposes. - **Azuma is more general than Hoeffding:** it works for **dependent** sequences, so always prefer Azuma when dependence is involved. - **Constant $c_i$ must be NON-NEGATIVE** ŌĆö they are upper bounds, not lower bounds. - **Set $X_0 = 0$ WLOG** ŌĆö shifting a martingale by a constant does not change its differences. - **Examiner's favorite trick question:** "Show that Hoeffding's Inequality follows from Azuma's." Know the **Doob construction** cold. - **Azuma requires finite mean** $\mathbb{E}[\vert X_i \vert] < \infty$ ŌĆö usually trivially satisfied. - **Hoeffding's Lemma** (unconditional) states: if $\mathbb{E}[Z] = 0$ and $a \leq Z \leq b$, then $\mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda Z}] \leq e^{\lambda^2(b-a)^2/8}$. The conditional form used in Azuma's proof has the **half-width** $(b-a)/2 = c$, giving $e^{\lambda^2 c^2/2}$. - **Numerical check:** for $n = 100$ and $t = 10$ with $c_i = 1$: bound is $2e^{-100/200} = 2e^{-0.5} \approx 1.213$ ŌĆö **vacuous** (always!). This shows Azuma is only useful for $t = \Omega(\sqrt{n})$. - **Module 4 context:** Azuma is the bridge between **concentration inequalities** (Module 4) and the **Markov/Chebyshev/Chernoff** machinery developed in Modules 1ŌĆō2.

topics.title: Randomized Complexity Classes: RP and BPP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST639 - RANDOMIZED ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Randomized Complexity Classes: RP and BPP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Randomized Complexity Classes ŌĆö Formal Introduction

In the theory of computation, **randomized complexity classes** are sets of decision problems solvable by probabilistic Turing machines whose running time is bounded by a polynomial in the input size, with a controlled probability of producing an incorrect answer. The two foundational classes covered in KTU Module 4 are:

- **RP** ŌĆö *Randomized Polynomial time* (one-sided error)
- **BPP** ŌĆö *Bounded-error Probabilistic Polynomial time* (two-sided error)

### 1.1.1 Formal Definition of RP

A language $L \subseteq \{0,1\}^*$ belongs to the class **RP** if there exists a probabilistic polynomial-time Turing machine $M$ such that for every input $x$:

$$
x \in L \;\Longrightarrow\; \Pr_{r \in \{0,1\}^{p(n)}}[M(x,r) = \text{ACCEPT}] \geq \tfrac{1}{2}
$$

$$
x \notin L \;\Longrightarrow\; \Pr_{r \in \{0,1\}^{p(n)}}[M(x,r) = \text{ACCEPT}] = 0
$$

where $n = \vert x \vert$ and $p(\cdot)$ is a fixed polynomial.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Characteristic of RP (One-Sided Error):**
> - If $x \in L$ (YES instance): the algorithm **accepts with probability Ōēź 1/2** and rejects otherwise.
> - If $x \notin L$ (NO instance): the algorithm **always rejects** (zero false-positive rate).

### 1.1.2 Formal Definition of BPP

A language $L \subseteq \{0,1\}^*$ belongs to the class **BPP** if there exists a probabilistic polynomial-time Turing machine $M$ such that for every input $x$:

$$
x \in L \;\Longrightarrow\; \Pr[M(x) = \text{ACCEPT}] \geq \tfrac{2}{3}
$$

$$
x \notin L \;\Longrightarrow\; \Pr[M(x = \text{REJECT})] \geq \tfrac{2}{3}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Characteristic of BPP (Two-Sided Bounded Error):**
> - Both types of errors (false positives and false negatives) are bounded above by **1/3**.
> - The constants 2/3 and 1/3 are arbitrary; any constants $1/2 + \epsilon$ and $1/2 - \epsilon$ for any constant $\epsilon > 0$ yield an equivalent class via **amplification**.

### 1.1.3 The Complementary Class ŌĆö co-RP

The class **co-RP** is the complement of RP. For $L \in$ co-RP, a probabilistic polynomial-time machine $M$ satisfies:

$$
x \notin L \;\Longrightarrow\; \Pr[M(x) = \text{REJECT}] \geq \tfrac{1}{2}
$$

$$
x \in L \;\Longrightarrow\; \Pr[M(x) = \text{REJECT}] = 0
$$

In other words, **co-RP is the class of problems where NO-instances are verified probabilistically**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

### 1.2.1 Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Courtroom

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a randomized algorithm as a jury of independent experts being polled on a verdict.**

- **RP Analogy (One-Sided Error):** Imagine a witness testifying that "the defendant is guilty" only if they are **certain** (NO-instance ŌćÆ never lies). However, if the defendant is indeed guilty, the witness may sometimes be too nervous to speak up (YES-instance ŌćÆ at least half the time, they do testify). **No innocent person is ever wrongly convicted, but some guilty persons may walk free.**

- **BPP Analogy (Two-Sided Error):** Imagine a jury that reaches the correct verdict (guilty or not guilty) with at least **2/3 probability**, but may occasionally err in **either direction**. With many independent polls, we can drive the error rate exponentially low.

### 1.2.2 Why Randomness Helps

The **probabilistic method** allows algorithms to:
1. **Break symmetries** (e.g., the Min-Cut algorithm using random edge contractions).
2. **Hedge against worst-case adversarial inputs** that deterministic algorithms may suffer from.
3. **Achieve speedups** ŌĆö BPP contains problems for which the best known deterministic algorithms are super-polynomial (e.g., polynomial identity testing).

### 1.2.3 Important Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Value / Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Error Probability (RP) | $\epsilon_{RP}$ | **Ōēż 1/2** | Maximum one-sided error for YES instances |
| Error Probability (BPP) | $\epsilon_{BPP}$ | **Ōēż 1/3** | Maximum two-sided error |
| Confidence Threshold | $\delta$ | **1 ŌłÆ ╬Ą** | Minimum correctness probability |
| Randomness Complexity | $r(n)$ | $O(p(n))$ bits | Number of random bits used |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics of RP

### 2.1.1 Algorithmic Behavior of an RP Machine

The probabilistic Turing machine for an RP language behaves as follows on input $x$:

1. **Randomness Generation:** The machine flips a sequence of fair coins to produce a random string $r$ of length $p(\vert x \vert)$.
2. **Deterministic Computation:** Given $x$ and $r$, the machine runs a deterministic polynomial-time computation.
3. **Output Decision:** It outputs either ACCEPT or REJECT.
4. **Error Bound:** The probability of error is restricted to NO-instances being rejected (zero error) and YES-instances being accepted with probability $\geq 1/2$.

### 2.1.2 Why the Constant 1/2?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The constant 1/2 is **not arbitrary** ŌĆö it serves as the amplification baseline. Any constant strictly greater than 0 (for RP) and strictly greater than 1/2 (for BPP) yields an equivalent class after polynomial amplification.

### 2.1.3 Examples of Problems in RP

- **Primality Testing (Miller-Rabin):** A composite number is **always** declared composite (NO-instance handled with certainty). A prime number is declared prime with probability Ōēź 3/4 per round.
- **Polynomial Identity Testing:** Given two polynomials $P(x)$ and $Q(x)$, deciding $P \equiv Q$ is in co-RP (and hence in BPP).

## 2.2 Operational Mechanics of BPP

### 2.2.1 Two-Sided Error and Symmetry

BPP is symmetric in the sense that **the algorithm's error bound applies to both accepting a NO-instance and rejecting a YES-instance**. This symmetry makes BPP closed under complement: $\text{BPP} = \text{co-BPP}$.

### 2.2.2 The Amplification Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Error Amplification for BPP):** Let $L \in \text{BPP}$ with success probability $1/2 + \epsilon$ for some constant $\epsilon > 0$. Then for every polynomial $q(n)$, there exists a probabilistic polynomial-time machine $M'$ such that:
> $$\Pr[M' \text{ is wrong on } x] \leq 2^{-q(\vert x \vert)}$$

This is achieved by running the original machine $M$ on $x$ using $O(q(n)/\epsilon^2)$ independent random strings and outputting the **majority vote**.

### 2.2.3 Examples of Problems in BPP

- **Polynomial Identity Testing** (Schwartz-Zippel Lemma)
- **Approximate Counting** (via the Karp-Luby FPRAS)
- **Primality Testing** (now known to be in **P** by the AKS algorithm, but historically was the canonical BPP example)

## 2.3 Containment Hierarchy of Complexity Classes

The fundamental relationships among randomized and deterministic classes are:

$$
P \subseteq RP \subseteq BPP \subseteq PP \subseteq PSPACE
$$

$$
P \subseteq co\text{-}RP \subseteq BPP
$$

$$
RP \cup co\text{-}RP \subseteq BPP
$$

$$
ZPP = RP \cap co\text{-}RP
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **ZPP (Zero-error Probabilistic Polynomial time):** The class of problems solvable in expected polynomial time with **zero error**. It equals the intersection of RP and co-RP. Equivalently, Las Vegas algorithms.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No** | **Concept** | **Formula / Statement** | **Application** |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | RP Acceptance (YES) | $\Pr[\text{accept}] \geq 1/2$ | One-sided upper bound on false negatives |
| 2 | RP Rejection (NO) | $\Pr[\text{accept}] = 0$ | Zero false-positive guarantee |
| 3 | BPP Acceptance (YES) | $\Pr[\text{accept}] \geq 2/3$ | Bounded false-negative rate |
| 4 | BPP Rejection (NO) | $\Pr[\text{reject}] \geq 2/3$ | Bounded false-positive rate |
| 5 | Amplification (Majority Vote) | $\Pr[\text{error after } k \text{ trials}] \leq e^{-2k\epsilon^2}$ | Chernoff bound on binomial sum |
| 6 | Geometric amplification (RP) | $\Pr[\text{error after } k \text{ trials}] \leq 2^{-k}$ | Probability all $k$ trials reject YES |
| 7 | Chernoff Bound | $\Pr[\vert X - \mu \vert \geq \delta\mu] \leq 2\exp(-\delta^2\mu/3)$ | Tail bound for sum of independent r.v.s |
| 8 | Total Randomness for $2^{-q}$ error | $r(n) = O(q(n)/\epsilon^2)$ | Bits needed for BPP amplification |
| 9 | Las Vegas expected time | $T_{exp} = \text{poly}(n)$ | ZPP runtime definition |
| 10 | ZPP identity | $ZPP = RP \cap co\text{-}RP$ | Las Vegas = one-sided error both ways |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Randomized Complexity Classes

| **Domain** | **Use Case** | **Class Used** |
|---|---|---|
| Cryptography | Primality testing for key generation | RP / co-RP |
| Verification | Probabilistically Checkable Proofs (PCP) | BPP-related |
| ML/AI | PAC learning, model evaluation | BPP-style sampling |
| Network Routing | Randomized distributed algorithms | BPP |
| Computational Biology | Pattern matching with errors | RP |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Error Amplification via Chernoff Bound

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let $M$ be a BPP algorithm with error probability $1/3$ (success probability $2/3$). We want to amplify the success probability to $1 - 2^{-k}$ by running $M$ a total of $T$ times and taking the majority vote.

Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_T$ be independent Bernoulli random variables where $X_i = 1$ if the $i$-th run of $M$ is correct, and $X_i = 0$ otherwise.

### 3.1.2 Expectation

Since each run has success probability $\geq 2/3$, the expected number of correct runs is:

$$
E\left[\sum_{i=1}^{T} X_i\right] = T \cdot E[X_i] \geq \frac{2T}{3}
$$

### 3.1.3 Error Event

The majority vote fails if the majority of runs are wrong, i.e., if:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{T} X_i \leq \frac{T}{2}
$$

This represents a deviation from the mean of at least:

$$
\frac{2T}{3} - \frac{T}{2} = \frac{4T - 3T}{6} = \frac{T}{6}
$$

### 3.1.4 Applying Chernoff Bound

The Chernoff bound for lower-tail deviation states that for $\delta \in (0,1)$:

$$
\Pr\left[\sum X_i \leq (1 - \delta)\mu\right] \leq \exp\left(-\frac{\delta^2 \mu}{2}\right)
$$

With $\mu = 2T/3$ and target $\leq T/2$, the relative deviation is:

$$
\delta = 1 - \frac{T/2}{2T/3} = 1 - \frac{3}{4} = \frac{1}{4}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\Pr[\text{majority vote fails}] \leq \exp\left(-\frac{(1/4)^2 \cdot (2T/3)}{2}\right)
$$

### 3.1.5 Final Bound

$$
\Pr[\text{majority vote fails}] \leq \exp\left(-\frac{T}{48}\right)
$$

To make this $\leq 2^{-k}$, we set:

$$
\exp\left(-\frac{T}{48}\right) \leq 2^{-k}
$$

$$
\frac{T}{48} \geq k \ln 2
$$

$$
T \geq 48 \cdot k \ln 2
$$

$$
T = O(k)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A constant-factor polynomial number of repetitions of the original BPP machine suffices to drive the two-sided error **exponentially low**, preserving polynomial runtime.

## 3.2 Derivation: RP Amplification (Geometric Decay)

### 3.2.1 Setup

For an RP algorithm $M$ with one-sided error at most $1/2$, we want to amplify by running $M$ independently $k$ times and accepting if **any single run accepts**.

### 3.2.2 Error Probability

If $x \in L$ (YES instance), the probability that all $k$ runs reject is:

$$
\Pr[\text{all reject} \mid x \in L] = \left(\Pr[\text{single run rejects}]\right)^k \leq \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^k = 2^{-k}
$$

### 3.2.3 Conclusion

To achieve error $\leq 2^{-q(n)}$, we need $k = q(n)$ independent runs, giving a randomness complexity of:

$$
r(n) = q(n) \cdot p(n) = O(q(n) \cdot p(n))
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **geometric decay** for RP is **stronger** than the Chernoff-based amplification for BPP, because RP has only one-sided error to amplify.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparative Summary

| **Property** | **RP** | **BPP** | **ZPP** | **PP** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Error Type | One-sided | Two-sided | Zero (Las Vegas) | Unbounded |
| Error Bound (YES) | $\Pr[\text{acc}] \geq 1/2$ | $\Pr[\text{acc}] \geq 2/3$ | $\Pr[\text{acc}] = 1$ | $\Pr[\text{acc}] > 1/2$ |
| Error Bound (NO) | $\Pr[\text{acc}] = 0$ | $\Pr[\text{rej}] \geq 2/3$ | $\Pr[\text{rej}] = 1$ | $\Pr[\text{acc}] \leq 1/2$ |
| Complement Closure | No ($\text{co-RP} \neq \text{RP}$ in general) | Yes ($\text{BPP} = \text{co-BPP}$) | Yes | Yes |
| Deterministic Sim. | $\exists$ using $\exp(n)$ random bits via derandomization | Same | Same | Unknown tighter |
| Amplification | Geometric ($2^{-k}$) | Chernoff ($\exp(-ck)$) | Trivial | Chernoff |
| Containment | $P \subseteq RP \subseteq BPP$ | $RP \subseteq BPP \subseteq PP$ | $ZPP = RP \cap co\text{-}RP$ | $PP \subseteq PSPACE$ |
| Open Question | $P = RP$? | $P = BPP$? | $P = ZPP$? | $PP = PSPACE$? |

## 3.4 Worked Example: Composite Number Detection (RP-style)

### 3.4.1 Problem

Decide whether an integer $N$ is composite, given that $N$ is the product of two distinct odd primes $p$ and $q$ (semiprime). The complementary language is "N is prime."

### 3.4.2 Algorithm (Witness-based RP)

A composite $N$ has a non-trivial square root of $1$ modulo $N$. Choose random $a \in \{2, \ldots, N-1\}$:

- If $\gcd(a, N) \neq 1$, then $a$ is a witness to compositeness ŌåÆ ACCEPT.
- Else compute $x = a^{(N-1)/2} \mod N$ using modular exponentiation.
- If $x \not\equiv \pm 1 \pmod N$, then $a$ is a witness to compositeness ŌåÆ ACCEPT.
- Else REJECT.

### 3.4.3 Analysis

- If $N$ is composite, at least half the choices of $a$ are witnesses ŌćÆ $\Pr[\text{ACCEPT}] \geq 1/2$.
- If $N$ is prime, no $a$ is a witness ŌćÆ $\Pr[\text{ACCEPT}] = 0$.

This satisfies RP. To achieve error $\leq 2^{-40}$ (the standard cryptographic threshold), we run the algorithm $k = 40$ times and accept if **any** iteration accepts. The amplified algorithm has runtime $O(40 \cdot \log^3 N) = O(\log^3 N)$, which is polynomial in the input length $n = \log N$.
